title: ACCTG 211H Companion
text:
Welcome
This is the ACCTG 211H Companion — the online textbook for Honors Financial and Managerial Accounting for Decision Making at the Smeal College of Business.
The Companion is organized around a single question: Why does accounting exist? Each chapter answers a smaller piece of that question, then introduces the mechanics needed to act on the answer. The goal of the course, and of this book, is to help you graduate from ACCTG 211H able to articulate, for any accounting choice, both what the rule is and why a sensible person would choose that rule over plausible alternatives.

title: ACCTG 211H Companion
text:
How to use this book
The chapters are designed to be read in order. Each builds on the last, and the spiral structure of the course means that ideas introduced early are revisited later at greater depth. If you read Chapter 7 before Chapter 3, the discussion of the accrual anomaly will not land in quite the same way.
That said, the chapters are also designed to be revisited. After we have studied Holmstrom’s stewardship model in Week 4, you may find it useful to reread Chapter 1’s discussion of the separation of ownership and control. After we have computed the Sloan accruals in Week 7, the Chapter 3 discussion of accruals as a signal will read very differently. Treat the Companion the way you would treat a good road atlas: you read it forward to plan, and you flip back to it constantly to figure out where you are.

title: ACCTG 211H Companion
text:
What you will learn
By the end of the course, you should be able to:

Explain the economic problems that gave rise to financial and managerial accounting, using the language of information asymmetry, signaling, moral hazard, and decentralization.
Prepare and analyze a basic set of GAAP financial statements for representative business transactions.
Read a canonical academic paper in financial or managerial accounting and articulate (a) the question it asks, (b) the empirical or theoretical method it uses, (c) what it finds, and (d) what its findings imply for the broader question of how accounting is used.
Conduct a small empirical analysis using Wharton Research Data Services (WRDS), including extracting data, computing standard accounting and capital-market measures, and summarizing findings in a written report.
Argue, in structured oral debate, both sides of contested conceptual questions in financial reporting.

title: ACCTG 211H Companion
text:
A note on the spiral
You will encounter accruals three times this semester: first in Chapter 3, where they appear as a signaling device; second in Chapter 6, where they appear as a value-relevant input to capital markets; and third in Chapter 7, where they appear as a noisy and sometimes mispriced metric. Each pass introduces what came before, deepens it, and adds a new layer.
This is not redundancy. It is the structure of the course. The reason you study accruals three times is that there are three different questions one can ask about them, and a serious treatment of accounting requires you to be able to answer all three.

title: ACCTG 211H Companion
text:
Conventions
In this book:

Bold indicates a defined term that you should be able to define yourself after reading.
Italic indicates emphasis or, occasionally, the title of a referenced work.
Monospaced indicates code or a variable name in a dataset.
A box like the one below indicates an important callout:







Why this matters



Callouts pull out a particular point or implication of the surrounding text. Read them carefully.








Common pitfall



Pitfalls flag mistakes that students reliably make on the relevant material. Read these especially carefully.








For the empirical labs



Lab tips are practical guidance for the WRDS work — Python/pandas syntax, data caveats, things to check.

title: ACCTG 211H Companion
text:
Errata and feedback
This is the first edition of the Companion. If you spot an error, an unclear passage, or a missing reference, please email me at sbb151@psu.edu, or — if you are comfortable with GitHub — submit an issue or pull request to the companion repository. Each year’s edition incorporates the previous year’s corrections.

title: Preface: How This Book Is Organized
text:
The four arcs
The Companion is divided into four arcs of unequal length, each animated by a single question.
Arc I — The Information Problem (Chapters 1–5). Why does accounting exist at all? The arc opens with the historical separation of ownership from control (Berle and Means 1932) and develops the four foundational economic problems accounting was invented to address: adverse selection, signaling, moral hazard, and the regulatory environment in which standards are set.
Arc II — Markets and Measurement (Chapters 6–10). How do capital markets use accounting numbers, and what should be measured? The arc moves from market efficiency (Fama 1970; Ball and Brown 1968) through accruals and earnings quality (Sloan 1996) to specific measurement issues for long-term assets, liabilities, and equity.
Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information (Chapters 11–13). How does accounting support contracts inside the firm? The arc reframes managerial accounting as the design of internal information systems for decentralized decision-making: cost behavior, performance measurement, and capital budgeting are all studied as instances of the agency and decision-rights problems introduced in Arc I.
Arc IV — Synthesis (Chapter 14). What have we learned, and what is unresolved? The arc returns to a small set of contested questions in financial reporting and uses the framework developed across the prior thirteen chapters to argue both sides.
crumbs: Preface: How This Book Is Organized

title: Preface: How This Book Is Organized
text:
Chapter template
Every chapter, except those in Arc IV, follows the same nine-section structure. The template is rigid by design — once you have read one chapter, you know how to read the next.

Opening question. A short economic puzzle that motivates the chapter. Most chapters open with a concrete fact about firm behavior or capital markets, supported by a citation to the relevant literature, before turning to the question the chapter sets out to address.
Stylized model. The simplest formal or semi-formal model that captures the puzzle. The math is kept light and the intuition heavy. Where a model is canonical (Akerlof, Holmstrom, Spence), the formal derivation is included; otherwise we rely on stylized numerical examples.
Institutional response. How GAAP, the SEC, the FASB, or firms in practice handle the underlying problem. This section connects the abstract model to the concrete world the student will encounter as a working professional.
Mechanics. Journal entries, financial statement preparation, ratios, or computations the student must master. The mechanics are introduced after the stylized model so that the student understands what the mechanics are for.
Empirical evidence. What the academic literature has found. Each chapter assigns one or two papers; this section explains them in the context of the chapter’s framework.
Discussion questions. Three to five open-ended questions intended to seed class discussion.
Problems. Practice problems, mostly mechanical, with a small number of conceptual problems mixed in.
Lab. For chapters that have an associated WRDS lab, a section pointing to the lab notebook and outlining what the student should expect to learn from it.
Further reading. An annotated list of papers and book chapters for students who want to go deeper.
crumbs: Preface: How This Book Is Organized

title: Preface: How This Book Is Organized
section: How to study from this book
text:
How to study from this book
Read the opening question and the stylized model carefully. Slow down. Most students who struggle in this course do so because they tried to memorize the institutional response (Section 3) and the mechanics (Section 4) without first internalizing the underlying problem. The mechanics make sense only after you have understood the puzzle they were designed to solve.
Work the problems. Reading is not enough. Reading the journal entries for revenue recognition is no substitute for actually constructing them. The problems at the end of each chapter are the minimum; if you find them easy, do additional problems from the resources listed in Further reading.
Read the assigned papers actively. For each paper, you should be able to write down — without looking — (a) the question, (b) the method, (c) the headline finding, (d) what you would believe differently after reading the paper than before. The pre-class reading questions on Canvas are designed to enforce this.
Engage with the discussion questions before class. The discussion questions are the rough outline for what we will actually discuss. Coming to class with a half-formed answer in your head is much more useful than coming in with no answer at all.
Use the appendices when you need them. The appendices are reference material rather than reading material. Appendix A1 is a primer on the financial statement effects template (FSET); Appendix A2 is a Python + WRDS in Colab quickstart for the labs; Appendix A3 is a debits-and-credits cheat sheet for the moments when the entry you are about to make does not feel quite right.
crumbs: Preface: How This Book Is Organized

title: Preface: How This Book Is Organized
text:
A note on the readings
The Companion is the primary text for the course. Each chapter also points you toward two or three academic papers and, occasionally, chapters from other books. The papers are the heart of the matter — they are how the field actually communicates — and the Companion’s job is to give you enough scaffolding that you can read them productively. Do not skip the papers in favor of the Companion. The Companion is not a replacement for the literature; it is a guide to how to read it.
crumbs: Preface: How This Book Is Organized

title: Preface: How This Book Is Organized
text:
A note on style
I have written the Companion in what I hope is a clear, measured tone. The book is more careful and more hedged than a typical introductory textbook, because the material itself is more careful and more hedged than introductory textbooks usually admit. Accounting is not a settled field. The standards we have are the standards we have because they are the best compromises a well-meaning standard-setter could find under the constraints — not because they are the unique correct answer to a well-posed problem. I would rather you finish this course with a slight discomfort about which choices are right than with a false confidence that the answers are obvious.






Ball, Ray, and Philip Brown. 1968. “An Empirical Evaluation of Accounting Income Numbers.” Journal of Accounting Research 6 (2): 159–78.


Berle, Adolf A., and Gardiner C. Means. 1932. The Modern Corporation and Private Property. New York: Macmillan.


Fama, Eugene F. 1970. “Efficient Capital Markets: A Review of Theory and Empirical Work.” Journal of Finance 25 (2): 383–417.


Sloan, Richard G. 1996. “Do Stock Prices Fully Reflect Information in Accruals and Cash Flows about Future Earnings?” The Accounting Review 71 (3): 289–315.
crumbs: Preface: How This Book Is Organized

title: 1  Foundations
text:
1.1 Opening question
The typical large U.S. public corporation is owned by people who have never met its managers. Apple has roughly fifteen billion shares outstanding, scattered across millions of brokerage accounts and retirement plans, and the firm is run day-to-day by a few dozen senior executives in Cupertino. A shareholder who buys one hundred shares of Apple on a Tuesday morning has, in legal terms, become a fractional owner of a half-trillion-dollar enterprise — and that same shareholder may, in practice, have no contact with the firm beyond a quarterly press release and a proxy ballot.
This arrangement is so familiar that its strangeness is easy to miss. The residual claimants on the firm’s profits do not run the firm. They have delegated control of an enormous pool of capital to a small group of agents whose interests are at most imperfectly aligned with their own. They cannot verify, day by day, that those agents are working hard, choosing prudently, or refraining from diverting resources to private benefit. They face, in short, an information problem (i.e., they have committed capital but cannot directly observe what is being done with it).
This chapter takes the position that the modern system of financial accounting and reporting is, at its core, a response to that problem. The argument is not new — it can be traced at least to Berle and Means (1932), who documented that ownership in the largest U.S. corporations had become dispersed across small shareholders while control had concentrated in professional management. The underlying logic was sharpened in the 1970s by Jensen and Meckling (1976), who modeled the firm as a nexus of contracts among parties with conflicting interests and imperfect information. The chapter develops the argument in a form the rest of the course can build on.
By the end of the chapter, we hope you can articulate why a market economy with separated ownership and control creates a demand for credible periodic reporting — even before you know anything about debits, credits, GAAP, or any specific accounting rule. The chapter does not yet introduce mechanics. We will not discuss journal entries until Chapter 3, and we will not prepare a balance sheet until Chapter 2. Starting with the foundational economic problem ensures that, when we do reach the mechanics, you understand what those mechanics are for.

title: 1  Foundations
section: 1.2 A stylized setup: capital, control, and the cost of cooperation
text:
1.2 A stylized setup: capital, control, and the cost of cooperation
Consider a small thought experiment. Two people form a partnership to operate a single restaurant. One — call her the owner — supplies the capital, say $100 of equity to lease space, buy equipment, and stock the pantry. The other — call him the manager — supplies the labor and operational expertise: he hires the staff, sets the menu, and runs the kitchen. The two agree, in advance, that the manager will run the business and the owner will receive a share of the profits.
If the owner can observe everything the manager does — every decision, every input, every output — the arrangement is straightforward. She can verify, at the end of each period, that the manager worked hard, chose sensibly, and reported honestly. The “accounting” of the operation is whatever she observed personally.
This is not the world in which firms operate. The owner cannot watch the manager all day. She cannot independently verify whether a slow Tuesday was the result of poor menu choices or simply bad weather. She cannot separate the manager’s effort from his competence, nor either from the realized state of the world. She can observe outputs — periodic measures of profit, cash, and net assets — but the interpretation of those outputs is at least partly ambiguous.






Why this matters



The owner faces two related but distinct problems. First, before contracting, she is uncertain whether the manager is in fact a good fit for the role — an adverse selection problem (i.e., one party knows more than the other about a relevant characteristic at the time of contracting; see Chapter 2). Second, after contracting, she cannot observe the manager’s effort or the quality of his decisions — a moral hazard problem (i.e., one party’s actions are imperfectly observable to the other after contracting; see Chapter 4). Each problem has its own information-economics literature, and each problem has its own characteristic accounting response.


Consider what happens if the parties cannot reduce the owner’s information disadvantage. The owner, anticipating that she cannot easily monitor the manager, will rationally demand a higher share of the surplus to compensate for the risk of shirking or self-dealing. The manager, anticipating that the owner will demand a higher share, may decline to enter the partnership at all — particularly if he can use his labor and expertise on a project he himself owns. Jensen and Meckling (1976) modeled this dynamic and showed that the agency costs of the relationship (i.e., the deadweight loss attributable to imperfect monitoring) can be large enough to prevent value-creating arrangements from forming. There is a real economic problem here, not merely a complaint about untrustworthy managers.
What might mitigate the problem? One natural answer, and the one this course will develop in detail, is credible periodic reporting. If the manager can produce, at regular intervals, a report on the firm’s economic activity that is detailed enough, comparable enough, and verified enough that the owner can use it to evaluate the manager’s stewardship, the agency cost shrinks. Some risk remains — no report is perfect — but the wedge between what the owner would do herself and what the manager will do on her behalf is narrower than in the absence of the report.
This is, in the broadest terms, what financial accounting is for. The income statement, the balance sheet, the statement of cash flows, the audit, the disclosure rules, the conceptual framework — none of these existed in their modern form three centuries ago. They emerged, slowly and sometimes painfully, as the institutional response to a particular problem: how owners can credibly delegate control of capital to managers without losing too much of the surplus to agency costs.

title: 1  Foundations
section: 1.3 The historical claim: Berle and Means
text:
1.3 The historical claim: Berle and Means
Berle and Means (1932), writing in the early 1930s, were the first to systematically document the separation of ownership and control in the modern American economy. By examining the share registries and proxy materials of the 200 largest non-financial corporations, they showed that a substantial fraction of American industrial capital was held in firms in which no single shareholder, or even small coalition of shareholders, owned a controlling stake. Control in these firms had passed, effectively, to professional management.
Berle and Means were not the first to notice the phenomenon — earlier observers including Marshall and Veblen had remarked on it — but they provided what no one had: a systematic empirical accounting of how widespread it had become. They documented that the typical large firm of the early twentieth century was no longer a partnership-like entity in which the providers of capital exerted direct managerial control. It had become an institution in which capital and control were structurally separate.
Their concerns were political-economic rather than narrowly accounting-focused. They worried about the consequences of dispersed ownership for corporate governance, for the regulation of monopoly, and for the distribution of economic power. But the empirical observation is the foundation on which the modern theory of the firm — and modern accounting — has been built.
A century later, the pattern is, if anything, more pronounced. Index funds and pension trusts now hold large fractions of the float of firms such as Apple, Microsoft, and ExxonMobil, while the operational decisions at those firms are made by a handful of executives. The largest individual shareholder of Boeing is not a person but a fund family; the typical retail shareholder votes her shares (if at all) through a brokerage that passes her ballot to a proxy advisor. The separation Berle and Means described in 1932 has, in important respects, deepened.






A point worth pausing on



The separation of ownership and control is not a flaw of the modern corporation. It is, in important respects, a feature. It allows capital to be raised at scale from a vast number of small savers, each of whom would be unable to monitor a firm individually but who, in aggregate, can finance enterprises far larger than any single owner could fund. The cost of the separation is that each individual capital provider has limited ability to verify what is being done with the capital. The system of financial accounting is part of how we make the trade-off worthwhile.

title: 1  Foundations
section: 1.4 Coase, the firm, and the boundary problem
text:
1.4 Coase, the firm, and the boundary problem
If the firm is the institutional form within which the agency problem arises, a prior question follows: why do firms exist at all? Why is much of the economy organized as long-lived hierarchical institutions, rather than as a continuous sequence of arms-length market transactions among individual specialists?
Coase (1937) gave what remains the canonical answer. The market is not free. Conducting transactions through the price system requires discovering what the relevant prices are, negotiating contracts, monitoring compliance, and adjudicating disputes. These transaction costs (i.e., the costs of using the price mechanism) are substantial. The firm exists because some sets of activities can be organized more cheaply within an authority relationship than through repeated arms-length contracting.
A simple example fixes the idea. Toyota could, in principle, purchase each component of a Camry from a separate independent supplier on the spot market, negotiating each transaction one by one. In practice, it does not. It maintains long-term relationships with a small set of suppliers, integrates some component production in-house, and coordinates the rest through hierarchical contracts that look nothing like spot-market exchange. The reason is precisely Coasean: the cost of using the market to coordinate thousands of interdependent activities — pricing each, contracting each, verifying each — would exceed the cost of coordinating them through internal authority.
Coase’s framework has implications for accounting that he himself did not pursue but that are central to this course. If the firm exists in part because internal organization is cheaper than market exchange when transaction costs are high, then the firm faces an internal information problem mirroring its external one. Inside Toyota, the manager who oversees a subordinate cannot directly observe everything the subordinate does, and the subordinate may have private information about the cost of his task. This internal information problem is the subject of managerial accounting (Arc III), where we will see that performance measurement, budgeting, and capital allocation are designed responses to the same underlying logic that motivates the financial reports the firm produces for its external owners.
The boundary of the firm — what is done internally and what is purchased externally — is itself a function of the relative costs of internal versus external information. Where internal information is cheap and external information is dear, the firm expands; where external information is cheap and internal coordination is expensive, the firm contracts. The deep continuity between Coase and the modern theory of the firm is that both treat information as the friction that determines economic structure.

title: 1  Foundations
text:
1.5 The Jensen-Meckling synthesis
Jensen and Meckling (1976) brought the Berle-Means observation and the Coasean framework into a unified analytical setting. Their contribution, which has structured roughly five decades of corporate finance and accounting research, was to model the firm explicitly as a nexus of contracts (i.e., a legal fiction within which shareholders, debtholders, managers, employees, customers, and suppliers interact under conditions of imperfect information).
The central concept they formalized is agency cost: the deadweight loss that arises when one party (the principal) delegates decision authority to another (the agent) and cannot perfectly monitor the agent’s actions. They decomposed agency cost into three components: (i) monitoring expenditures by the principal, (ii) bonding expenditures by the agent, and (iii) the residual loss left on the table even after monitoring and bonding have been undertaken at their cost-minimizing levels.






The three components of agency cost




Monitoring expenditures. Costs the principal incurs to observe and constrain the agent’s behavior. Examples: audit fees paid to a firm such as Deloitte or PwC, board oversight, performance-evaluation systems.
Bonding expenditures. Costs the agent incurs to commit to acting in the principal’s interest. Examples: a CEO holding a meaningful equity stake in her own firm, contractual non-compete clauses, voluntary disclosure beyond what is mandated.
Residual loss. The remaining shortfall between the principal’s first-best outcome and the equilibrium outcome, after monitoring and bonding have been done. This is the welfare loss the system cannot eliminate.



Two implications of the framework matter for the rest of the course. First, financial reporting is naturally understood as a monitoring technology — a costly activity undertaken by the principal (or mandated by regulation acting on behalf of the principal) to reduce the agency cost of the relationship. The audit is part of the monitoring expenditure. Voluntary disclosure beyond the mandatory minimum (e.g., a firm releasing detailed segment data not required by GAAP) is part of the bonding expenditure. The choice of accounting policy, where discretion exists, is a choice the manager makes that can either narrow or widen the residual loss.
Second, the framework provides discipline for thinking about the costs of accounting. Mandatory disclosure rules are not free. The compliance costs of preparing financial reports are real, the audit costs are real, and the costs of complying with detailed standards are non-trivial — particularly for smaller firms. A defensible standard-setting process imposes a given mandatory cost only when the expected reduction in agency cost exceeds it. Whether the actual standard-setting process achieves that calibration is a separate empirical question, one we return to in Chapter 5.

title: 1  Foundations
section: 1.6 Recordkeeping is older than the corporation
text:
1.6 Recordkeeping is older than the corporation
The joint-stock corporation is a relatively young institution. Recordkeeping itself is much older. 1 argue that systematic recordkeeping has co-evolved with cooperative economic activity for several thousand years, predating the development of money, writing as we now understand it, and certainly the modern corporation. They cite archaeological evidence from the ancient Near East — clay envelopes and tokens recording grain consignments, cuneiform tablets documenting loans and partnerships, proto-cuneiform inventories of temple stores — as evidence that human cooperation at scale has always required some external mechanism to record claims, document obligations, and verify performance.
Their claim is broader than the corporate context, but it is consistent with the framework of this chapter. Wherever cooperative economic activity exceeds the parties’ ability to remember and verify, some form of external record becomes necessary. The corporation introduces a particular set of separations — between owner and manager, between manager and employee, between current and future periods — that intensify the underlying information problem. The basic demand for credible records is older and more general.
This historical observation has a practical implication. It is sometimes argued that financial accounting is an artifact of recent regulation — that, but for the SEC and the FASB, firms would not bother to produce accounts. The Basu-Waymire evidence cuts against this view. Cooperative economic activity has produced accounts for at least five thousand years, often without state mandate. Mandatory financial reporting under U.S. securities law dates from 1934; cooperative recordkeeping dates from at least the third millennium BCE. The economic problem that gives rise to accounting is older and more durable than any particular regulatory regime.
Even in the modern era, voluntary recordkeeping appears wherever cooperation exists. Family-owned firms that have no public shareholders still prepare internal financial statements. Privately held partnerships such as the major U.S. accounting firms maintain elaborate internal accounts that no securities regulator requires. Even households increasingly keep budgets, ledgers, and records of their financial activity through software such as Mint or YNAB. These observations are consistent with a demand for recordkeeping that arises from the structure of cooperation itself, not from the requirements of any regulator.

title: 1  Foundations
text:
1.7 What comes next
The remainder of this book proceeds as follows. Chapter 2 gives the abstract argument of this chapter a sharp empirical edge through Akerlof (1970)’s lemons model, which shows that markets with asymmetric information about quality can unravel and that credible disclosure can help keep them from doing so. Chapter 3 turns to the supply side: when and why do firms reveal their private information voluntarily, and what does the income statement do that the cash flow statement does not? Chapter 4 develops the moral hazard problem and shows how accounting reports become part of the contracting environment that aligns managerial incentives with owner interests. Chapter 5 closes Arc I at the institutional level — the FASB, the SEC, the conceptual framework — and asks why we have public accounting standards at all.
The remaining arcs return to these themes at greater depth. Arc II (Chapters 6–10) examines how capital markets actually use accounting numbers: how prices respond to earnings news, why some accounting metrics appear to be mispriced, and what the empirical literature has found about the relevance of different measurement bases. Arc III (Chapters 11–13) turns inside the firm, where the same agency logic that motivates external reporting also motivates internal performance measurement, transfer pricing, and capital budgeting. Arc IV (Chapter 14) returns to a small set of contested questions and asks you to argue both sides.
If the chapter is doing its work, you should leave with one specific intuition. Accounting exists because the modern economy requires owners to delegate control of capital to managers they cannot directly observe, and credible periodic reporting is part of how the parties can make that arrangement work. Everything that follows — the journal entry, the audit, the conceptual framework, the value-relevance literature — is in the service of that underlying problem.

title: 1  Foundations
text:
1.8 Key takeaways






Key takeaways




Ownership and control are structurally separate in the modern corporation. Capital is supplied by dispersed shareholders, while operational decisions are made by professional managers the shareholders cannot directly observe.
The separation creates an information problem, not merely a trust problem. Owners can observe outputs (i.e., profit, cash, net assets) but cannot easily distinguish managerial effort from luck or competence, which gives rise to agency costs in the sense of Jensen and Meckling (1976).
Financial accounting is best understood as an institutional response. Periodic, comparable, and verified reporting reduces the wedge between what the owner would do herself and what the manager will do on her behalf.
The framework integrates three traditions. Berle and Means (1932) documented the empirical separation, Coase (1937) explained why firms exist as authority relationships in the first place, and Jensen and Meckling (1976) modeled the agency cost the separation produces.
Agency cost has three components. Monitoring expenditures by the principal, bonding expenditures by the agent, and an irreducible residual loss — a decomposition that organizes how we will think about audits, voluntary disclosure, and accounting-policy choice throughout the course.
The demand for recordkeeping predates the corporation by millennia. 1 show that cooperative economic activity has produced accounts for thousands of years, suggesting that the underlying economic problem is more durable than any particular regulatory regime.

title: 1  Foundations
text:
1.9 Discussion questions

Berle and Means (1932) treated the separation of ownership and control as a problem. Jensen and Meckling (1976) treated it as a feature whose costs can be partially mitigated by monitoring and bonding. Are these two views in tension, or are they complementary? Defend your answer.
The chapter argues that accounting is an institutional response to an information problem. What other institutions can you identify that respond to the same problem (e.g., credit rating agencies, financial analysts, the financial press, securities litigation)? How do they differ from accounting in what they offer the principal?
1 argue that recordkeeping co-evolved with cooperative economic activity over several millennia. If accounting predates the modern corporation by thousands of years, what do you make of arguments that current financial accounting is “broken” or “outdated”? What standard would you use to evaluate such claims?
Suppose a sole proprietor — someone who owns and runs her own small business — were not subject to any accounting requirements. Would she still keep some kind of accounts? Why or why not? What would her accounts look like, and how would they differ from those of a public company such as Starbucks?
The chapter has not yet introduced any mechanics. Based on the framework presented here, what are the properties an accounting report would need to have to be useful in the agency context? Be as specific as you can, in your own words, before reading Chapter 5.

title: 1  Foundations
text:
1.10 Problems
There are no mechanical problems associated with this chapter — those begin in Chapter 2. The problems below are short writing exercises intended to make the framework above concrete. Each is meant to be roughly one page, double-spaced, unless otherwise stated.

Mapping an agency relationship at a specific firm. Choose one of the following public companies: Apple, Boeing, Disney, JPMorgan Chase, or Procter & Gamble. In approximately one page, do the following: (i) describe the agency relationship between its shareholders and its CEO using the Jensen-Meckling framework; (ii) identify one specific observable signal (e.g., a line item, ratio, or disclosure that appears in the firm’s 10-K) that shareholders can use to evaluate the CEO’s stewardship; (iii) identify one specific dimension of the CEO’s behavior that shareholders cannot directly observe; and (iv) explain how the firm’s mandatory financial reports help narrow the gap between (ii) and (iii). Cite the 10-K item or page on which your observable signal appears.
A non-corporate agency relationship. Identify an example, from your own experience or reading, of an agency relationship that is not between shareholders and managers. Possibilities include a homeowner and a remodeling contractor, a patient and a primary-care physician, a citizen and an elected official, or a student-athlete and an NCAA coach. Describe (i) the principal, (ii) the agent, (iii) what the principal cannot directly observe, and (iv) at least two institutional mechanisms (other than financial accounting) that have evolved to mitigate the problem (e.g., licensing, malpractice litigation, performance ratings, elections). Then identify one feature financial accounting provides that those mechanisms do not.
Summarizing Jensen and Meckling. Read Section II of Jensen and Meckling (1976) (the introduction to agency costs). In approximately 250 words, summarize the three components of agency cost and give one concrete example of each drawn from a real firm in the news within the past five years. Do not reuse the authors’ examples, and do not reuse the examples given in the chapter callout.
Coase at the corporate boundary. Pick one firm whose vertical-integration choices you find interesting — for example, Apple’s decision to design its own silicon (the M-series chips) rather than purchase processors from Intel, or Tesla’s decision to operate its own dealerships rather than franchise. In approximately one page, describe the information and transaction-cost considerations that, consistent with Coase (1937), plausibly motivate the choice. Identify one specific way the firm’s internal accounting system would need to differ under the integrated structure relative to the arms-length alternative.
A failure of credible reporting. Choose a well-known case in which financial reporting failed to control the agency problem (e.g., Enron, WorldCom, Wells Fargo’s account-opening scandal, Wirecard). In approximately one page, identify (i) the specific information the report should have conveyed but did not, (ii) which of the three components of agency cost in Jensen and Meckling (1976) was most clearly compromised, and (iii) what subsequent institutional response (regulatory, contractual, or market-based) emerged to address the failure. The point of the exercise is not to assign blame but to use a concrete failure to illuminate the framework.

title: 1  Foundations
text:
1.11 Lab
The in-class activity for this week is the Information Game (classroom games/information game/), which we will play together in the second session of the week. Bring your laptop. The game is a coordination exercise designed to make tangible the cost that information asymmetry can impose on parties trying to cooperate.
There is no out-of-class lab associated with this chapter. The first WRDS lab arrives in Week 6 (Chapter 6).

title: 1  Foundations
text:
1.12 Further reading
The five readings below are the assigned readings for Week 1. You should read all five.

Berle and Means (1932), Chapters 1 and 2. The classic statement of the separation of ownership and control. The authors document that the 200 largest U.S. non-financial corporations had become, by the early 1930s, institutions in which control had passed from dispersed shareholders to professional management.
Coase (1937), “The Nature of the Firm.” A short and remarkable paper. Coase (1937) argues that firms exist because organizing certain activities within an authority relationship is cheaper than organizing them through repeated arms-length market exchange. Read it for the central question and the answer; the formal apparatus is light.
Jensen and Meckling (1976), the introduction (pp. 305–311) and Section II on agency costs. Jensen and Meckling (1976) model the firm as a nexus of contracts and decompose the agency cost of equity into monitoring, bonding, and residual loss. The paper is long, technical, and influential; read for the central concepts and skim the formal model on a first pass.

1 synthesize the archaeological evidence on the antiquity of recordkeeping and argue that systematic accounts co-evolved with cooperative economic activity for several thousand years before the modern corporation.

Optional, for those who want more: Hayek (1945), on the use of knowledge in society. Hayek (1945) argues that knowledge in an economy is dispersed across many individuals and that the price system is a mechanism for aggregating it. The argument is about prices rather than accounting, but the underlying point — that institutions exist in part to coordinate dispersed information — is foundational to the rest of this book and is brought back explicitly in Chapter 11.







Reading the assigned papers



For each of the assigned papers, you should be able to write down — without looking — (i) the question, (ii) the method or argument, (iii) the headline finding, and (iv) what you believe differently after reading the paper than before. The pre-class reading questions on Canvas are designed to enforce this. If you cannot answer (iv), you have not yet finished reading the paper.








Akerlof, George A. 1970. “The Market for ‘Lemons’: Quality Uncertainty and the Market Mechanism.” Quarterly Journal of Economics 84 (3): 488–500.


Berle, Adolf A., and Gardiner C. Means. 1932. The Modern Corporation and Private Property. New York: Macmillan.


Coase, Ronald H. 1937. “The Nature of the Firm.” Economica 4 (16): 386–405.


Hayek, Friedrich A. 1945. “The Use of Knowledge in Society.” American Economic Review 35 (4): 519–30.


Jensen, Michael C., and William H. Meckling. 1976. “Theory of the Firm: Managerial Behavior, Agency Costs and Ownership Structure.” Journal of Financial Economics 3 (4): 305–60.

title: 2  The Lemons Problem
text:
2.1 Opening question
Some markets that look like they should work do not. Consider three examples drawn from very different settings.
A used car that is two years old has, on average, lost roughly thirty percent of its original sticker price — far more than the depreciation a comparable lightly driven rental vehicle would suggest. A great many cars in the second-hand market are, in fact, in good condition; some are not, and the buyer often cannot tell which is which.
Before the Affordable Care Act of 2010, the individual health insurance market in many U.S. states was thin and expensive, and a substantial share of would-be participants were quoted premiums so high that they declined to enroll. The buyers who remained were, on average, sicker than the population at large, which pushed premiums higher still, which drove more healthy buyers out. Insurers and regulators referred to the dynamic as an adverse selection death spiral (i.e., a sequence of premium hikes and exits that ends with a tiny pool of high-cost enrollees).
In March 2017, the social-media company Snap Inc. went public at an offering price of seventeen dollars per share, valuing the firm at approximately twenty-four billion dollars. Snap’s S-1 registration statement, the document the firm filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission to register the offering, ran to roughly four hundred pages. The filing contained more than sixty distinct risk factors, three years of audited financial statements, and a detailed accounting of how the proceeds would be used. The audit was conducted by a Big Four firm. The underwriting syndicate of Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs, and J.P. Morgan signed off on the price. Within eighteen months, Snap shares had fallen below five dollars.
Each of these examples is an instance of the same underlying phenomenon. The people who choose to sell — used-car owners, the chronically ill, IPO issuers — know more about what they are offering than the people who buy. The buyers know that the sellers know more, and they price-protect themselves accordingly. The sellers, anticipating that the buyers will price-protect, may either decline to sell at all or attempt, at substantial expense, to convince the buyers that what is being offered is in fact worth more than the buyers initially suspect.
This chapter develops the canonical economic framework for analyzing markets of this kind. The framework is due to Akerlof (1970), who in a few short pages established the reference model for asymmetric information (i.e., a setting in which one party to a transaction has private knowledge the other party lacks) in product markets. Although Akerlof’s example was the used-car market, his result generalizes far beyond it; it is, on a large class of problems, the simplest result that captures the central tension that financial reporting was developed to address. By the end of the chapter you should be able to articulate why uncertainty about quality — combined with the seller’s superior knowledge — can lead a market to unravel, and why the mandatory disclosure regime under U.S. securities law can be understood, in important respects, as a market-design response to that unraveling.
The chapter also introduces, for the first time, the structure of the balance sheet. The introduction is deliberately positioned here rather than in Chapter 1. The balance sheet has a specific job in the framework we are developing: it is a credibly verifiable summary of the firm’s stocks of assets and obligations at a point in time, prepared in a structurally rigid form that constrains the issuer’s ability to misstate. Before the institutional context is in place, the form of the balance sheet can feel arbitrary. With that context in place, it should feel as though the form is performing real work.

title: 2  The Lemons Problem
text:
2.2 Stylized model

2.2.1 The Akerlof setup
Consider a market for used cars. Suppose, for concreteness, that there are many cars on offer, each with a quality \(\theta\) drawn from a uniform distribution on the interval \([0, 1]\). The quality \(\theta\) summarizes everything a buyer would want to know about the car (i.e., mechanical condition, accident history, expected residual life). A car of quality \(\theta\) is worth \(\theta\) to its current owner — the value she places on continuing to drive it — and is worth \(\beta \theta\) to a prospective buyer, where \(\beta > 1\). The wedge between \(\beta\theta\) and \(\theta\) is the gain from trade: there is a buyer for whom the car is more valuable than it is to the current owner, so in principle a transaction can be arranged that makes both parties better off.
Suppose, first, that quality is observable to both parties before any transaction occurs. The market in this benchmark case operates simply: buyers and sellers can negotiate any price between \(\theta\) and \(\beta\theta\), and every car trades at some price within that range. Every gain from trade is realized, and the outcome is Pareto efficient.
Now consider the realistic case in which quality is observed only by the seller. The buyer knows that quality is uniformly distributed on \([0, 1]\) but does not know any individual seller’s \(\theta\). The buyer is rational and offers, for any car, a price equal to her expected willingness to pay conditional on the information she has. If the buyer believed the cars offered for sale were drawn at random from the population of all cars, she would offer a price of \(\beta \cdot E[\theta] = \beta/2\). The buyer, being rational, knows that not every car will be offered: a seller with a \(\theta\) above the price will keep her car rather than sell it.
Let \(p\) denote the price the buyer offers. A seller is willing to sell if and only if her quality \(\theta \leq p\). The set of cars actually offered for sale is therefore the subset with \(\theta \in [0, p]\), and the expected quality conditional on being offered is
\[
E[\theta \mid \theta \leq p] = \frac{p}{2}.
\tag{2.1}\]
The buyer, facing this conditional distribution, is willing to pay \(\beta \cdot p/2\). For the market to clear, the buyer’s willingness to pay must equal the price she offers — that is,
\[
p = \beta \cdot \frac{p}{2},
\tag{2.2}\]
which holds only when \(p \cdot (1 - \beta/2) = 0\). If \(\beta < 2\), the only price at which the market clears is \(p = 0\). The market collapses, and no trade occurs.


2.2.2 Unraveling, step by step
The fixed-point algebra above hides a dynamic story that is worth walking through explicitly. Suppose \(\beta = 1.5\) (i.e., buyers value each car at one-and-a-half times what the seller values it), and consider what happens as buyers learn, round by round, which cars are actually for sale.
Round 0. A naive buyer assumes the seller pool resembles the full population and offers \(p_0 = \beta \cdot E[\theta] = 1.5 \cdot 0.5 = 0.75\).
Round 1. Sellers with \(\theta > 0.75\) keep their cars. The pool of cars actually offered is the subset with \(\theta \in [0, 0.75]\), which has expected quality \(0.375\). A rational buyer updates her offer to \(p_1 = 1.5 \cdot 0.375 = 0.5625\).
Round 2. With the lower offer, only sellers with \(\theta \leq 0.5625\) participate. Expected quality drops to \(0.5625 / 2 = 0.281\), and the buyer offers \(p_2 = 1.5 \cdot 0.281 \approx 0.422\).
The process continues. The price falls in each round, the average quality of the remaining pool falls with it, and the iteration converges to \(p^\* = 0\).



Table 2.1: Iterative unraveling in the Akerlof model with \(\beta = 1.5\). Each round the buyer’s offer falls, and the cars that remain in the pool are progressively lower quality. The fixed point is \(p^\* = 0\).
