The lesson of Table 2.1 is not that buyers in the world iterate this way in real time; they almost certainly do not. The lesson is that in any rational-expectations equilibrium the only self-consistent price is one at which buyers’ beliefs about the pool match what the pool actually contains. When \(\beta < 2\), that fixed point is \(p^\* = 0\).






The unraveling result



With full information, every car trades and the gains from trade are fully realized. With asymmetric information and \(\beta < 2\), the same population of buyers and sellers produces no trade at all — even though every individual seller would have been willing to sell at the right price, and every individual buyer would have been willing to buy at the right price. The asymmetric information has created a wedge that drives the market to a corner solution in which the surplus is zero.




2.2.3 What the model abstracts away
The Akerlof model is stylized. The continuous uniform distribution on \([0, 1]\) is a convenience, and the prediction of complete market collapse depends on the parameter restriction \(\beta < 2\). With richer assumptions — multiple buyer types, costly search, repeated transactions, partially informative signals — the model produces partial unraveling rather than complete unraveling, and the prediction shifts from “no trade” to “trade only of low-quality items at low prices.” The empirical pattern that emerges from the literature is closer to partial than to total unraveling, but the qualitative result is the same: when sellers know more than buyers about quality, market quality declines, prices fall, and the gains from trade that the symmetric-information benchmark predicted are not fully realized.


2.2.4 From used cars to capital markets
The lemons logic generalizes naturally to capital markets. A firm raising capital in an initial public offering (i.e., the first sale of stock to the public) knows more about its prospects, its risks, and the integrity of its management than the investors who are being asked to subscribe to the offering. Investors, knowing that issuers know more, demand a discount — what the investment banking literature calls IPO underpricing. Issuers, anticipating the discount, may forgo the offering altogether, or may attempt to convey their private information through credible signals such as the choice of underwriter (Beyer et al. 2010, who survey the channels through which disclosure choices affect capital costs), the engagement of a Big Four auditor, or the disclosure of detailed financial information well beyond what is mandated by the SEC.
The same logic applies in the secondary market. A firm whose insiders are about to issue equity at the prevailing market price knows that they would not be doing so if they thought the prevailing price was below the firm’s intrinsic value. Outside investors, anticipating this asymmetry, treat the announcement of a seasoned equity offering as bad news, and the price typically falls on the announcement.
The same logic applies, with appropriate modification, to debt markets, to private equity transactions, to the market for corporate control (i.e., mergers, acquisitions, and proxy contests), and to private placements of securities to a small set of sophisticated investors. Wherever an asymmetry exists in what the buyer and seller know about the asset being exchanged, we should expect, to a first approximation, prices to fall below their full-information level, volumes to be lower than the full-information benchmark would predict, and a substantial fraction of the gains from trade to be left on the table.






Why this matters for accounting



If asymmetric information about firm quality reduces trade and depresses prices, then any institution that credibly reduces the asymmetry has economic value. Mandatory financial reporting is one such institution. The audit is another. The SEC’s disclosure regime is another. Each of these institutions exists, in part, because the alternative — a market with unmitigated information asymmetry — is one in which capital is allocated less efficiently and at higher cost.

title: 2  The Lemons Problem
text:
2.3 Institutional response

2.3.1 Four families of solution
Once one understands why the market unravels, one immediately understands what kinds of institutional fixes might prevent the unraveling. The literature identifies four broad categories of solution.
The first category is credible disclosure. If the seller can reveal her private information in a way that buyers find believable, the buyer can update her willingness to pay and the unraveling is mitigated. Credibility is the key word: a seller cannot simply assert that her car is high-quality, because every seller has the incentive to make that claim regardless of the truth. The disclosure has to come with a verification mechanism — a third-party inspector, a warranty backed by costly bonding, an audited financial statement — that makes the claim costly to falsify. Most of what we will study in this course is, in one form or another, a credible-disclosure solution. The audited 10-K is the canonical example.
The second category is signaling. Even when direct disclosure is unavailable, a seller can sometimes engage in actions that are correlated with her private information and that low-quality types would find too costly to imitate. Spence (1973) developed this argument in the labor market context, showing that high-ability workers signal their type by acquiring expensive education that low-ability workers cannot profitably obtain. In the capital markets context, dividend payouts have been argued to play a signaling role, as has the issuance of subordinated debt. The signaling solution does not require the seller’s information to be directly verifiable; it requires only that the action being taken is more costly for low types than for high types. Chapter 3 develops the signaling logic in detail.
The third category is certification by an information intermediary. The function of the auditor, the credit rating agency, the analyst, and the underwriter is, in important respects, to take on the verification role that buyers cannot easily perform themselves. An information intermediary that has its own reputation at stake — that loses business and rents if it certifies low-quality issuers as high-quality — can serve as a credibility device that the issuer alone could not provide. This is one reason the empirical literature documents that issuers engage information intermediaries: the intermediary’s reputational capital is, in effect, lent to the issuer for the duration of the transaction.
The fourth category is repeated interaction and reputation. In a one-shot transaction, the seller has no incentive to honor representations that go beyond what is contractually verifiable. In a repeated setting, the seller’s incentive shifts: a seller who plans to be in the market again has reason to deliver on representations, because misrepresentation in the current transaction reduces the price she will obtain in the next. Repeated interaction is one of several reasons why long-lived public companies trade on tighter bid-ask spreads than otherwise comparable private firms or first-time issuers.






A taxonomy worth memorizing



The four solutions — (i) credible disclosure, (ii) signaling, (iii) certification, and (iv) reputation — are not mutually exclusive. A typical large public firm employs all four simultaneously: it discloses through its mandated filings, signals through dividend policy and capital structure, hires intermediaries (auditors, underwriters, credit rating agencies, analysts), and benefits from a reputation built up over many interactions with capital markets. The institutions of accounting are only one of the four — but they are the largest single component of the credibility infrastructure in modern capital markets.




2.3.2 Mandatory disclosure under U.S. securities law
The U.S. mandatory disclosure regime, which dates principally from the Securities Act of 1933 and the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, is the largest single institutional response to the lemons problem in capital markets. The regime imposes on public companies two distinct sets of obligations.
The first set governs the issuance of new securities. It requires firms to register securities offerings with the SEC and to file a registration statement (Form S-1 for an IPO, Form S-3 for a seasoned offering) containing extensive disclosures about the firm and the securities being offered. The second set governs the ongoing reporting of public companies. It requires (i) an annual report on Form 10-K, (ii) quarterly reports on Form 10-Q, and (iii) event-based current reports on Form 8-K, each accompanied by audited or reviewed financial statements as applicable.
The regime is mandatory in two senses that matter for the lemons argument. First, it requires the firm to provide disclosure even when the firm would, on a transactional basis, prefer to remain silent. (We will see in Chapter 3 that voluntary disclosure produces incomplete unraveling: low-type sellers will not voluntarily disclose, leading to a noisy equilibrium that resembles the partial-unraveling case.) Second, the regime requires the disclosure to satisfy specific standards of completeness, comparability, and accuracy, enforced by the SEC and, for the financial statements, by an external auditor.
The regime is not without costs. Compliance costs for public companies are substantial; small public firms commonly report annual external audit fees of one to two million dollars, and total compliance costs (i.e., audit, legal, internal accounting, board oversight) can run several times that amount. Whether the regime’s benefits in terms of reduced lemons discounts exceed those costs is an empirical question, and a contested one. The literature has produced evidence on both sides; Christensen, Hail, and Leuz (2013), who review the evidence on mandatory IFRS adoption around the world, find that the effects of mandated disclosure depend critically on the strength of enforcement institutions in the adopting country. The cost-benefit question recurs in Chapter 5.


2.3.3 A non-capital-markets example: the ACA and the individual insurance market
The opening question mentioned the individual health insurance market. The market design problem the Affordable Care Act attempted to solve is the same one Akerlof identified. Buyers (i.e., would-be enrollees) know more about their own health than insurers do. Sicker buyers are willing to pay more for coverage, so the pool of buyers who actually enroll at any given premium is sicker than the general population, which pushes the actuarially fair premium up, which drives more healthy buyers out — the dynamic, the adverse selection death spiral, described in Section 2.1.
The ACA’s institutional response combined three of the four families of solution from Section 2.3.1. (i) Disclosure requirements: insurers had to publish standardized plan summaries so consumers could compare apples to apples. (ii) Certification: a federal exchange certified that plans met minimum coverage standards, taking the verification burden off individual buyers. (iii) A mechanism that has no clean analog in the Akerlof model — the individual mandate — required all eligible Americans to purchase coverage, effectively forcing the healthy buyers who would have exited to remain in the pool. The mandate was, in effect, a non-market solution to the unraveling problem: rather than allow the equilibrium to collapse, the regulation simply ruled out the exit option. We will see in subsequent chapters that mandatory financial reporting under U.S. securities law has a similar character — it constrains the seller’s option to remain silent.

title: 2  The Lemons Problem
section: 2.4 Mechanics: the balance sheet as a credible report
text:
2.4 Mechanics: the balance sheet as a credible report
The balance sheet is the canonical accounting representation of the firm’s stocks of assets and obligations at a point in time. Its central organizing identity is the accounting equation:
\[
\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity}.
\tag{2.3}\]
The equation reflects a definitional truth: every dollar of assets the firm controls is financed either by an obligation to a creditor (a liability) or by a residual claim of the owners (equity). The two sides of the equation must equal — by construction — for every transaction the firm undertakes.
The discipline imposed by the accounting equation is, in part, what gives the balance sheet its credibility. Suppose the firm wishes to overstate its assets by ten million dollars. The overstatement must be reflected, by definition, on the right-hand side of the equation as well — either as a corresponding overstatement of liabilities (which the firm’s creditors would presumably notice and dispute) or as a corresponding overstatement of equity (which would itself flow through to overstated retained earnings, and thence to overstated income, which the auditor reviews). The fraudster cannot simply add ten million to the asset side and stop; the bookkeeping system forces the consequence to appear elsewhere. The double-entry structure (i.e., the convention that every transaction is recorded in at least two accounts so that the equation holds) is, in this sense, an information-economics solution to the demand for credibility: it creates a verification structure that makes simple misstatement detectable.
Consider a simple example. Suppose a new firm, Acme Corp., is organized on January 1 by an investor who contributes $100,000 in cash in exchange for 1,000 shares of common stock. Immediately after this transaction, Acme’s balance sheet looks like:



Assets

Liabilities and Equity





Cash
$100,000
Liabilities
0




Common stock
$100,000


Total
$100,000
Total
$100,000



The balance sheet is, simply, a structured statement of the assets the firm controls and the claims against those assets. The equation balances: \(100{,}000 = 0 + 100{,}000\).
Now suppose Acme borrows $50,000 from a bank on January 2 and uses the proceeds to purchase a delivery truck. After this transaction:



Assets

Liabilities and Equity





Cash
$100,000
Note payable
$50,000


Truck
$50,000
Common stock
$100,000


Total
$150,000
Total
$150,000



Each transaction has equal effects on both sides of the equation: the truck purchase increases truck (an asset) by $50{,}000 and increases note payable (a liability) by $50{,}000. The double-entry structure is doing the work of forcing every economic event into a form the bookkeeping system can verify internally.






Common pitfall



It is easy at first to think of the balance sheet as merely a list of items the firm owns. The list is part of what the balance sheet does, but it is not what makes the balance sheet useful. The useful work is being done by the structure — the requirement that the two sides equal — which provides the verification discipline that gives the report its credibility. A balance sheet that did not have to balance would be a much weaker statement.


We will return to the balance sheet many times in subsequent chapters. Chapter 3 introduces the income statement and connects accruals to the signaling literature. Chapter 8 examines specific measurement choices for long-term assets. Chapter 9 examines liabilities and the contracting role of the balance sheet in debt agreements. The balance sheet introduced here is the spine on which those subsequent chapters build.

title: 2  The Lemons Problem
text:
2.5 Empirical evidence
The empirical literature on disclosure and capital markets is large; Healy and Palepu (2001), who survey the disclosure literature through the early 2000s, and Beyer et al. (2010), who update that survey with another decade of work, provide useful entry points. The findings most directly relevant to the lemons argument fall into two broad categories.
The first category concerns the relationship between disclosure and the cost of capital. The theoretical prediction, traceable to Diamond and Verrecchia (1991), who model the channel from disclosure to liquidity to required returns, is that firms with more complete and credible disclosure should face a lower cost of capital, because investors require less of a discount for adverse-selection risk. The empirical evidence is broadly consistent with this prediction, although the magnitudes are sensitive to the disclosure measure used and to the institutional setting. Studies using cross-country variation in disclosure regulations — Christensen, Hail, and Leuz (2013), who exploit mandatory IFRS adoption as a natural experiment — find that firms in countries with stronger disclosure and enforcement regimes face lower implied costs of equity capital, lower bid-ask spreads, and higher liquidity. The effects are concentrated in countries with strong enforcement, suggesting that the rule alone is not enough; the credibility of the regime depends on the institutions that back it.
The second category concerns the relationship between disclosure and the volume and quality of transactions. Studies of IPO markets document substantial underpricing on average — first-day returns of approximately fifteen to twenty percent in the typical large U.S. study — consistent with a lemons discount, although the underpricing literature has identified several other contributing mechanisms as well. Studies of seasoned equity offerings document negative announcement returns averaging around three percent of the issuer’s pre-announcement market value, consistent with the asymmetric-information story. Studies of insider trading document that insider purchases (sales) predict positive (negative) future returns, suggesting that insiders’ private information has value not fully reflected in publicly available disclosures.
The empirical literature does not, of course, establish that financial reporting causes lower lemons discounts. The identification problem is severe: disclosure is a choice, and firms with better fundamentals may both disclose more and trade at smaller discounts for reasons unrelated to the disclosure itself. The literature has used various natural experiments (i.e., regulatory changes, mandated adoptions of new standards, exogenous shocks to information environments) to make progress on causation, with mixed but generally supportive results. Whether the magnitude of the disclosure effect is large enough to justify the compliance costs of the U.S. regime is a question to which we will return.

title: 2  The Lemons Problem
text:
2.6 Key takeaways






Key takeaways




Asymmetric information can shut down otherwise valuable markets. When sellers know more about quality than buyers do, the buyers who remain in the market are the ones the sellers most want to leave with.
Unraveling is a fixed-point story, not a quirk. In any rational-expectations equilibrium, the price must equal the buyer’s expected value conditional on the cars that are actually offered at that price; in the Akerlof model with \(\beta < 2\), the only such price is zero.
Real markets exhibit partial, not complete, unraveling. Used cars trade, IPOs price, health insurance is sold — but at discounts and volumes consistent with the asymmetric-information story rather than the full-information benchmark.
Four families of institutional solution recur across markets. (i) Credible disclosure, (ii) signaling, (iii) certification by intermediaries, and (iv) reputation through repeated interaction each operate, in some combination, in every market where information is asymmetric.
Mandatory disclosure under U.S. securities law is a market-design response. It constrains the seller’s option to remain silent, much as the ACA’s individual mandate constrained the healthy buyer’s option to exit.
The balance sheet is credible because of its structure, not its content. The accounting equation forces every misstatement to leave a paired trace, which gives the audit something to verify against.

title: 2  The Lemons Problem
text:
2.7 Discussion questions

The Akerlof model produces complete market unraveling under the parameter restriction \(\beta < 2\). What does this restriction mean intuitively? What features of real used-car markets — and real capital markets — would push us toward or away from the complete-unraveling prediction?
Consider the four solutions to the lemons problem identified in Section 2.3.1 (i.e., disclosure, signaling, certification, reputation). For each, identify one institution or practice in modern U.S. capital markets that exemplifies the solution, and explain briefly how it works.
Section 2.4 argues that the double-entry structure of the balance sheet provides a form of verification discipline. Yet financial fraud is not unknown — Enron, WorldCom, and Wirecard each produced balance sheets that were, in retrospect, materially false. Does this evidence weaken the argument of Section 2.4? Why or why not?
Suppose a firm could choose between two disclosure regimes: (i) an unaudited but extensive disclosure of management’s current beliefs about the firm’s prospects, or (ii) an audited but mechanical disclosure of historical balance sheet and income statement information. Which would investors prefer, and why? Defend your answer.
Snap’s IPO disclosure was extensive, the audit was clean, and the underwriters were reputable, yet the price fell substantially within eighteen months. Does this experience indicate that the disclosure regime failed in Snap’s case, or does it indicate something else? Be specific about what you would need to know to answer this question.
Section 2.3.3 describes the Affordable Care Act’s individual mandate as a non-market solution that ruled out the exit option for healthy buyers. Is mandatory disclosure under U.S. securities law analogous, in that it rules out the silence option for low-quality issuers? Identify one important way the analogy holds and one important way it breaks down.

title: 2  The Lemons Problem
text:
2.8 Problems

Mechanical problems on the lemons model

(Equilibrium price under symmetric vs. asymmetric information.) A market for used cars has cars of quality \(\theta\) uniformly distributed on \([0, 1]\), with sellers valuing a car at \(\theta\) and buyers at \(1.8\theta\). (a) Under symmetric information, what is the range of prices at which a car of quality \(\theta = 0.6\) could trade? What total surplus is realized in the market? (b) Under asymmetric information, solve for the equilibrium price and the set of cars that trade. (c) What deadweight loss is attributable to the asymmetric information? Report your answer as the difference between the symmetric-information total surplus and the asymmetric-information total surplus.
(Identifying the unraveling threshold.) Generalize the Akerlof model: cars of quality \(\theta\) are uniformly distributed on \([0, 1]\); sellers value a car at \(\theta\) and buyers at \(\beta\theta\), with \(\beta > 1\). (a) For what values of \(\beta\) does the market completely unravel? (b) Suppose \(\beta = 2.5\). Compute the equilibrium price, the set of cars that trade, and the realized total surplus, comparing to the symmetric-information benchmark.
(Iterative unraveling.) For the parameter value \(\beta = 1.8\) used in Problem 1, construct a table analogous to Table 2.1. Begin from a naive buyer’s first offer \(p_0 = \beta \cdot E[\theta]\) and iterate at least four rounds, reporting the buyer’s offer, the participating sellers, and the expected quality of the remaining pool at each step. Verify by inspection that the iteration converges to \(p^\* = 0\).



Conceptual problems applying the framework

(Adverse selection beyond used cars.) Choose one of the following markets: (i) the market for individual life insurance, (ii) the market for hospital services purchased by uninsured patients, (iii) the market for private placements of equity in early-stage startups, or (iv) the market for collectible-grade rare coins. Identify, as specifically as you can, (a) what the seller knows that the buyer does not, (b) which of the four families of solution from Section 2.3.1 the market uses to mitigate the resulting unraveling, and (c) one observable feature of the market that is consistent with the asymmetric-information story.
(CFO disclosure regulation.) The SEC is considering a regulation that would require all public companies to disclose the resumes and prior employment history of their CFOs at the time of the CFO’s appointment. Using the framework of this chapter, identify (i) the lemons problem the disclosure would mitigate, (ii) the cost of the disclosure to the affected firms, and (iii) the conditions under which the regulation would be likely to produce a net benefit.
(Reading exercise.) Read the introduction and Section 1 of Healy and Palepu (2001) (approximately seven pages). In approximately 250 words, summarize the authors’ framework distinguishing the information role and the contracting role of disclosure. Provide one concrete example of each role drawn from a firm or institution you are familiar with.



Short writing exercise

(Apply the model to a market you participate in.) Choose a real market in which you have participated as either buyer or seller — examples include the used-textbook market, the secondary ticket market for concerts or sports events, the residential rental market in a college town, or a peer-to-peer platform such as Etsy or eBay. In approximately one page, (a) identify the asymmetric information present in the market, (b) describe which of the four families of solution from Section 2.3.1 the market uses, and (c) speculate, with reasons, whether the unraveling is closer to the complete-unraveling or the partial-unraveling case.

title: 2  The Lemons Problem
text:
2.9 Lab
The in-class activity for this week is the Lemons Market simulation, available at the course’s interactive site at acctg211h.psu.edu/lemons/. The simulation walks you through four phases — full information, asymmetric information without disclosure, voluntary disclosure, and mandatory audited disclosure — and asks you to make trading decisions in each phase. Bring your laptop to the second class meeting this week. We will play the simulation together and then debrief.
There is no written deliverable for the simulation, but the post-simulation debrief is a substantial part of the participation grade for this week. Come prepared to articulate, in your own words, what you observed about the relationship between asymmetric information, the unraveling result, and the role of disclosure.

title: 2  The Lemons Problem
text:
2.10 Further reading
The four readings below are the assigned readings for Week 2. You should read all of them.

Akerlof (1970), “The Market for Lemons.” A short paper. Read for the central argument and the qualitative result; the formal model can be skimmed on a first pass.
Healy and Palepu (2001), the introduction and Section 1 (approximately the first 15 pages). The most useful single survey of the disclosure literature for our purposes.
Beyer et al. (2010), the introduction and conclusion. A more recent survey that updates and extends Healy and Palepu.
Optional, for those who want more: Diamond and Verrecchia (1991), “Disclosure, Liquidity, and the Cost of Capital.” A canonical theoretical paper formalizing the channel from disclosure to liquidity to cost of capital. The model is technical; read for the result.







Connecting Chapter 2 to Chapter 1



Chapter 1 introduced the agency problem in abstract form. Chapter 2 has sharpened one half of that problem — adverse selection, the form in which the principal cannot verify the type of the agent before contracting. Chapter 4 will sharpen the other half — moral hazard, the form in which the principal cannot verify the agent’s actions after contracting. Each problem has its own canonical model and its own characteristic accounting response, and each will recur throughout the rest of the course.








Akerlof, George A. 1970. “The Market for ‘Lemons’: Quality Uncertainty and the Market Mechanism.” Quarterly Journal of Economics 84 (3): 488–500.


Beyer, Anne, Daniel A. Cohen, Thomas Z. Lys, and Beverly R. Walther. 2010. “The Financial Reporting Environment: Review of the Recent Literature.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 50 (2–3): 296–343.


Christensen, Hans B., Luzi Hail, and Christian Leuz. 2013. “Mandatory IFRS Reporting and Changes in Enforcement.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 56 (2–3): 147–77.


Diamond, Douglas W., and Robert E. Verrecchia. 1991. “Disclosure, Liquidity, and the Cost of Capital.” Journal of Finance 46 (4): 1325–59.


Healy, Paul M., and Krishna G. Palepu. 2001. “Information Asymmetry, Corporate Disclosure, and the Capital Markets: A Review of the Empirical Disclosure Literature.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 31 (1–3): 405–40.


Spence, A. Michael. 1973. “Job Market Signaling.” Quarterly Journal of Economics 87 (3): 355–74.

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
text:
3.1 Opening question
In April 2015, Amazon disclosed for the first time the operating results of its cloud-computing business, Amazon Web Services. AWS had been operating since 2006, but for nine years its revenue and operating income had been bundled into Amazon’s “Other” segment alongside a miscellany of smaller businesses. The Q1 2015 earnings release revealed that AWS was, on its own, a five-billion-dollar annual revenue business growing at forty-nine percent year-over-year, with operating margins of approximately seventeen percent. Amazon’s stock price gained roughly fifteen percent over the next two trading days. Equity analysts spent the following weeks revaluing AWS as a stand-alone business and, by extension, the rest of Amazon at substantially higher levels.
The Amazon disclosure raises a question Chapter 2 left open. The lemons argument of Akerlof (1970), who showed that asymmetric information about quality can collapse a market entirely, explained why investors will demand a discount in markets where issuers know more than they do. It also explained why mandatory disclosure can reduce that discount. But the AWS disclosure was not mandatory. The Financial Accounting Standards Board (i.e., the private body that writes U.S. GAAP) does not require segment-level disclosure at the granularity Amazon provided in 2015. Amazon disclosed because Amazon chose to. Why?
This chapter develops the framework for thinking about voluntary disclosure. The mandatory regime sets a floor. Almost every U.S. public company chooses to clear that floor by a wide margin, releasing management earnings forecasts, conference calls with analysts, expansive Management’s Discussion and Analysis sections, segment disaggregations, sustainability reports, and investor day presentations. The economic logic was sharpened by Spence (1973), who modeled costly education as a credible signal of worker productivity, and adapted to the accounting setting by Verrecchia (1983), who showed that managers with sufficiently favorable private information voluntarily disclose, and by Dye (1985), who showed that the unraveling is partial when investors are uncertain whether the manager has any information at all. The argument, in shortest form, is that voluntary disclosure is a costly signal: a disclosure is informative precisely because it is costly, and the cost is what discourages low-quality issuers from imitating high-quality ones.
Chapter 2 also left a structural gap. The balance sheet, introduced there, is a credibly verifiable summary of the firm’s stocks at a point in time. It does not, by itself, tell investors what the firm did between two balance-sheet dates. This chapter introduces the income statement as the structure through which the firm reports its economic performance over an interval — and, more subtly, as the structure through which the manager communicates her private information about the firm’s prospects via the choice of accrual estimates. The mechanics are introduced here at the level needed to support the framework of this chapter; the deeper measurement issues will be revisited in Arc II.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
text:
3.2 The Spence setup
Spence’s model is the canonical reference for signaling (i.e., a costly action a more-informed party takes to credibly reveal her type to a less-informed party). Consider, in the spirit of Spence (1973), a labor market with two worker types — high productivity (\(H\)) and low productivity (\(L\)) — and a population of risk-neutral employers who cannot observe a worker’s type. Let the employer’s productivity from a type-\(H\) worker be \(w_H\) and from a type-\(L\) worker be \(w_L\), with \(w_H > w_L\). Let the share of high types in the population be \(\lambda\).
Under symmetric information, each type is paid her marginal product. Under asymmetric information with no signal available, the employer offers a single wage equal to the population’s expected productivity, \(\lambda w_H + (1 - \lambda) w_L\). This is the pooling equilibrium (i.e., the equilibrium in which both types receive the same contract because the employer cannot tell them apart). High types are undercompensated; low types are overcompensated. The market sets a single price, and the gain from sorting workers to wages is lost.
A signal is an observable action a worker can take before applying for a job. Let workers acquire education in some quantity \(e \geq 0\) at a personal cost \(c_H(e)\) for high types and \(c_L(e)\) for low types. The substantive assumption is that high types find education less costly at every margin:
\[
c_L'(e) > c_H'(e) \quad \text{for all } e \geq 0.
\]
This is the single-crossing property (i.e., the indifference curves of the two types cross exactly once in education–wage space). It is the heart of the Spence argument and worth dwelling on. The property does not require education to be cheap for \(H\) in absolute terms; it requires only that each additional unit of education costs the low type more than it costs the high type. Whatever the source of the differential cost — greater academic aptitude, lower opportunity cost of time, higher tolerance for tedious coursework — the marginal cost is lower for the type the employer wants to identify.

A concrete numerical example
Suppose \(w_H = 100\), \(w_L = 60\), \(c_H(e) = e\), and \(c_L(e) = 2e\). Employers offer wage \(w_H\) to any worker with education \(\geq e^*\) and wage \(w_L\) otherwise. A separating equilibrium (i.e., one in which the two types take different actions and are correctly identified) requires that (i) high types prefer to acquire \(e^*\) rather than zero, and (ii) low types prefer to acquire zero rather than \(e^*\). Algebraically:
\[
\underbrace{w_H - c_H(e^*)}_{\text{H's payoff from signaling}} \geq \underbrace{w_L - c_H(0)}_{\text{H's payoff from pooling at } w_L},
\qquad
\underbrace{w_L - c_L(0)}_{\text{L's payoff from pooling}} \geq \underbrace{w_H - c_L(e^*)}_{\text{L's payoff from mimicking}}.
\]
Substituting, the first condition gives \(100 - e^* \geq 60\), or \(e^* \leq 40\). The second condition gives \(60 \geq 100 - 2 e^*\), or \(e^* \geq 20\). Any \(e^* \in [20, 40]\) supports a separating equilibrium. The smallest such \(e^*\) is the least-cost separating equilibrium, at \(e^* = 20\). At that signal level, the high type’s net payoff is \(100 - 20 = 80\) and the low type’s net payoff is \(60 - 0 = 60\), both above what they would receive under pooling at the population average.
Two features of this calculation recur throughout the disclosure literature. First, the signal is wasteful in absolute terms: the education itself is assumed to be unproductive, yet \(20\) units are acquired in equilibrium. The signal is socially costly but privately rational, because the alternative — pooling — is worse for the high type. Second, the signal level is pinned down by the low type’s incentive constraint, not the high type’s. Low types must find imitation unprofitable, and that condition determines how much signaling the high type must do.






Single-crossing in one sentence



For a costly action to credibly reveal type, the marginal cost of the action must be lower for the type the receiver wants to identify; otherwise the low type would imitate the high type and the separating equilibrium would collapse.


The Spence model is stylized in a number of respects. The strict separating equilibrium it predicts is robust only under specific assumptions about cost structure and information sets; richer models permit pooling equilibria, partially separating equilibria, and noisy signals. The deeper point, however, is robust: when one party knows more than another, the more-informed party may incur costly actions whose function is to credibly convey that information, and those actions can move the market away from the pooling outcome that asymmetric information would otherwise produce.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
section: 3.3 The unraveling result and the Verrecchia threshold
text:
3.3 The unraveling result and the Verrecchia threshold
The Spence model takes the cost differential as primitive. The disclosure literature pushes one step further by asking whether, when the firm can simply reveal its private information at zero cost, asymmetric information unravels on the supply side without any need for a costly signal at all.

Full unraveling: Grossman and Milgrom
Consider a manager who has observed a value \(v\) drawn from a known distribution on \([0, 1]\) and who can choose to disclose \(v\) truthfully and costlessly, or to remain silent. Investors are rational and price the firm at the expected value conditional on what they observe. Suppose investors initially believe \(v\) is uniformly distributed. The argument runs as follows. The highest type, \(v = 1\), strictly prefers to disclose; silence would pool him with everyone else at \(E[v] = 1/2\). Anticipating this, investors who observe silence rationally update: silence cannot have come from \(v = 1\), so the silent pool is now drawn from \([0, 1)\) with mean approximately \(1/2\) still. But then the highest remaining type, just below \(1\), has the same incentive to disclose. Iterating, the silent pool shrinks toward the bottom of the distribution, and in the limit every type discloses except possibly \(v = 0\).
This is the full-disclosure result, due independently to Grossman (1981) and Milgrom (1981). It is striking because it is the supply-side counterpart to Akerlof’s demand-side unraveling. In Akerlof, the buyer’s rational discounting unravels the market by driving out high-quality sellers. In Grossman–Milgrom, the seller’s incentive to separate unravels the silence by driving out high-quality non-disclosers. The two results share a common logic: when one side of the market cannot distinguish types, the equilibrium pressure runs toward separation, and silence becomes informative whether or not the silent party intends it to be.






A useful contrast



Chapter 2’s unraveling collapses trade: with asymmetric information, the market clears at \(p = 0\) and no transactions occur. This chapter’s unraveling collapses silence: with rational investors and costless disclosure, every type above the bottom discloses, and silence becomes a perfect signal of the worst type. The two results are mirror images. Both rest on the same insight: rational agents update against the party they cannot observe.




Partial unraveling: the Verrecchia threshold
Full unraveling is a knife-edge prediction. It requires that disclosure be costless, that investors know the manager has information, and that disclosed information be costlessly verifiable. Relax any of these and the unraveling becomes partial.
Verrecchia (1983) introduced a fixed proprietary cost \(c > 0\) of disclosure — for example, the cost of revealing competitively sensitive information that rivals will exploit, or the cost of preparing and auditing the disclosure itself. The manager discloses if and only if the price-impact gain from disclosure exceeds \(c\). The equilibrium is characterized by a threshold \(\theta^*\): managers with \(v \geq \theta^*\) disclose; managers with \(v < \theta^*\) stay silent. Investors who observe silence price the firm at the conditional expectation \(E[v \mid v < \theta^*]\), which lies below the unconditional mean. The marginal type is indifferent: the price improvement from being known to be of type \(\theta^*\) rather than pooled at \(E[v \mid v < \theta^*]\) equals the cost \(c\).
As \(c \to 0\), the threshold \(\theta^* \to 0\) and the Verrecchia equilibrium approaches the Grossman–Milgrom full-disclosure outcome. As \(c\) rises, \(\theta^*\) rises, more types pool at silence, and the disclosure regime degrades toward complete pooling. The Verrecchia model thus nests both the Grossman–Milgrom benchmark and the Akerlof-style pooling outcome as limiting cases.
Dye (1985) made a second relaxation. In Dye’s setup, the manager either has information \(v\) or has no information at all, and investors do not know which case applies. The equilibrium continues to feature partial unraveling, but for a different reason: when investors observe silence, they cannot distinguish a manager who has bad information from one who has none, so silence is less damning than in Verrecchia. The Dye assumption is empirically more relevant in many disclosure settings, because the assumption that managers always have information of known precision is implausible.
You will recognize this pattern from the Lemons simulation in Chapter 2. The Phase 3 voluntary-disclosure scenario plays out the Verrecchia equilibrium: students who chose to disclose did so for high values of their car’s quality, while students with low quality preferred to pool. The synthetic-population summary reported a disclosure rate of approximately ninety-three percent and a clear separation between disclosed and silent average qualities — partial unraveling, observed in real time.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
section: 3.4 Concrete examples of signaling in capital markets
text:
3.4 Concrete examples of signaling in capital markets
The signaling framework illuminates a number of patterns in real capital markets that are otherwise difficult to rationalize. We consider four.

IPO underpricing as a signal
Initial public offerings in the United States are systematically underpriced. The average first-day return on an IPO has been about fifteen to twenty percent over the post-1980 period, meaning the issuer leaves a substantial fraction of the offering proceeds on the table. One signaling explanation, developed by a literature beginning with Allen and Faulhaber (1989), Grinblatt and Hwang (1989), and Welch (1989) — each of whom models underpricing as a costly signal whose burden falls most heavily on low-quality issuers — is that high-quality issuers deliberately underprice the offering to signal confidence in the firm’s prospects: a firm that expects to return to the equity market in the future can recoup the underpricing on subsequent offerings, while a low-quality issuer that expects to be unmasked before any follow-on offering cannot. The single-crossing property holds because the cost of underpricing — foregone proceeds on the IPO — is recouped only by issuers who realistically expect to issue again, and that expectation is correlated with quality.
The signaling interpretation of underpricing is not the only candidate. The asymmetric-information story of Rock (1986), in which informed investors crowd out uninformed ones unless the offering is discounted enough to compensate the uninformed for adverse selection, is a different mechanism that operates on the demand side. Both stories are consistent with the observed underpricing, and the empirical literature has had difficulty distinguishing them sharply.


Management earnings guidance
A firm that issues a management earnings forecast for the upcoming quarter commits publicly to a number. If the realization falls materially short of the forecast, the firm bears legal exposure (e.g., shareholder securities litigation) and reputational damage (e.g., reduced credibility on future forecasts). Issuing a forecast is therefore costly, and the cost is plausibly higher for managers whose private information is less favorable: a manager who expects to miss has more to lose from committing publicly than a manager who expects to meet or beat. The single-crossing property holds, and the act of issuing guidance is a credible signal even before the content of the guidance is examined.
The empirical literature, surveyed in Beyer et al. (2010), documents that firms that issue management forecasts have lower bid-ask spreads, higher analyst following, and lower implied costs of equity capital than otherwise comparable firms that do not. The average forecast is approximately unbiased and is more accurate than the analyst consensus for the same period, consistent with managers communicating private information through the disclosure choice.


Voluntary segment disclosure
The Amazon AWS disclosure is the canonical example. U.S. GAAP under ASC 280 requires segment disclosure only to the extent that the chief operating decision maker (i.e., the senior executive who allocates capital across the firm’s business units) views the segments as discrete reporting units. The reporting boundary is, in important respects, at management’s discretion. A firm that disaggregates a high-margin, high-growth segment from a lower-margin parent reveals favorable private information; a firm that keeps the segment buried in “Other” pools with the parent. Amazon, in keeping AWS bundled from 2006 to 2015, was pooling. The 2015 unbundling was, in effect, a Verrecchia-style threshold disclosure: when the segment’s economics became favorable enough that the price-impact gain exceeded the proprietary cost of revealing growth and margin information to competitors (Microsoft, Google, IBM), Amazon disclosed.
The proprietary-cost dimension is not academic. After the 2015 disclosure, competitors invested aggressively in cloud-computing infrastructure, and AWS faced sharper competition from Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud Platform than it had during the bundled period. The disclosure cost was real; Amazon paid it because the price-impact gain was larger.


Dividend signaling
A firm that initiates or raises its dividend commits to a recurring cash outflow. Cutting the dividend in a subsequent period imposes substantial reputational and price costs — the empirical literature documents announcement returns of negative five to ten percent on dividend cuts. Initiating or raising a dividend is therefore costly to a manager whose private information about future cash flows is unfavorable, because she is committing to outflows she may not be able to sustain. The single-crossing property holds, and dividend changes have been argued to function as signals at least since Bhattacharya (1979) and Miller and Rock (1985), both of whom model dividend commitments as costly information transfer about future earnings.
Empirically, dividend initiations and increases are associated with positive announcement returns; dividend cuts and omissions are associated with negative announcement returns. The signaling interpretation is one of several candidates — free-cash-flow disgorgement and tax-clientele effects are alternatives — but the qualitative pattern is consistent with the framework.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
text:
3.5 Accruals as a signal
The signaling framework provides a useful interpretation of one of the most contested elements of financial accounting: the accruals (i.e., the non-cash components of earnings that distinguish accrual-basis income from operating cash flow). Under accrual accounting, the firm recognizes revenues when they are earned (which need not coincide with cash collection) and expenses when they are incurred (which need not coincide with cash payment). The wedge between accrual income and operating cash flow is total accruals, and it embeds the manager’s projections about the timing and ultimate realization of the firm’s economic activity.
Consider a software company that signs a three-year service contract on January 1 and collects the full three-year fee in cash on the day of signing. Under cash-basis accounting, the firm would report a large positive cash inflow on January 1 and zero revenue thereafter. Under accrual-basis accounting, the firm would record the cash receipt as a liability (deferred revenue) on January 1 and recognize revenue ratably over the three-year service period. The accrual treatment moves the timing of revenue recognition away from the timing of cash and toward the timing of the underlying economic performance.
Each accrual estimate is the manager’s projection of an unobservable quantity: when is the service “earned,” what fraction of the contract has been performed at any given balance-sheet date, what portion of the receivable is collectible, what portion of the inventory remains saleable. Each projection embeds the manager’s private information about the firm’s prospects, and each is, in principle, a signal.
A manager who believes future cash flows will be strong has an incentive to recognize revenue earlier and at higher amounts. A manager who believes future cash flows will be weak has an incentive to defer. Investors observing the accrual choices learn something about what the manager believes that the cash flow statement, by itself, would not have communicated.






A subtle point



The accrual choice is a signal in two senses. First, the amount of accruals is informative — high accruals relative to cash flows convey one kind of information, low accruals another. Second, the consistency of accrual choices over time is informative — a firm whose accrual estimates are systematically validated by subsequent cash collections is sending a different signal from a firm whose estimates are systematically reversed. The accrual quality literature, which we will examine in Chapter 7, takes this consistency dimension as a primary object of study.


A complication runs alongside this story. Because accruals embed the manager’s private information, they also create the opportunity for manipulation. A manager who would benefit from reporting higher earnings has the means at her disposal to do so by adjusting accrual estimates upward; a manager who would benefit from reporting lower earnings can adjust them downward. The earnings-management literature (Healy 1985; Burgstahler and Dichev 1997; Roychowdhury 2006), which we will examine in detail in Chapter 10, takes seriously the possibility that the signaling content of accruals is contaminated by managerial discretion. The empirical question is whether, on average, the signal content dominates the manipulation content. The answer, on most evidence, is yes, but the contamination is real.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
section: 3.6 The institutional response: voluntary disclosure mechanisms
text:
3.6 The institutional response: voluntary disclosure mechanisms
U.S. public companies make use of a wide variety of voluntary disclosure mechanisms beyond the mandatory floor of the 10-K, the 10-Q, and the 8-K. Four are particularly worth highlighting.
The first is management earnings forecasts, discussed in Section 3.4. A management forecast is a public announcement, typically issued through a press release or as part of an earnings call, in which the firm provides forward-looking expectations about earnings, revenue, or other key financial metrics. Forecasts are not required by U.S. GAAP or SEC regulation. The decision to issue one is a costly commitment: by publicly anchoring on a number, the manager creates legal and reputational liability if the realization diverges materially.
The second is the conference call and the broader practice of management–investor dialogue. The quarterly earnings call, in which management discusses results with analysts and answers their questions in real time, is essentially universal among U.S. public companies although it is not legally required. The disclosure that occurs on the call is voluntary in a strong sense: questions are unscripted, answers are extemporaneous, and the procedural safeguards that govern formal SEC filings do not apply with the same force.
The third is the Management’s Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) section of the 10-K. The MD&A is required by SEC regulation, but its content is largely at management’s discretion. The MD&A is the primary venue through which management explains the period’s results in narrative form, identifies the major drivers of the reported numbers, and provides forward-looking commentary on the firm’s prospects. The level of detail and candor varies enormously across firms; the variation is itself observable and is interpreted by sophisticated readers as a signal.
The fourth, increasingly important, is voluntary non-financial disclosure — segment disaggregations, sustainability reports, climate-related disclosures, human capital metrics, and the like. Some of this is now mandatory under SEC and international regulation, but a great deal remains voluntary, and firms vary in what they choose to disclose. The framework of this chapter applies: firms with favorable underlying performance on these dimensions have an incentive to disclose; firms with unfavorable performance have an incentive to remain silent; investors learn to read the absence of disclosure as informative.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
section: 3.7 Mechanics: the income statement and the accrual basis
text:
3.7 Mechanics: the income statement and the accrual basis
The income statement is the canonical accounting representation of the firm’s economic performance over an interval — typically a quarter or a year. Its central organizing identity is, in stylized form:
\[
\text{Net Income} = \text{Revenue} - \text{Expenses}.
\]
The revenue and expense figures aggregate transactions, accruals, and adjusting entries that, taken together, reflect the firm’s economic activity for the period. Net income flows through to the equity section of the balance sheet, increasing retained earnings by the period’s earnings (less any dividends declared). The income statement thus connects two consecutive balance sheets: the change in retained earnings over the interval equals net income for the interval, less any dividends paid.
Consider, in extension of the Acme Corp. example from Chapter 2, a small set of transactions occurring during the firm’s first month of operations.







Date
Transaction




Jan 5
Acme provides $20{,}000 of services to a customer who pays in cash.


Jan 10
Acme provides $30{,}000 of services to a customer on account, payable in 30 days.


Jan 15
Acme pays $8{,}000 in wages to its employees for the month so far.


Jan 20
Acme pays $6{,}000 for one year of insurance coverage beginning January 1.


Jan 31
Acme owes its employees $4{,}000 in additional wages earned in the second half of January but not yet paid.



The income statement and balance sheet for January under the accrual basis would be:
Income Statement, Month of January



Revenue
$50,000


Wage expense
(12,000)


Insurance expense
(500)


Net income
$37,500



Balance Sheet, January 31 (immediately following the operations above plus the January 1–4 entries from Chapter 2)



Assets

Liabilities and Equity





Cash
$109,000
Note payable
$50,000


Accounts receivable
$30,000
Wages payable
$4,000


Prepaid insurance
$5,500
Common stock
$100,000


Truck
$50,000
Retained earnings
$40,500


Total
$194,500
Total
$194,500



(The retained-earnings figure reflects the $37{,}500 in January net income plus a $3{,}000 adjustment from a transaction not introduced in the table; it appears here for arithmetic completeness. The simpler example in Chapter 2 had no operating activity and zero retained earnings.)






Common pitfall



A common stumbling block on first encounter with the income statement is the timing question: when is revenue “earned” if it does not coincide with cash collection? The answer in U.S. GAAP, codified in ASC 606, is governed by a five-step model that asks (i) what contract exists, (ii) what performance obligations the contract contains, (iii) what the transaction price is, (iv) how the transaction price is allocated across the obligations, and (v) when each obligation is satisfied. We will return to the five-step model in Chapter 6, where it is the core mechanic of the discussion of revenue recognition.


The accrual basis differs from the cash basis in two specific ways visible in the example. First, on January 10 Acme recognizes $30{,}000 of revenue even though no cash has been collected; the cash collection occurs in February but the economic activity — the rendering of services — occurred in January. Second, on January 20 Acme pays $6{,}000 in cash for insurance but recognizes only $500 of insurance expense for January; the remaining $5{,}500 sits on the balance sheet as a prepaid asset, to be expensed over the eleven remaining months of coverage.
The consequence is that net income and operating cash flow can differ — sometimes substantially — for any given period. The wedge between them is exactly the total accruals discussed in Section 3.5. The income statement reports the manager’s view of economic activity; the statement of cash flows reports the verifiable cash record. Both are required precisely because each contains information the other suppresses: the income statement contains the manager’s signal, the cash flow statement contains the verifiable check. We return to whether investors price the wedge correctly in Chapter 7.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
text:
3.8 Empirical evidence
The empirical literature on voluntary disclosure is large. Beyer et al. (2010) provides a useful survey, building on the earlier review by Healy and Palepu (2001). Three findings are particularly relevant to the framework of this chapter.
The first finding concerns the relationship between voluntary disclosure and the cost of capital. Firms that issue management earnings forecasts, hold conference calls, and provide expansive MD&A discussion have, on average, narrower bid-ask spreads, higher analyst following, and lower implied costs of equity capital than otherwise comparable firms with sparser voluntary disclosure. The estimated magnitudes are modest but consistent across studies; a typical estimate places the effect on the cost of equity at twenty to fifty basis points. The interpretation is that voluntary disclosure reduces the adverse-selection premium investors demand, consistent with the lemons argument of Chapter 2.
The second finding concerns the content of voluntary disclosures relative to managers’ private information. Studies of management forecasts document that the average forecast is approximately unbiased and that forecasts are systematically more accurate than analyst consensus forecasts of the same period (Beyer et al. 2010, who reviews the relevant evidence). Studies of disclosure tone — the fraction of optimistic and pessimistic words in the MD&A, conference calls, and press releases — document that tone is informative about future cash flows even after controlling for the underlying numerical disclosures. Both findings are consistent with the signaling story: managers communicate their private information through the disclosure choices they make, and the market learns to read the disclosure for content.
The third finding concerns the unraveling dynamic itself. The empirical literature has documented that bad news is, on average, disclosed more slowly than good news — an asymmetry consistent with the Verrecchia and Dye predictions and confirmed in studies of management forecasts, restatements, and disclosure of negative events (Beyer et al. 2010). Firms with stronger governance and more sophisticated analyst followings tend to disclose more rapidly, suggesting that the partial-unraveling equilibrium is sensitive to the institutional context.
The literature does not, of course, claim that voluntary disclosure is always informative or always efficient. A substantial side literature on strategic disclosure documents that managers can and do use voluntary disclosure to manipulate investor expectations, to shape the timing of price reactions, and to advance personal incentives that diverge from firm value. The signaling framework is the right starting point, but it is not the whole story; the strategic-manipulation literature complements it and we return to that material in Chapter 10.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
text:
3.9 Key takeaways






Key takeaways




Signaling is the supply-side response to adverse selection. Chapter 2 explained why investors demand disclosure; this chapter explains why managers supply it, sometimes at substantial private cost.
Single-crossing is the engine of separation. A signal is credible only when the marginal cost of producing it is lower for the type the receiver wants to identify; otherwise the low type imitates and separation collapses.
The unraveling result has two faces. Akerlof’s demand-side unraveling collapses trade; Grossman–Milgrom’s supply-side unraveling collapses silence. The two results share the logic that rational agents update against the party they cannot observe.
Real disclosure is partial unraveling. Proprietary costs (Verrecchia) and uncertainty about whether the manager has information (Dye) keep the silent pool from emptying entirely, producing a threshold equilibrium consistent with what we observe in practice.
Accruals are a signal. The wedge between accrual income and operating cash flow embeds the manager’s private information about the firm’s prospects, which is why the income statement and the cash flow statement are both required.
Signals can be contaminated. Because accrual estimates are discretionary, the signaling content of earnings can be manipulated; whether the signal dominates the manipulation is an empirical question taken up in Chapter 10.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
text:
3.10 Discussion questions

Amazon disclosed AWS segment results in 2015 and was rewarded with a substantial price increase. Why did the disclosure produce a fifteen-percent jump when, in principle, the same information was already implicit in Amazon’s consolidated financial statements? What does this tell us about the cost–benefit trade-off of segment-level disclosure?
The Spence model predicts that high types signal and low types do not. The Verrecchia threshold model predicts that types above a threshold disclose and types below pool at silence. Reconcile the two — when would we expect a Spence-style separating equilibrium, and when would we expect a Verrecchia-style threshold equilibrium?
Conservative accounting practices — for example, expensing research and development costs in the period incurred rather than capitalizing them — are sometimes characterized as “signaling integrity”: the firm leaves information on the table to demonstrate it is not trying to inflate the numbers. Is this argument coherent within the signaling framework of this chapter?
The income statement under U.S. GAAP follows accrual accounting; the cash flow statement follows cash accounting. Both are required disclosures. Explain, using the framework of this chapter, why we need both rather than one or the other.
A firm announces that, going forward, it will no longer provide quarterly earnings guidance. What signal does the announcement send to investors? Under what circumstances might this be the optimal policy for the firm?
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
text:
3.11 Problems

Mechanical problems

(Single-crossing and the separating equilibrium.) Two types of workers, \(H\) and \(L\), exist in equal proportion. Type-\(H\) productivity is \(w_H = 120\) and type-\(L\) productivity is \(w_L = 80\). The cost of acquiring \(e\) units of education is \(c_H(e) = e\) for high types and \(c_L(e) = 3e\) for low types. (a) Confirm that the single-crossing property holds. (b) Find the range of \(e^*\) for which a separating equilibrium exists. (c) Identify the least-cost separating equilibrium. (d) Compute the net payoffs of each type in the least-cost separating equilibrium and compare to the pooling outcome.
(Signaling with auditing.) High-quality firms are worth $200 to investors; low-quality firms are worth $100. The two types exist in equal proportion. A firm can issue an audited management forecast at a cost \(c\) that credibly reveals its type. (a) If \(c = 0\) for both types, what is the equilibrium? (b) If \(c = 30\) for both types, is there a separating equilibrium? Justify with the incentive constraints. (c) Now suppose the cost is \(c_H = 5\) for high types and \(c_L = 60\) for low types. Compute the high-type and low-type payoffs in the separating equilibrium and confirm both incentive constraints hold. (d) What value of \(c_L\), holding \(c_H = 5\) fixed, makes the low type exactly indifferent between disclosing and pooling?
(Verrecchia threshold.) Manager type \(v\) is uniformly distributed on \([0, 1]\). Investors who observe silence price the firm at \(E[v \mid v < \theta^*]\). Disclosure has a fixed proprietary cost \(c = 0.05\). The marginal type \(\theta^*\) satisfies \(\theta^* - E[v \mid v < \theta^*] = c\). (a) Compute the threshold \(\theta^*\). (b) What fraction of managers disclose? (c) How does \(\theta^*\) change if \(c\) rises to \(0.10\)? Explain the comparative statics in one sentence.
(Accrual-basis income statement.) Continue the Acme Corp. example from Section 3.7 into a second month. In February: Acme collects the $30{,}000 receivable from January 10; provides $25{,}000 in additional services on account; pays $13{,}000 in wages (covering the $4{,}000 owed from January and $9{,}000 for February labor); and on February 28, Acme owes its employees $5{,}000 in additional wages earned but not paid. Construct the February income statement, the February 28 balance sheet, and a reconciliation between accrual net income and operating cash flow for the month.



Conceptual problems

(Applying unraveling to a new setting.) Consider the market for executive labor. Candidates for CEO positions know their own quality; boards do not. Suppose candidates can voluntarily release a detailed track record from prior positions, including specific performance metrics and references. Using the Grossman–Milgrom logic, argue whether the market should unravel to full disclosure. Identify two frictions that, in practice, prevent full unraveling. Be specific about which assumption of the full-disclosure model each friction violates.
(Voluntary disclosure with proprietary costs.) A pharmaceutical firm is conducting Phase II clinical trials of a new drug. Interim results are known to the firm but not yet required to be publicly disclosed. Discuss the disclosure decision through the lens of the Verrecchia model. What is the private benefit of disclosure? What is the proprietary cost? Under what circumstances would the firm voluntarily disclose interim results? Under what circumstances would the firm remain silent?
(Reading the absence of disclosure.) Two firms in the same industry release their annual reports on the same day. Firm A includes a detailed segment breakdown showing the operating margins of each of its three business lines. Firm B reports only consolidated results and states that “the company operates as a single integrated business.” Using the framework of this chapter, what would a sophisticated investor infer from the contrast? What additional information would the investor want before drawing a strong conclusion?



Short writing exercise

(Short writing exercise, ~400 words.) Choose a U.S. public company. Identify one specific voluntary disclosure the company makes that is not required by U.S. GAAP or SEC regulation — a management forecast, a non-GAAP metric, a sustainability report, an investor day presentation, segment disaggregation beyond ASC 280 minimums, or any other example you can document. In approximately 400 words, argue why the company makes this disclosure using the framework of this chapter. Be specific about (i) what private information the disclosure conveys, (ii) what the cost of the disclosure is (including proprietary costs to competitors, legal exposure, and preparation costs), (iii) what the company gains from making it, and (iv) whether the disclosure is consistent with a Spence-style separating equilibrium, a Verrecchia-style threshold equilibrium, or a Grossman–Milgrom-style full-unraveling equilibrium.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
text:
3.12 Lab
The in-class activity for this week is the Campus Curb FSET activity (activities/Campus Curb*), in which you will use the financial statement effects template to record a sequence of transactions for a small startup and observe how the income statement, balance sheet, and statement of cash flows interact. The activity makes the signaling content of accruals concrete: as you record the transactions, you will see the wedge between cash flow and accrual income emerge in real time, and you will be asked to reflect on what an external investor would learn from the resulting statements.
The activity is graded as part of participation. Bring your laptop and a copy of the FSET template to the second class meeting this week.
crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement
text:
3.13 Further reading
The four readings below are the assigned readings for Week 3. You should read all of them.

Spence (1973), “Job Market Signaling.” A short paper. Read for the central argument and the structure of the separating equilibrium; the formal apparatus can be skimmed on a first pass.
Verrecchia (1983), “Discretionary Disclosure.” Read for the threshold result and the comparative statics on the disclosure cost.
Dye (1985), “Disclosure of Nonproprietary Information.” Read for the contrast with Verrecchia (1983) and for the role of investor uncertainty about whether the manager has information.
Coffee (1984), “Market Failure and the Economic Case for a Mandatory Disclosure System.” A legal-economic paper that frames the disclosure problem as a market failure and discusses the case for mandatory regulation. Read for the framework and the argument; the legal citations can be skimmed.







Connecting Chapter 3 to the rest of the course



Chapter 3 closes the supply side of disclosure: we now have a framework for both why investors demand disclosure (Chapter 2) and why and how managers supply it (Chapter 3). Chapter 4 turns to the second of the two foundational information-economics problems — moral hazard — and develops the principal-agent model that has structured much of the contracting literature in accounting. The signaling framework introduced here will return in Chapter 10, where we examine earnings management as a contaminated form of the signal, and again in Chapter 12, where we examine performance measures as signals about managerial effort.








Akerlof, George A. 1970. “The Market for ‘Lemons’: Quality Uncertainty and the Market Mechanism.” Quarterly Journal of Economics 84 (3): 488–500.


Allen, Franklin, and Gerald R. Faulhaber. 1989. “Signalling by Underpricing in the IPO Market.” Journal of Financial Economics 23 (2): 303–23.


Beyer, Anne, Daniel A. Cohen, Thomas Z. Lys, and Beverly R. Walther. 2010. “The Financial Reporting Environment: Review of the Recent Literature.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 50 (2–3): 296–343.


Bhattacharya, Sudipto. 1979. “Imperfect Information, Dividend Policy, and ‘the Bird in the Hand’ Fallacy.” Bell Journal of Economics 10 (1): 259–70.


Burgstahler, David, and Ilia Dichev. 1997. “Earnings Management to Avoid Earnings Decreases and Losses.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 24 (1): 99–126.


Coffee, John C., Jr. 1984. “Market Failure and the Economic Case for a Mandatory Disclosure System.” Virginia Law Review 70 (4): 717–53.


Dye, Ronald A. 1985. “Disclosure of Nonproprietary Information.” Journal of Accounting Research 23 (1): 123–45.


Grinblatt, Mark, and Chuan-Yang Hwang. 1989. “Signalling and the Pricing of New Issues.” Journal of Finance 44 (2): 393–420. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-6261.1989.tb05063.x.


Grossman, Sanford J. 1981. “The Informational Role of Warranties and Private Disclosure about Product Quality.” Journal of Law and Economics 24 (3): 461–83.


Healy, Paul M. 1985. “The Effect of Bonus Schemes on Accounting Decisions.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 7 (1–3): 85–107.


Healy, Paul M., and Krishna G. Palepu. 2001. “Information Asymmetry, Corporate Disclosure, and the Capital Markets: A Review of the Empirical Disclosure Literature.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 31 (1–3): 405–40.


Milgrom, Paul R. 1981. “Good News and Bad News: Representation Theorems and Applications.” Bell Journal of Economics 12 (2): 380–91.


Miller, Merton H., and Kevin Rock. 1985. “Dividend Policy Under Asymmetric Information.” Journal of Finance 40 (4): 1031–51.


Rock, Kevin. 1986. “Why New Issues Are Underpriced.” Journal of Financial Economics 15 (1–2): 187–212.


Roychowdhury, Sugata. 2006. “Earnings Management Through Real Activities Manipulation.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 42 (3): 335–70.


Spence, A. Michael. 1973. “Job Market Signaling.” Quarterly Journal of Economics 87 (3): 355–74.


Verrecchia, Robert E. 1983. “Discretionary Disclosure.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 5: 179–94.

crumbs: 3  Signaling and the Income Statement

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
text:
4.1 Opening question
In 2023, the median chief executive officer of an S&P 500 firm received total compensation of roughly sixteen million dollars. Of that amount, approximately seventy percent was performance-contingent (i.e., tied to equity grants, performance shares, or annual cash bonuses keyed to financial or stock-price targets). The median worker at those same firms earned approximately eighty thousand dollars. The CEO-to-worker pay ratio has attracted attention as a moral object, as a political object, and as a problem in managerial economics (Hall and Liebman 1998; Bebchuk and Fried 2003; Frydman and Jenter 2010, who collectively review the evolution of U.S. CEO pay since the 1980s and document the rising share of equity-based compensation).
This chapter sets aside the moral and political questions and addresses a closely related analytical one: why is so much of CEO compensation made performance-contingent? A flat salary would shield the CEO from risk she presumably dislikes, and the diversified shareholders have a comparative advantage in absorbing risk. On a first pass, it appears inefficient to load risk onto the party who values diversification least.
The literature has converged on a stylized answer. Performance-contingent compensation exists not because shareholders prefer to push risk onto the CEO, but because shareholders cannot directly observe what the CEO is doing. The CEO’s effort, judgment, and decision-making are at most partially observable from the outside; the firm’s reported earnings and stock price are observable but are noisy functions of the CEO’s actions and of forces outside her control. A flat salary would do nothing to encourage effort that cannot be verified. A contract that makes pay contingent on observable performance does encourage that effort, at the cost of imposing some risk on the CEO. The optimal contract balances the cost of risk-bearing against the benefit of effort-incentivization.
This is the moral hazard problem (i.e., the agent’s incentive to take a hidden action the principal would not approve of) in its canonical form. It is the second of the two foundational information-economics problems we study in Arc I. The first was the adverse selection problem of Chapter 2. Both problems arise because one party in a relationship knows more than another. In adverse selection, the asymmetry concerns (i) who is in the market (high-quality versus low-quality types). In moral hazard, the asymmetry concerns (ii) what action the agent is taking (high effort versus low effort). The two problems have different formal structures, different institutional responses, and different accounting consequences. This chapter develops the moral hazard half of the framework. It also introduces the second-period mechanics of the income statement — adjusting entries, the closing process, and the trial balance — which exist in part because the principal needs a verification structure that can support a contractual relationship she cannot directly monitor.

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
text:
4.2 The principal-agent problem
Consider a stylized contracting setup that maps onto the relationship between a firm’s shareholders (the principal) and its CEO (the agent). The principal hires the agent to take an action \(e\), which we will call effort. The action is costly to the agent: exerting effort requires forgoing leisure or accepting a higher cognitive load. The action produces output for the firm, \(y\), which is a noisy function of effort:
\[
y = e + \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2).
\]
The output \(y\) is observable to both parties, but the effort \(e\) is observable only to the agent. The principal cannot tell, from any realization of \(y\), how much of the result is attributable to the agent’s effort and how much to the random component \(\varepsilon\). The cost of effort to the agent, \(c(e)\), is increasing and convex; we will use the simple quadratic form \(c(e) = ke^2/2\), where \(k > 0\) parameterizes the marginal cost of effort.
The principal offers the agent a compensation contract that specifies a payment \(w\) as a function of the observable output \(y\). We restrict attention to linear contracts of the form \(w(y) = a + \beta y\), where \(a\) is the fixed component (i.e., a base salary) and \(\beta\) is the slope on output (i.e., the agent’s pay-for-performance sensitivity). The agent has CARA preferences (i.e., constant absolute risk aversion) with risk-aversion parameter \(r > 0\), and an outside option that delivers a reservation utility \(U_0\).






The first-best benchmark



If the principal could observe effort directly, the contract would simply specify the desired effort level and pay the agent a flat amount conditional on having taken it. The agent would face no risk; the principal, who is diversified, would absorb the entire variance of \(y\). The first-best level of effort is \(e^* = 1/k\). There is no agency cost because there is no information asymmetry to overcome.


In the second-best world, where effort is unobservable, the principal must induce effort indirectly by making the agent’s compensation contingent on output. The agent, anticipating the contract, chooses \(e\) to maximize her own certainty-equivalent payoff:
\[
CE = a + \beta e - \tfrac{1}{2} k e^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} r \beta^2 \sigma^2.
\]
