The first term is the expected wage. The second, \(-\tfrac{1}{2} k e^2\), is the disutility of effort. The third, \(-\tfrac{1}{2} r \beta^2 \sigma^2\), is the risk premium the agent demands for bearing the variability in \(y\) that the contract has loaded onto her compensation.
Maximizing with respect to \(e\) yields the agent’s optimal effort: \(e = \beta/k\). The agent supplies more effort when her pay-for-performance sensitivity \(\beta\) is higher and less when her marginal cost of effort \(k\) is higher. The principal, anticipating this response, chooses \(\beta\) to maximize the joint surplus net of the risk premium. The resulting optimal slope is
\[
\beta^* = \frac{1}{1 + r k \sigma^2}.
\]
The formula carries the central intuition of the section. The slope \(\beta\) is bounded above by one (i.e., full effort incentivization, in which the agent bears the entire residual risk and the contract reproduces the first-best effort level). The slope is reduced from this benchmark by the term \(r k \sigma^2\), which captures the joint cost of risk-bearing and effort-distortion. Three comparative statics follow:


higher agent risk-aversion (\(r\)) reduces \(\beta^*\);


higher marginal cost of effort (\(k\)) reduces \(\beta^*\);


higher variance of output (\(\sigma^2\)) reduces \(\beta^*\).


In the limit \(\sigma^2 \to 0\) — output perfectly reveals effort — the slope approaches one. In the limit \(\sigma^2 \to \infty\) — output is pure noise — the slope falls toward zero, and the contract reverts to a flat wage because pay-for-performance imposes risk without informative content.
This stylized model is known in the contracting literature as the LEN model (i.e., Linear contract, Exponential utility, Normally distributed output). It is a workhorse rather than a definitive characterization of the agency problem. Its predictions are nonetheless informative: in richer models the same forces are at work, and pay-for-performance sensitivities are tempered by risk-aversion and noise even when the contract form is more complex (Holmstrom and Milgrom 1991, who extend the framework to multitask settings and show that the same logic governs how a contract weights multiple performance measures).

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
text:
4.3 The Holmstrom informativeness principle
A central result in the agency literature, due to Holmstrom (1979), generalizes the LEN intuition in a useful way. Suppose, in addition to output \(y\), the principal can also contract on a second observable signal \(s\) (e.g., an audit report, an internal performance metric, or a peer-firm benchmark). Should the additional signal enter the optimal contract?
Holmstrom’s answer is sharp. Any signal that is incrementally informative about the agent’s action, conditional on the signals already in the contract, should enter the contract with non-zero weight. The converse also holds: a signal that contains no information about the action beyond what is already conveyed by other signals adds nothing useful and should receive zero weight, however precise it may be in isolation. The right test for inclusion is incremental informativeness, not precision.
The intuition runs as follows. If \(s\) contains information about \(e\) that is not already contained in \(y\), then conditioning compensation on both signals lets the principal filter some of the noise out of \(y\), reducing the risk premium the agent demands without weakening effort-incentivization. The agent is paid more accurately for what she did, and less for what the world did to her. If \(s\) contains no incremental information, then conditioning on it imposes additional risk on the agent (i.e., \(s\) has its own noise) without any offsetting reduction in noise from \(y\), and the principal does strictly better by excluding it.

A stylized two-signal example
Consider a CEO whose effort \(e\) affects two observable signals — accounting earnings \(y_1\) and the firm’s stock return \(y_2\):
\[
y_1 = e + \varepsilon_1, \quad y_2 = e + \varepsilon_2,
\]
with \(\varepsilon_1 \sim N(0, \sigma_1^2)\), \(\varepsilon_2 \sim N(0, \sigma_2^2)\), and \(\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2) = 0\). The principal chooses weights \(\beta_1\) and \(\beta_2\) on the two signals to maximize joint surplus. The optimal weights are proportional to the precisions (i.e., inverse variances) of the signals:
\[
\frac{\beta_1^*}{\beta_2^*} = \frac{1/\sigma_1^2}{1/\sigma_2^2} = \frac{\sigma_2^2}{\sigma_1^2}.
\]
Three implications follow. First, both signals enter the contract with positive weight, consistent with Holmstrom’s principle. Second, the noisier signal receives a smaller weight: if earnings are five times noisier than the stock return (\(\sigma_1^2 = 5\sigma_2^2\)), the optimal weight on earnings is one-fifth of the weight on returns. Third, neither weight is set to zero merely because the other signal exists. Each contributes incremental information about \(e\), and excluding either would force the principal to demand more effort from the remaining noisier metric.
Now extend the example to a peer-firm benchmark. Suppose the CEO’s effort affects firm output, but firm output is also exposed to an industry shock \(\delta\) that affects every firm in the sector:
\[
y_1 = e + \delta + \varepsilon_1, \quad \tilde{y} = \delta + \tilde{\varepsilon},
\]
where \(\tilde{y}\) is the average performance of the firm’s peers, \(\delta\) is the common industry shock, and \(\tilde{\varepsilon}\) is independent noise in the peer benchmark. The peer signal \(\tilde{y}\) contains no direct information about \(e\), but it contains information about \(\delta\). Conditioning on \(\tilde{y}\) lets the principal subtract the industry shock from \(y_1\), sharpening the inference about effort. The optimal contract takes the form
\[
w = a + \beta \left( y_1 - \gamma \tilde{y} \right),
\]
where \(\gamma > 0\) is the optimal filtering weight on the peer benchmark. This is the formal logic behind relative performance evaluation (RPE): pay the CEO based on firm performance minus a peer-group benchmark, not on raw firm performance. The peer benchmark is informative about effort even though it does not respond to effort directly — it is incrementally informative because it isolates the component of firm performance that the CEO can be held accountable for.






A subtle implication



The informativeness principle does not say that a signal must be precise to enter the contract. A noisy but conditionally informative signal still adds value, just less of it. Conversely, a precise but already-redundant signal adds nothing, however precise it may be. The implication for compensation design is that signals should be evaluated jointly, not one at a time.




Why earnings remain in CEO contracts alongside stock returns
The informativeness principle illuminates a question often asked about accounting numbers: should the CEO be compensated on accounting earnings, on stock returns, or on both? The stock return is, in many respects, more comprehensive — it incorporates not only the period’s earnings but also forward-looking information about the firm’s prospects. Yet accounting earnings continue to play a substantial role in CEO compensation contracts.
The Holmstrom logic supplies an answer consistent with the empirical pattern. Earnings and stock returns are imperfectly correlated. Each contains information about CEO effort that the other does not. Earnings reflect operational decisions that have not yet shown up in returns. Returns reflect investor expectations about future cash flows that the period’s earnings have not yet realized. Neither signal is redundant given the other, so both enter the contract with positive weight, with the weight on each scaled by its conditional informativeness about effort. Hall and Liebman (1998), in their study of pay-for-performance sensitivities in U.S. firms, document that accounting-based and stock-based metrics each carry economically meaningful weight in CEO compensation, consistent with the joint-informativeness reading.

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
section: 4.4 The institutional response: executive compensation contracts
text:
4.4 The institutional response: executive compensation contracts
The framework of Sections 4.2 and 4.3 explains the architecture of executive compensation at U.S. public firms. CEO pay at large firms typically has four components, each of which can be read as the institutional response to a feature of the agency problem.
Base salary is the fixed component \(a\) in the LEN model. It typically accounts for ten to fifteen percent of total compensation at large U.S. firms. Its function is to satisfy the participation constraint — the CEO must be willing to take the job rather than her outside option — while imposing no incremental risk.
Annual cash bonus is tied to accounting-based metrics for the current fiscal year, most commonly earnings per share (EPS), operating income, return on equity (ROE), or EBITDA, evaluated against a target set at the beginning of the year. The bonus typically has a threshold (below which payout is zero), a target (at which the agreed-upon bonus is paid), and a cap (above which additional performance produces no incremental payout). The threshold-target-cap structure is the source of the bunching documented by Healy (1985), discussed in Chapter 3 and again below.
Performance shares (sometimes called performance share units, or PSUs) are equity grants whose vesting is contingent on the firm meeting multi-year performance targets, typically over a three-year period. The performance metrics are usually stock-based — most commonly total shareholder return (TSR) relative to a peer group or a market index — and the structure is explicitly relative: the CEO earns the target share count if TSR falls at the median of the peer group, more if at the seventy-fifth percentile, less if at the twenty-fifth percentile, and zero if at the bottom. Performance shares are the dominant vehicle through which RPE is implemented at U.S. public firms.
Restricted stock (or restricted stock units, RSUs) are equity grants that vest with the passage of time, conditional on continued employment. They provide an undifferentiated exposure to the firm’s stock price and are intended in part to align the CEO’s wealth with the long-run value of the firm, in part to discourage short-tenure turnover. Restricted stock typically vests in equal installments over three to four years.
A representative compensation mix for the CEO of a large U.S. firm in 2023 might be (i) ten percent base salary, (ii) fifteen percent annual cash bonus, (iii) thirty-five percent performance shares, and (iv) forty percent restricted stock, with the exact mix varying by firm size, industry, and governance practices.
Three features of this architecture follow directly from the framework.
First, the contract uses multiple performance measures, consistent with the informativeness principle. Accounting earnings enter via the annual cash bonus. Stock returns enter via performance shares and restricted stock. The two measures are conditionally informative about different aspects of CEO effort, and neither is redundant.
Second, the most heavily weighted stock-based metric — TSR in performance shares — is typically evaluated relative to a peer group, not in absolute terms. This is the RPE structure of Section 4.3. Industry-wide shocks (e.g., a recession, a commodity price swing, a sector-wide regulatory change) are filtered out, so the CEO is rewarded for outperforming her peers rather than for riding a sector wave. The empirical literature documents that the use of explicit RPE in performance share plans has grown substantially since the early 2000s, consistent with the Holmstrom prescription.
Third, the contract distinguishes between accounting metrics and stock metrics in their treatment. Accounting metrics are typically evaluated against an absolute internal target (i.e., a budgeted EPS or ROE figure), while stock metrics are typically evaluated on a relative basis (i.e., TSR versus a peer group). The pattern is consistent with the framework: accounting metrics are firm-specific outputs of operating decisions, so an absolute internal target measures the CEO against her own commitments; stock returns are exposed to systematic shocks that are not the CEO’s responsibility, so a relative benchmark filters those shocks out.

The role of audited financial statements
Each of the accounting-based metrics in the contract is computed from the firm’s audited financial statements. The audit (i.e., independent third-party verification of the firm’s financial statements and, since SOX, of its internal controls over financial reporting) serves two functions in the framework of this chapter. It functions as a monitoring expenditure in the sense of Jensen and Meckling (1976), who define monitoring costs as expenditures by the principal to verify the agent’s actions. It also functions as a Holmstrom-informative signal in its own right: the auditor’s report adds independent information about the reliability of the firm’s reported numbers, which can then enter the CEO’s compensation contract through clawback provisions, performance metric definitions, or board-discretionary adjustments.
The system of internal controls sits beneath the audit. Internal controls are the policies, procedures, and infrastructure within the firm that ensure transactions are recorded properly, assets are safeguarded, and financial reports are reliable. The Committee of Sponsoring Organizations (i.e., COSO) framework, originally issued in 1992 and updated in 2013, is the dominant conceptual framework for internal controls in U.S. firms. It identifies five components — control environment, risk assessment, control activities, information and communication, and monitoring — that together are intended to produce a system in which the agent’s actions can be monitored at reasonable cost.
The Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, passed in the immediate aftermath of the Enron, WorldCom, and Tyco scandals, added a mandatory layer to the internal-controls structure for U.S. public companies. Section 404 of SOX requires management to assess and report on the effectiveness of internal control over financial reporting, and requires the firm’s external auditor to issue an opinion on the same matter. Compliance with SOX 404 is expensive — small public companies often report annual SOX-related compliance costs in the millions of dollars — and the academic literature has been mixed on whether the benefits in reporting reliability are commensurate with the costs. The Holmstrom framework supplies a tool for thinking about that question: SOX 404 creates an additional signal of management’s stewardship, valuable to the extent that the signal is incrementally informative about effort and not redundant with existing signals.


Accruals inside compensation contracts
The accruals discussed in Chapter 3 are embedded in the accounting metrics that drive the annual cash bonus. Healy (1985) documented in a foundational paper that managers respond to the structure of bonus plans by adjusting accruals. When a manager is below the lower threshold of her bonus plan, she takes an income-decreasing accrual to clean the slate (i.e., a “big bath”); when she is above the upper cap, she takes another income-decreasing accrual to bank earnings for future periods; when she is in the incentive-eligible range, she takes income-increasing accruals to maximize the bonus. The Healy result is the classic empirical demonstration that compensation contracts and accruals are mutually endogenous — the contract creates incentives that the agent responds to with the accounting tools at her disposal. We will return to the earnings-management literature in detail in Chapter 10.
The Healy pattern is a cost of using accounting metrics in compensation, but it does not by itself imply that accounting metrics should be excluded. The informativeness principle compares signals jointly, and accounting metrics retain incremental content about effort even after accounting for managerial discretion. The institutional response has been to constrain the discretion (e.g., through audited statements, internal controls, clawback provisions, and audit-committee oversight) rather than to abandon the metric.

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
section: 4.5 Mechanics: the closing process and the trial balance
text:
4.5 Mechanics: the closing process and the trial balance
The mechanics of the income statement and the balance sheet have, by this chapter, been introduced in pieces. Chapter 2 introduced the accounting equation and the structure of the balance sheet. Chapter 3 introduced the income statement and the accrual basis. This chapter introduces the operational machinery that takes a period’s transactions and adjusting entries and produces the audited financial statements that will eventually enter the CEO’s compensation contract. The machinery is the closing process.
The closing process has three steps. First, adjusting entries are posted at the end of the period to bring revenue and expense accounts into line with the accrual basis. The adjustments include (i) revenues earned but not yet collected, (ii) expenses incurred but not yet paid, (iii) revenues collected but not yet earned, and (iv) expenses paid but not yet incurred. Each adjustment is an estimate the manager makes about the timing of economic activity; in aggregate, the adjustments determine the period’s accruals.
Second, the trial balance is computed to verify that the total of all debits equals the total of all credits across all accounts. The trial balance is the verification step in the accounting system: if the equation \(\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity}\) does not hold, something is wrong. The trial balance is an internal check that the bookkeeping infrastructure is self-consistent. It does not verify that any individual entry is correct — only that the entries are mutually consistent. A firm whose trial balance does not tie has, at minimum, a bookkeeping error; it may have something worse.
Third, the closing entries move the period’s revenues and expenses from the income-statement accounts into the equity section of the balance sheet via the retained-earnings account. After the closing entries are posted, the income-statement accounts have a zero balance and are ready to begin accumulating the next period’s activity. The balance-sheet accounts carry forward into the next period at their post-closing balances.
The closing process is straightforward as a piece of mechanics, but it is worth pausing to note what it accomplishes from the perspective of this chapter. The process produces a structured, internally consistent set of financial statements that has been subjected to the verification discipline of the double-entry system. The output of the process — the period-end financial statements — is what the auditor reviews, what the SEC files contain, what the compensation committee uses to determine bonus payouts, what shareholders receive, and what enters the empirical literature as the firm’s reported earnings, assets, liabilities, and equity for the period. The closing process is, in an important sense, the bridge between the firm’s underlying economic activity and the public artifact that the rest of the institutional system acts upon.






Common pitfall



It is tempting on first encounter to treat the closing process as a clerical step — once the transactions have been recorded, the statements simply emerge. The framework of this chapter suggests a different view. The closing process is the moment at which the manager’s discretion, exercised through the choice of accruals and adjusting entries, gets baked into the period’s reported numbers. The verification structure of the trial balance ensures that the resulting numbers are arithmetically consistent. It does not ensure that the underlying estimates are unbiased. That is the auditor’s job.

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
text:
4.6 Empirical evidence
The empirical literature on the moral hazard problem in executive compensation is large; Frydman and Jenter (2010) provides a useful survey that documents the rise of equity-based pay since the 1980s and the shift toward performance-vesting structures. Three findings are particularly relevant to the framework of this chapter.
The first concerns the magnitude of pay-for-performance sensitivity in U.S. CEO compensation contracts. Hall and Liebman (1998) documented that, accounting for the value of stock and option holdings, a CEO of a typical U.S. public firm in the 1990s gained or lost approximately twenty-five dollars in personal wealth for each thousand-dollar change in the firm’s market value. The figure has grown substantially since: with the rise of equity-based compensation in the 2000s and 2010s, modern estimates of the wealth-performance sensitivity for the median S&P 500 CEO are an order of magnitude larger than the 1990s benchmark. The growth in sensitivity is consistent with the framework of this chapter — the contract is increasingly designed to load risk onto the CEO in exchange for effort-incentivization.
The second finding concerns the rent-extraction critique of the standard model. Bebchuk and Fried (2003) argued, drawing on a substantial empirical literature, that the magnitude and structure of CEO compensation in U.S. firms cannot be explained by the standard agency model alone. They proposed a complementary view in which CEOs exercise managerial power over the compensation-setting process and extract rents that exceed what would be optimal in a clean principal-agent relationship. The Bebchuk-Fried view does not displace the standard model, but it qualifies it: the contracts we observe reflect a mixture of efficient incentive design and managerial influence, and the relative weight of the two channels remains a contested empirical question.
The third finding concerns the role of accounting numbers in compensation contracts. Accounting-based performance metrics — earnings, ROE, EBITDA, growth rates — are nearly universal in CEO bonus plans, and the weight on accounting metrics relative to stock-price metrics varies systematically with firm characteristics. Smaller firms, firms in less-volatile industries, and firms with more-complex operations tend to put more weight on accounting metrics. The pattern is consistent with the informativeness principle: accounting metrics are most useful as performance signals when stock-price signals are noisy or slow to incorporate operational improvements (Ittner and Larcker 2003, who document the role of non-financial measures in compensation and argue that firms select performance measures based on their informativeness about the actions the firm wishes to incentivize; Antle and Eppen 1985, who study the role of accounting numbers in capital-budgeting and compensation settings).
A separate empirical literature documents the prevalence of explicit RPE in performance share plans, particularly for TSR-based awards. Although RPE is the analytically clean implementation of the informativeness principle, the share of contracts that use it is smaller than the framework would naively predict. One reason, suggested in Holmstrom and Milgrom (1991), is that adding metrics to a contract creates multitask distortions (i.e., the CEO may shift effort toward the measured dimension at the expense of unmeasured ones). The fact that the empirical adoption of RPE has nonetheless grown over the past two decades is consistent with the principle, even if the speed of adoption is slower than the framework would predict.
Finally, managers respond to the structure of accounting-based incentives by adjusting accrual estimates, as Healy (1985) documented and as discussed in Section 4.4. The empirical regularity is strong enough that it has motivated a substantial literature on earnings management, which we will examine in Chapter 10.

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
text:
4.7 Key takeaways






Key takeaways




Moral hazard creates the demand for performance-contingent pay. When effort is unobservable, a flat salary cannot induce effort, so the contract loads risk onto the agent in exchange for incentivization, balancing risk-bearing cost against effort-incentivization benefit.
The LEN model identifies three forces that reduce the optimal pay-for-performance sensitivity. Higher agent risk-aversion, higher marginal cost of effort, and higher noise in output each push the optimal slope \(\beta^*\) below one.
The Holmstrom informativeness principle is a test of incremental informativeness, not precision. A signal enters the optimal contract if and only if it contains information about the agent’s action that is not already conveyed by signals already in the contract.
Earnings and stock returns are jointly informative about CEO effort. Neither is redundant given the other, which is why CEO contracts include both an accounting-based annual bonus and stock-based performance shares and restricted stock.
Relative performance evaluation operationalizes the informativeness principle. Subtracting a peer-group benchmark filters out industry-wide shocks that are not attributable to the focal CEO’s effort, which is why TSR in performance share plans is typically evaluated relative to a peer group rather than in absolute terms.
Audited financial statements and internal controls support the contract. The audit, internal controls, and the closing process supply the verification infrastructure that makes accounting numbers usable as contractible signals in the first place.

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
text:
4.8 Discussion questions

The chapter argues that CEO pay-for-performance sensitivity exists because shareholders cannot observe CEO effort. Yet many CEOs of large U.S. firms are also subject to substantial monitoring — boards meet regularly, audit committees oversee internal controls, and shareholders can vote on say-on-pay and other governance matters. Does the existence of these monitoring mechanisms reduce the optimal pay-for-performance sensitivity, increase it, or have an ambiguous effect? Defend your answer.
The Holmstrom informativeness principle says that any signal informative about the agent’s effort should enter the contract. The principle is silent on the weight the signal should receive. Suppose two signals are both informative about effort; one is more precise than the other. Should the more-precise signal receive a larger weight? Why or why not?
SOX 404 imposes substantial compliance costs on U.S. public firms. Some critics have argued that the cost is excessive and that firms would be better off if SOX 404 were repealed. Using the framework of this chapter, identify the channels through which SOX 404 produces benefits and the channels through which it produces costs. Under what conditions would the benefits dominate the costs?
The Bebchuk-Fried managerial-power view holds that CEO compensation reflects, in part, the CEO’s ability to influence the compensation-setting process. The standard agency view holds that CEO compensation is the efficient outcome of an incentive-design problem. Suppose you were to test whether the managerial-power view contributes meaningfully to the explanation of observed pay levels. What would you measure, and what would you predict?
The chapter described the closing process as the moment at which the manager’s discretion gets baked into the period’s reported numbers. Is this characterization fair? Could one argue that the manager’s discretion is actually exercised earlier — at the point at which transactions are originally recorded — and that the closing process is purely mechanical? Take a position and defend it.

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
text:
4.9 Problems

(Numerical, LEN model.) Consider the LEN model with \(k = 1\), \(r = 1\), and \(\sigma^2 = 1\). (a) Compute the optimal pay-for-performance sensitivity \(\beta^*\). (b) Compute the agent’s resulting effort, expected output, expected wage (given \(a\) chosen to satisfy the participation constraint at \(U_0 = 0\)), and certainty-equivalent payoff. (c) Now suppose the variance of output rises to \(\sigma^2 = 4\). Recompute \(\beta^*\), effort, expected output, and the agent’s certainty equivalent. Comment on the direction of the changes and reconcile them with the comparative statics in Section 4.2.
(Numerical, informativeness principle, two signals.) A CEO’s effort affects two observable signals: accounting earnings \(y_1 = e + \varepsilon_1\) and stock return \(y_2 = e + \varepsilon_2\), with \(\varepsilon_1 \sim N(0, 4)\), \(\varepsilon_2 \sim N(0, 1)\), and \(\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2) = 0\). The principal uses a linear contract of the form \(w = a + \beta_1 y_1 + \beta_2 y_2\). (a) Using the precision-weighting result from Section 4.3, compute the ratio \(\beta_1^* / \beta_2^*\). (b) Suppose the principal proposes to drop earnings from the contract on the grounds that the stock return is the more precise signal. Argue, using the informativeness principle, whether dropping earnings improves or worsens the contract. (c) Now suppose \(\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2) = 1.8\) (i.e., the two signals share noise from a common source). Argue qualitatively how the optimal weights change relative to part (a), and explain the intuition.
(Numerical, relative performance evaluation.) Continue the two-signal setup of Problem 2. Suppose firm output is exposed to an industry shock \(\delta \sim N(0, 9)\) that also enters the firm’s stock return, so that \(y_2 = e + \delta + \tilde{\varepsilon}_2\) with \(\tilde{\varepsilon}_2 \sim N(0, 1)\). The principal observes a peer-firm benchmark \(\tilde{y} = \delta + \tilde{\varepsilon}\) with \(\tilde{\varepsilon} \sim N(0, 1)\), independent of all other noise terms. (a) Argue why the optimal contract subtracts a multiple of \(\tilde{y}\) from \(y_2\) before paying the CEO. (b) Sketch how the optimal weight on residual (filtered) stock performance compares to the optimal weight that would apply without the peer benchmark. Be specific about the direction. (c) Interpret the result in the language of performance share plans at large U.S. firms.
(Conceptual, designing a CEO contract.) You are advising the compensation committee of a mid-cap U.S. manufacturing firm. The firm operates in a single industry with three close peers, has stable operating margins, and is in the middle of a multi-year capital investment program whose payoff will not be reflected in earnings or stock price for at least two years. Design a compensation contract for the new CEO, specifying (i) the mix of base salary, annual cash bonus, performance shares, and restricted stock as percentages of total compensation; (ii) the performance metric(s) for the annual cash bonus and the rationale for choosing them; (iii) the performance metric(s) for the performance shares and whether they should be evaluated on an absolute or relative basis; and (iv) one feature of the contract designed specifically to address the multi-year capital investment program. Justify each choice using the framework of this chapter, approximately 400 words total.
(Conceptual, whether a metric belongs in the bonus formula.) A board is considering whether to add customer satisfaction (measured via a Net Promoter Score survey administered quarterly by an independent vendor) to the CEO’s annual cash bonus formula, currently based only on EPS. The CFO argues that customer satisfaction is a leading indicator of future earnings and therefore belongs in the contract; the CEO argues that it adds noise and exposes her to factors outside her control. Apply the Holmstrom informativeness principle to evaluate the proposal. What information would you want to see before recommending for or against? In approximately 300 words, identify (a) the empirical conditions under which the metric should be added, (b) the conditions under which it should not, and (c) one diagnostic the board could compute from existing data to discriminate between the two cases.
(Conceptual.) Read Section II of Jensen and Meckling (1976) (the formal development of agency costs). In approximately 250 words, summarize the three components of agency cost they identify and give one concrete example of each that connects to the institutional features discussed in Section 4.4 of this chapter.
(Numerical, closing process.) Continue the Acme Corp. example from the end of Chapter 3. Acme’s pre-closing trial balance at February 28 includes the following accounts (some balances are to be supplied by the student from the worked transactions of Section 3.6 plus February’s additional activity): cash, accounts receivable, prepaid insurance, truck, accumulated depreciation, accounts payable, wages payable, note payable, common stock, retained earnings, revenue, wage expense, insurance expense, and depreciation expense. (a) Construct the trial balance, ensuring debits and credits are equal. (b) Post the closing entries to move revenue and expenses into retained earnings. (c) Construct the post-closing balance sheet and verify that the accounting equation holds.
(Short writing exercise.) Locate the most recent proxy statement (DEF 14A) of a U.S. public company of your choice. In the Compensation Discussion and Analysis (CD&A) section, identify (i) the performance metric(s) used in the annual cash bonus, (ii) the performance metric(s) used in the long-term incentive plan and whether each is evaluated on an absolute or relative basis, and (iii) the peer group used for any RPE-based metric. In approximately 400 words, evaluate the contract through the lens of the Holmstrom informativeness principle. Identify one feature of the contract that is consistent with the principle and one feature that is harder to reconcile with it. Be specific about what private information the contract is attempting to elicit and which signal is being used to elicit it.

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
text:
4.10 Lab
The in-class activity for this week is a principal-agent simulation. You will be assigned a role — either principal or agent — and will participate in a structured contracting exercise. Principals will design compensation contracts; agents will choose effort levels in response. The exercise will play out over several rounds, with the resulting outcomes (effort, output, wages, profits) tabulated and discussed in the second class meeting of the week.
Quiz 1 (covering Chapters 1–3 of the Companion) is administered at the start of the second class meeting this week. Bring a writing implement, your laptop or a calculator, and an analog watch if you have one — phones are not permitted during the quiz.

title: 4  Stewardship and Moral Hazard
text:
4.11 Further reading
The four readings below are the assigned readings for Week 4. You should read all of them.

Holmstrom (1979), “Moral Hazard and Observability.” A short paper. Read for the central result (i.e., the informativeness principle) and the framework; the formal proofs can be skimmed on a first pass.
Holmstrom and Milgrom (1991), “Multitask Principal-Agent Analyses.” Read for the extension of the informativeness principle to settings where the agent allocates effort across multiple tasks, and for the implications regarding when to add performance measures to a contract.
Jensen and Meckling (1976), Sections II and III on agency costs. (You will have read the introduction and Section II for Chapter 1; this week, focus on Section III’s discussion of the implications for outside equity and outside debt.)
A short selection from Demski (2008) on stewardship and the role of accounting in performance measurement (chapter assignment to be specified on Canvas).
Optional, for those who want more: Hall and Liebman (1998) on the magnitude of CEO pay-for-performance sensitivity in U.S. public firms. The paper is accessible at the level of an honors freshman; the formal modeling is light.







Connecting Chapter 4 to the rest of the course



Chapter 4 closes Arc I’s main theoretical content. We now have a framework for adverse selection (Chapter 2) and moral hazard (Chapter 4), the two foundational information-economics problems that accounting was developed to address; we have a framework for voluntary disclosure (Chapter 3) and for the institutional response — internal controls, audit, mandatory reporting — that has emerged around them. Chapter 5 closes Arc I by stepping up to the institutional level itself, asking why we have a FASB and what the conceptual framework is for. Arc II then turns to how capital markets actually use the accounting numbers we have spent Arc I learning to construct.








Antle, Rick, and Gary D. Eppen. 1985. “Capital Rationing and Organizational Slack in Capital Budgeting.” Management Science 31 (2): 163–74.


Bebchuk, Lucian Arye, and Jesse M. Fried. 2003. “Executive Compensation as an Agency Problem.” Journal of Economic Perspectives 17 (3): 71–92.


Demski, Joel S. 2008. Managerial Uses of Accounting Information. 2nd ed. New York: Springer.


Frydman, Carola, and Dirk Jenter. 2010. “CEO Compensation.” Annual Review of Financial Economics 2: 75–102.


Hall, Brian J., and Jeffrey B. Liebman. 1998. “Are CEOs Really Paid Like Bureaucrats?” Quarterly Journal of Economics 113 (3): 653–91.


Healy, Paul M. 1985. “The Effect of Bonus Schemes on Accounting Decisions.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 7 (1–3): 85–107.


Holmstrom, Bengt. 1979. “Moral Hazard and Observability.” Bell Journal of Economics 10 (1): 74–91.


Holmstrom, Bengt, and Paul Milgrom. 1991. “Multitask Principal-Agent Analyses: Incentive Contracts, Asset Ownership, and Job Design.” Journal of Law, Economics, and Organization 7: 24–52.


Ittner, Christopher D., and David F. Larcker. 2003. “Coming up Short on Nonfinancial Performance Measurement.” Harvard Business Review 81 (11): 88–95.


Jensen, Michael C., and William H. Meckling. 1976. “Theory of the Firm: Managerial Behavior, Agency Costs and Ownership Structure.” Journal of Financial Economics 3 (4): 305–60.

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
text:
5.1 Opening question
The United States sets accounting standards in an unusual way. The Securities and Exchange Commission (i.e., the SEC, the federal agency created by the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 with plenary authority over the financial reporting of registrants) has, since 1973, delegated the day-to-day work of writing accounting standards to a private nonprofit body, the Financial Accounting Standards Board (i.e., the FASB). The SEC retains the formal power to override or supplement the FASB’s pronouncements, but has done so rarely. The result is a regulatory structure in which most of the substantive rule-making for U.S. public-company financial reporting is conducted at substantial remove from the federal government. Most other large economies set accounting standards through government bodies, or through international bodies in which government participation is more visible.
The structural choice raises a question this chapter takes up: why does the United States set accounting standards through a private body rather than through a public agency? Behind that question is a deeper one. Why do we have mandatory accounting standards at all? Chapter 2 argued that the lemons problem creates a demand for credible disclosure, and Chapter 3 argued that voluntary disclosure unravels only partially, leaving an incomplete equilibrium. Mandatory standards fill the gap, on one reading. But the standard-setting process is itself costly — both in the direct compliance burden on preparers (i.e., the firms that produce financial statements) and in the indirect cost of standards that turn out, in retrospect, to have been poorly designed. A serious treatment of standard setting takes both sides of this ledger.
This chapter develops the framework. It traces the institutional history (the SEC’s 1934 founding, the AICPA’s Committee on Accounting Procedure and Accounting Principles Board, the FASB’s creation in 1973, the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 and the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board, and the FASB-IASB convergence project). It introduces the positive theory of accounting standards (Watts and Zimmerman 1978, which examines how firms respond to standards rather than what standards ought to be). It introduces the FASB Conceptual Framework (Financial Accounting Standards Board 2010, the FASB’s own articulation of the objective and qualitative characteristics of financial reporting). It draws on the empirical literature on the consequences of standard adoption, principally the natural experiment of mandatory adoption of International Financial Reporting Standards (Daske et al. 2008, who found that mandatory IFRS adoption was associated with increases in market liquidity and decreases in cost of capital; Christensen, Hail, and Leuz 2013, who showed that the effects were concentrated in countries that simultaneously strengthened enforcement). The chapter closes Arc I by stepping up from the firm-level analysis of Chapters 1 through 4 to the institutional level at which the rules of the reporting environment are set.
crumbs: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
text:
5.2 A short institutional history
U.S. accounting standard-setting evolved through three regimes. We sketch each briefly because each one is, in part, a response to the failure of the one before.
The pre-1934 era: stock-exchange listing rules. Before federal securities legislation, accounting requirements for publicly traded firms were set principally by the New York Stock Exchange through its listing agreements. The arrangement produced uneven coverage — firms not listed on the NYSE faced little disclosure obligation — and the substantive content of the listing rules was relatively thin. The 1929 stock market crash and the disclosures that emerged in the subsequent congressional hearings (the Pecora hearings of 1932–1934) generated political pressure for a federal regime.
The SEC and the AICPA, 1934–1973. The Securities Act of 1933 and the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 created the SEC and gave it authority over the financial reporting of public companies. The Commission could have set accounting standards directly. It chose, instead, to delegate the substantive work to the accounting profession, retaining a supervisory role. The American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (i.e., the AICPA, the professional association of U.S. certified public accountants) took up the delegation through two successive bodies: the Committee on Accounting Procedure (CAP), which issued Accounting Research Bulletins from 1939 to 1959, and the Accounting Principles Board (APB), which issued Opinions from 1959 to 1973. Both bodies were part-time committees of practicing accountants. By the late 1960s the APB had become controversial: its members were widely viewed as too close to their audit clients, its pronouncements were criticized as ad hoc, and several specific opinions (notably the 1962–1964 investment tax credit episode, in which the APB reversed itself under industry pressure) suggested that the structure was not producing standards of stable quality.
The FASB era, 1973–present. In response, the AICPA convened the Wheat Committee, which recommended replacing the APB with an independent full-time body. The recommendation produced, in 1973, the present three-tier structure: the Financial Accounting Foundation (i.e., the FAF, a private nonprofit foundation that appoints board members and oversees funding), the FASB itself (a seven-member full-time board), and the Financial Accounting Standards Advisory Council (i.e., the FASAC, a constituent-advisory body). The SEC formally endorsed the FASB’s pronouncements as authoritative GAAP in Accounting Series Release No. 150 (1973), and has accepted them with rare exceptions ever since.
The FASB’s first three decades were not without controversy. The funding structure raised concerns about independence — until 2002, the FAF was funded principally by voluntary contributions from preparers and audit firms, which created at least the appearance of dependence on the parties whose reporting the FASB was regulating. The Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (i.e., the federal legislation passed in the aftermath of the Enron, WorldCom, and Tyco failures) altered this in two ways relevant to this chapter. First, it created the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (i.e., the PCAOB, a federally chartered nonprofit that supervises the audits of public companies), shifting audit standard-setting away from the AICPA and toward a body with statutory authority. Second, it directed the SEC to fund the FASB through a mandatory accounting support fee assessed on public companies, removing the most direct lobbying channel between preparers and the standard setter.
Convergence with the IASB. The International Accounting Standards Board (i.e., the IASB, a private standard-setting body based in London that produces IFRS for adoption by jurisdictions outside the United States) and the FASB began a formal convergence project in 2002 with the Norwalk Agreement. The project produced several joint standards (revenue recognition, ASC 606 / IFRS 15, effective 2018; leases, ASC 842 / IFRS 16, effective 2019) but converged only partially in many areas and not at all in others (notably inventory, where U.S. GAAP continues to permit LIFO while IFRS does not). The SEC considered, but ultimately declined, a 2008–2012 proposal that would have allowed or required U.S. public companies to file under IFRS. The convergence project has since been described by both boards as substantially concluded, with the two bodies cooperating on specific projects but no longer pursuing a single global standard.
The institutional history is worth tracing because it locates the present structure in a particular set of compromises. We do not have the SEC setting standards directly; we have a private body operating under a public delegation. We do not have a single global standard; we have two large standards with substantial but incomplete overlap. Each feature of the structure is, in part, a response to a perceived failure of an earlier arrangement, and each carries forward both the strengths and the limitations of that response.
crumbs: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
text:
5.3 The economic theory of regulation
The standard economic model of regulation begins from a simple asymmetry. Regulation imposes concentrated costs on identifiable parties (i.e., the regulated firms, their employees, sometimes their customers) and produces diffuse benefits across a broader population (i.e., consumers, investors, society at large). The party bearing the concentrated cost has a strong incentive to lobby against the regulation; the parties bearing the diffuse benefit have a weak per-party incentive to lobby for it. The regulatory-capture literature (Stigler 1971, who modeled regulation as supplied to the industries with the highest demand for it; Easterbrook and Fischel 1984, who applied a similar logic to mandatory securities disclosure) takes seriously the possibility that, at equilibrium, regulation often serves the interests of the regulated parties more than the interests of those the regulation is nominally designed to protect.
Whether the regulatory-capture argument applies to accounting standard-setting is a contested empirical question. The argument predicts that the FASB’s pronouncements should, in equilibrium, reflect the preferences of preparers more than the preferences of users (i.e., investors, creditors, and other capital-market participants who consume financial statements). The pre-2002 funding structure described in Section 5.2 gave the prediction some bite. Since SOX, the FASB has been funded by mandatory accounting support fees, which removes the most direct lobbying channel but does not eliminate the underlying political economy. Constituents continue to participate intensively through the FASB’s due-process procedures: comment letters, public roundtables, and private meetings with the staff.
A more nuanced view, closer to the position the empirical literature has converged on, is that standard setting is influenced by all of the major constituencies — preparers, auditors, users, regulators, and academics — but that the influence is neither unidirectional nor reducible to capture. Different standards activate different coalitions. Standards that reduce reported earnings (e.g., FAS 123R on stock-based compensation, discussed in Section 5.4) face vigorous opposition from preparers but support from users; standards that primarily impose disclosure costs without altering recognized amounts face more uniform opposition from preparers. The equilibrium reflects a balance of these coalitions rather than a clean capture by any single one.
There is also a tension that runs through the structure of U.S. standard-setting itself. The FASB is, simultaneously, a private-sector self-regulator (drawing its members from the preparer, auditor, and user communities, operating without statutory authority over its own pronouncements) and an instrument of economic policy (issuing rules that bind every U.S. public company and that have measurable effects on capital markets). The two roles are not always compatible. Private-sector self-regulation prizes responsiveness to constituents and technical expertise; economic policy prizes accountability to the public interest and independence from regulated parties. Standard-setting institutions in other jurisdictions resolve the tension differently — the IASB, for example, is closer to the private-self-regulator pole and depends on jurisdictional adoption for legal force, while government-set standards in some economies are closer to the economic-policy pole. The U.S. structure occupies a middle position whose stability depends on continued SEC acceptance of FASB pronouncements and on the FAF’s ability to fund the FASB without recreating the appearance of preparer dependence.






Why this matters



The political-economy analysis is a tool for prediction, not just critique. Standards that generate large, concentrated compliance costs for major preparers face vigorous opposition during the comment-letter process. Standards that primarily benefit dispersed user constituencies struggle to generate the lobbying support needed to push them through. The framework gives us a way to think about why the FASB’s standards look the way they do.
crumbs: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
text:
5.4 The case for mandatory standards
A central economic question about accounting standards is why we have them at all. Why not let firms choose their own accounting policies, with the market disciplining inadequate disclosure through higher cost of capital? Three answers emerge from the literature.
Positive externalities of comparability. Investors pricing a firm need to compare it to peer firms in the same market. If each firm chooses its own measurement bases, the comparison requires the investor to translate across measurement conventions before drawing inferences. The translation is costly, and the cost is borne by each user separately, while the benefit of standardized measurement is enjoyed by all users collectively. A single firm cannot capture the full social benefit of conforming to a common measurement convention, because the benefit accrues to users who do not pay the firm for it. The argument is a textbook positive-externality case for coordination: each firm, choosing in isolation, may underinvest in comparability relative to what is socially optimal.
Coordination failures in voluntary disclosure. Chapter 3 developed the unraveling argument for voluntary disclosure, in which firms with favorable private information disclose to separate themselves from worse types. The argument is partial: unraveling stalls when disclosure is costly, when the relevant information is uncertain to the manager herself, or when the manager has multiple audiences with conflicting interests. Mandatory standards remove the manager’s discretion not to disclose, and so produce disclosure equilibria that voluntary regimes cannot reach. The argument does not depend on any particular standard being well-designed; it depends only on the claim that some baseline of disclosure is socially valuable and would not be supplied at sufficient volume by uncoordinated firms.
Network externalities and switching costs. Once a measurement convention is in place, the convention itself generates value that grows with the number of firms using it. Investors trained to read U.S. GAAP, auditors trained to attest to it, and software systems built to produce it constitute a network whose value to each participant rises with the size of the network. Network externalities argue for a single convention rather than multiple competing ones, and for stability of the convention over time. They are part of the reason that wholesale changes to GAAP — the IFRS adoption proposal of 2008–2012, for example — generate substantial resistance from preparers, auditors, and users alike. They are also part of the reason that the FASB-IASB convergence project ultimately stopped short of a single global standard: the costs of disrupting two large existing networks were judged to exceed the benefits of merging them.
Against these arguments stands a set of standard objections. Mandatory standards can codify particular measurement choices that turn out, in retrospect, to be inferior to alternatives the standards have foreclosed. The political-economy analysis of Section 5.3 suggests that the codified choices may reflect the influence of preparers more than that of users. And mandatory standards impose compliance costs that fall disproportionately on smaller firms, which face a fixed cost of standard-setting compliance that does not scale with firm size. The literature reflected in Easterbrook and Fischel (1984) takes the objections seriously and reaches a qualified conclusion: mandatory disclosure regulation is defensible when the benefits of standardization exceed the costs of regulatory error, but the comparison is sensitive to features of the specific standard and the institutional setting.






A concrete illustration: FAS 123R and stock-based compensation



The contested 2004 adoption of Statement of Financial Accounting Standards No. 123R (i.e., the standard requiring U.S. public companies to recognize the fair value of stock-based compensation as an expense on the income statement) illustrates the political economy and the externality argument together. Before FAS 123R, firms granting stock options to employees were permitted to disclose the fair value of the options in the footnotes while reporting zero compensation expense for the options on the income statement. The footnote disclosure was, in principle, sufficient to allow sophisticated users to compute an adjusted measure of earnings, but the disclosure was inconsistent across firms and the adjustment was costly to perform.
The FASB proposed mandatory recognition (i.e., bringing the expense onto the face of the income statement rather than leaving it in the footnotes) in the early 2000s. The proposal faced organized opposition from the technology industry, which argued that the resulting reduction in reported earnings would harm capital formation and that the fair value of employee stock options was difficult to estimate. Congress held hearings; legislation was introduced to override the FASB. The standard ultimately took effect in 2005–2006 in substantially the form the FASB had proposed. Subsequent empirical work documented that recognized stock-based compensation expense did enter the prices of affected firms more completely than the prior footnote disclosure had, consistent with the externality and unraveling arguments for mandatory recognition.
The episode is a clean illustration of the tension Section 5.3 described. Mandatory recognition of stock-based compensation was, in part, an exercise of economic policy — the FASB was choosing, on behalf of capital markets, how a particular form of compensation should be measured. It was also, in part, an exercise of private-sector self-regulation — the FASB’s decision survived congressional pressure because the SEC and a coalition of users supported it. The outcome was neither a clean capture by preparers nor a clean victory for users, but a contested equilibrium produced by the structure described in Section 5.2.
crumbs: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
section: 5.5 Watts and Zimmerman: a positive theory of standards
text:
5.5 Watts and Zimmerman: a positive theory of standards
Watts and Zimmerman (1978) made a related but distinct contribution. Where the Stigler–Peltzman–Posner literature analyzed the supply of regulation, Watts and Zimmerman analyzed the demand for accounting choices — how firms respond to the discretion that accounting standards leave them. Their argument was framed as a positive theory (i.e., a theory about what firms actually do, rather than what they ought to do under some normative criterion). They asked which accounting methods firms select and what the regularities in those selections reveal about the economic incentives at work.
Watts and Zimmerman proposed three hypotheses, each of which generated a substantial empirical literature.
The first is the bonus plan hypothesis: managers whose compensation depends on reported earnings will, all else equal, select accounting methods that increase current-period earnings. The hypothesis follows from the moral hazard analysis of Chapter 4 — when the manager’s contract is contingent on a reported metric, the manager has an incentive to influence the metric in directions favorable to her compensation. Healy’s (1985) subsequent empirical work, discussed in Chapter 4, made the hypothesis concrete by documenting that managers respond to bonus-plan kinks by adjusting accruals in predictable directions.
The second is the debt/equity hypothesis: firms with higher leverage will, all else equal, select accounting methods that increase reported earnings or reported equity. The hypothesis follows from the contracting analysis we will develop in Chapter 9: debt contracts often condition on accounting numbers via covenants (i.e., contractual restrictions that limit what the firm may do if the numbers fall below specified thresholds). Firms close to a covenant boundary have an incentive to manage the relevant numbers, and accounting choices that increase the numbers are preferred.
The third is the political cost hypothesis: firms subject to political scrutiny will, all else equal, select accounting methods that decrease reported earnings. Politically visible firms (i.e., large firms, firms in regulated industries, and firms in consolidated industries that may attract antitrust attention) pay a cost when reported profits are unusually high, in the form of regulatory action, antitrust scrutiny, or unfavorable tax treatment. They have an incentive to mute reported earnings rather than amplify them.
The three hypotheses are positive rather than normative. Watts and Zimmerman did not claim the choices were optimal or socially desirable; they claimed only that the choices were predictable from the underlying economic incentives. The subsequent empirical literature has documented support for each hypothesis with varying strength, and the framework remains an organizing tool for the empirical literature on accounting choice.
The Watts-Zimmerman framework has implications for the standard-setting process itself. If firms respond to standards with the predictable patterns of the three hypotheses, then a standard setter who designs standards without anticipating those responses produces equilibria different from the ones intended. A standard that, in a frictionless world, would yield a particular distribution of reported earnings, will in practice produce a different distribution shaped by managerial discretion within the standard’s bounds. The implication is that “standards plus discretion” is the relevant unit of analysis, not “standards alone.”
crumbs: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
text:
5.6 The FASB Conceptual Framework
The FASB Conceptual Framework (i.e., the Statement of Financial Accounting Concepts, with the most recent integrated version published as Concepts Statement No. 8) is the FASB’s own articulation of the objective of financial reporting and the qualitative characteristics that financial information should possess. The Conceptual Framework is not itself authoritative GAAP — firms cannot violate the Conceptual Framework, and auditors cannot opine on conformity with it — but it serves as the conceptual scaffold the FASB uses when drafting and revising specific standards. It is also a useful introduction to the FASB’s own theory of what financial reporting is for.
The first chapter of the Conceptual Framework states the objective of general purpose financial reporting: to provide financial information about the reporting entity that is useful to existing and potential investors, lenders, and other creditors in making decisions about providing resources to the entity. The objective is explicitly decision-useful. The information is intended to support an external party’s decision to commit or withhold capital. It is not principally about managerial control, taxation, or regulatory compliance, though financial reports are used for those purposes as well.
The decision-usefulness orientation has consequences we will see throughout Arc II. It implies, for example, that information about future cash flows is often more relevant than information about historical cash inflows, even though the historical information is more verifiable. It implies that timely information is preferable to delayed information, even at some cost in precision. Each of these implications is contested in specific applications, and the trade-offs surface as the qualitative characteristics develop in the Framework’s third chapter.






Decision-usefulness vs. stewardship



A long-running debate concerns whether the objective of financial reporting should be decision-usefulness (the FASB position) or stewardship (the position more prominent in some European traditions and in the academic agency-theory literature of Chapter 4). The two objectives often produce similar conclusions about specific accounting choices but occasionally diverge. The 2018 update to the Conceptual Framework moved partway toward integrating stewardship, recognizing that information useful for monitoring management’s stewardship is one input into the decision to commit capital. The integration is uneasy, and the debate continues.


Qualitative characteristics: relevance and faithful representation. Chapter 3 of the Conceptual Framework articulates the qualitative characteristics that financial information should possess to satisfy the decision-usefulness objective. The Framework identifies two fundamental qualitative characteristics — relevance and faithful representation — and four enhancing qualitative characteristics — comparability, verifiability, timeliness, and understandability.
The fundamental characteristics are the two without which information is not useful for decision-making. Relevance means that the information has the capacity to make a difference in the decision being made: financial information is relevant if it has predictive value (i.e., it helps the user form expectations about future outcomes), confirmatory value (i.e., it provides feedback about prior expectations), or both. Faithful representation means that the information depicts the underlying economic phenomenon completely, neutrally, and free from material error.
The two fundamental characteristics frequently come into tension. Information about a long-lived intangible asset (e.g., a brand name developed over decades) is highly relevant to investors trying to value the firm, but it is difficult to measure faithfully because intangible asset valuation requires substantial estimation. Information about a financial instrument carried at amortized cost is faithfully represented (the carrying amount is grounded in observable historical transactions) but may be less relevant than the instrument’s current fair value. The Conceptual Framework treats this tension as inherent in the trade-off between the two characteristics and does not provide a single rule for resolving it. Each accounting standard navigates the trade-off in the context of its specific subject matter.
The four enhancing characteristics support the fundamental characteristics rather than substituting for them. Comparability requires that similar transactions be reported similarly across firms and across periods, so users can compare. Verifiability requires that knowledgeable, independent observers could reach consensus that the information represents the underlying phenomenon faithfully; verifiability is closely related to but distinct from faithful representation, in that verifiability concerns the capacity for independent verification rather than the absence of error itself. Timeliness requires that information be available in time to affect decisions, recognizing that delayed information is less useful even if more accurate. Understandability requires that information be presented clearly, with the intended user assumed to have a reasonable knowledge of business and economic activities.
Each of the enhancing characteristics is the framework’s response to a problem we have already encountered. Comparability is the response to the cross-firm-pooling problem implicit in the Akerlof model: investors pricing a firm need to compare it to other firms in the same market, and the comparison requires consistent measurement bases. Verifiability is the response to the credibility problem of Chapter 2: information that cannot in principle be verified by an independent third party loses much of its value as a credible report.






Common pitfall



The qualitative characteristics are sometimes presented in introductory courses as a hierarchy or a checklist. The Conceptual Framework does not endorse that view. The fundamental characteristics are necessary conditions; the enhancing characteristics raise or lower the value of information that already satisfies the fundamental characteristics. There is no formula by which a standard setter mechanically combines the characteristics to produce a standard. The trade-offs require judgment and are at the center of every contested standard-setting decision.
crumbs: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
text:
5.7 The standard-setting process in practice
The FASB’s standard-setting process is structured to balance technical expertise, accountability, and responsiveness to constituent input. The process is often referred to as the FASB’s due process. The principal elements are as follows.
The FASB itself consists of seven members appointed to renewable five-year terms. Members are typically drawn from the preparer, auditor, user, and academic communities, with substantial financial-reporting experience in each case. The chair is appointed by the trustees of the FAF. Day-to-day work is supported by a professional staff of approximately seventy.
A new project on the FASB’s technical agenda typically begins with a project proposal and the issuance of an invitation to comment or a discussion paper. Constituent feedback is solicited through written comment letters, public roundtables, and meetings with affected parties. The Board considers the feedback in formulating a tentative position, which is issued as an exposure draft of the proposed standard. Additional comment letters are solicited; the Board considers them and may revise the proposal. The final standard is issued as an Accounting Standards Update (i.e., an ASU, which amends the Accounting Standards Codification — the authoritative compilation of U.S. GAAP).
The process can take years. The lease accounting project that produced ASC 842 (i.e., the standard that brought operating leases onto the balance sheet) was on the FASB and IASB agenda for more than a decade before the final standard became effective in 2019. The crypto-asset project that produced ASU 2023-08 moved more quickly, but most major projects involve an extended period of consultation, drafting, and revision.
The Emerging Issues Task Force (i.e., the EITF, a standing committee of practitioners that addresses narrower interpretive questions arising in practice) handles questions that do not require a full FASB project. The EITF’s consensus positions are typically incorporated into the ASC.
The structure of the process gives substantial voice to constituent groups, particularly preparers and auditors who have the expertise and incentive to participate intensively. It is, in the framework of Section 5.3, a process designed to produce information-rich standard setting under conditions of significant constituent influence. Whether the resulting equilibrium reflects the public interest, the regulated parties’ interest, or some balance of the two is a question on which reasonable analysts disagree.






IFRS vs. GAAP: the LIFO divergence



A clean illustration of the limits of convergence is the treatment of inventory. U.S. GAAP permits firms to assign costs to inventory and cost of goods sold using the last-in-first-out method (i.e., LIFO, which assumes that the most recently purchased units are the first sold). IFRS prohibits LIFO and requires either first-in-first-out (i.e., FIFO, which assumes the earliest purchased units are sold first) or weighted-average cost. The divergence is consequential: in periods of rising input prices, LIFO produces a higher cost of goods sold and lower reported earnings than FIFO, with corresponding effects on reported inventory and on taxable income.
LIFO persists in U.S. GAAP for a reason that is institutional rather than conceptual. The U.S. Internal Revenue Code’s LIFO conformity rule requires that a firm using LIFO for tax purposes also use it for financial reporting. Eliminating LIFO from U.S. GAAP would, in effect, eliminate the tax deferral that LIFO provides to firms in industries with persistent input-price inflation. The political economy of that change has been sufficient to keep LIFO in U.S. GAAP through multiple convergence rounds. The episode illustrates that convergence is bounded not only by technical disagreement but by external institutional constraints (i.e., tax law, here) that operate outside the standard-setter’s control.
crumbs: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
section: 5.8 Empirical evidence on the consequences of standards
text:
5.8 Empirical evidence on the consequences of standards
The empirical literature on the consequences of accounting standards is large and continues to grow. Two strands are particularly relevant.
The first strand examines the direct effects of specific standards on the financial statements of affected firms. The lease accounting change (i.e., ASC 842, effective 2019) added approximately two trillion dollars of operating-lease right-of-use assets and corresponding lease liabilities to the balance sheets of U.S. public companies. The revenue recognition change (i.e., ASC 606, effective 2018) reshaped the timing and amount of revenue reported by firms with multiple-element arrangements, particularly in software and telecommunications. The credit losses change (i.e., ASC 326, “CECL,” effective 2020 for public companies) altered the loss reserves of financial institutions in ways that were quantitatively material. Each of these standards generated a literature documenting its effects on reported numbers and, in some cases, on real corporate decisions. The empirical pattern is that standards do change reported numbers in the directions intended, although the magnitudes and spillovers onto real activity vary considerably.
The second strand examines the capital-market consequences of large-scale standard-setting changes. The most extensively studied case is the mandatory adoption of IFRS by approximately two dozen economies during 2005–2008. Daske et al. (2008) documented that mandatory IFRS adoption was associated, on average, with statistically significant increases in market liquidity and decreases in cost of capital. Christensen, Hail, and Leuz (2013) extended the analysis and showed that the effects were concentrated in countries that simultaneously strengthened enforcement institutions; in countries where IFRS adoption was layered onto weak enforcement, the effects were attenuated or absent. The interpretation is that the capital-market benefits of standardization depend not only on the standards themselves but on the institutional infrastructure that makes the standards credible.
The literature does not establish that any specific standard produced a net benefit to society after compliance costs are netted against capital-market gains. The cost-benefit calculation is difficult to perform and is contested in specific cases. The general claim is more modest: standards-based reporting environments produce capital-market benefits (i.e., lower cost of capital, narrower bid-ask spreads, higher liquidity) when paired with credible enforcement, and the benefits scale with the size of the affected economies’ capital markets. Whether the magnitude of the benefits exceeds the magnitude of the costs in any given case is, the literature suggests, sensitive to features of the specific standard and the institutional setting in which it operates.
crumbs: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
text:
5.9 Key takeaways






Key takeaways




U.S. accounting standard-setting is delegated. The SEC has formal authority over public-company reporting but, since 1973, has delegated substantive standard-setting to the FASB, a private nonprofit body overseen by the FAF.
The institutional structure is a product of historical compromise. The AICPA’s earlier CAP and APB were replaced because they were perceived as too close to audit clients; the FASB’s funding was restructured under SOX (2002) for similar reasons; convergence with IFRS produced a partial alignment but not a single global standard.
Mandatory standards address coordination failures voluntary disclosure cannot. Comparability is a positive externality, unraveling under voluntary disclosure is only partial, and network effects favor a single stable convention.
Standard-setting is simultaneously economic policy and private-sector self-regulation. The two roles are not always compatible, and the resulting equilibrium reflects influence from preparers, auditors, users, regulators, and academics rather than capture by any single constituency.
The Conceptual Framework grounds standards in decision-usefulness. Relevance and faithful representation are the fundamental qualitative characteristics, with comparability, verifiability, timeliness, and understandability as enhancing characteristics; trade-offs among them require judgment.
The empirical literature is supportive but conditional. Standardization produces capital-market benefits (lower cost of capital, higher liquidity) when paired with credible enforcement; the benefits are attenuated where enforcement is weak, suggesting that standards and institutions are complements rather than substitutes.
crumbs: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
text:
5.10 Discussion questions

The chapter argues that the United States is unusual in delegating accounting standard-setting to a private body rather than to a government agency. Identify one advantage and one disadvantage of the U.S. arrangement compared with the alternative of having the SEC set standards directly. Which arrangement would you defend, and why?
Watts and Zimmerman’s three hypotheses (bonus plan, debt/equity, political cost) are positive hypotheses about firm behavior. Are they consistent with the normative presumption of the FASB Conceptual Framework that financial information should be relevant and faithfully represented? If so, why? If not, what does the inconsistency imply for the standard-setting process?
The Conceptual Framework treats relevance and faithful representation as fundamental qualitative characteristics that come into trade-off with each other. Identify a specific accounting choice in current GAAP where you believe the trade-off has been resolved on the side of relevance, and another where you believe it has been resolved on the side of faithful representation. Defend your characterization in each case.
The empirical literature on mandatory IFRS adoption suggests that the capital-market benefits of standardization depend on the enforcement environment. Suppose you were advising a developing economy considering mandatory IFRS adoption. What enforcement institutions would you argue need to be in place for the IFRS adoption to produce the benefits documented in the literature?
Section 5.4 introduces FAS 123R on stock-based compensation as an episode in which the FASB resisted organized industry opposition and ultimately required recognition of an item that had previously been disclosed only in the footnotes. Construct the argument the technology industry made against recognition, and then construct the argument the FASB and the SEC made in favor. On the framework of this chapter, whose argument is stronger?
crumbs: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework

title: 5  Standard Setting and the Conceptual Framework
text:
5.11 Problems

(Conceptual.) Read Chapter 1 (Objective) and Chapter 3 (Qualitative Characteristics) of the FASB Conceptual Framework (Financial Accounting Standards Board 2010). In approximately 400 words, summarize (i) the objective of general purpose financial reporting as the Framework articulates it, (ii) the two fundamental qualitative characteristics and the relationship between them, and (iii) the four enhancing qualitative characteristics. Identify one specific accounting choice (for example, the choice between historical cost and fair value for an asset class) and use the qualitative characteristics to argue for one side of the choice.
(Conceptual — evaluating a proposed standard.) Suppose the FASB issues an exposure draft requiring U.S. public companies to recognize an estimate of the fair value of internally developed brand intangibles as an asset on the balance sheet, with annual remeasurement through other comprehensive income. Evaluate the proposal against the Conceptual Framework. Specifically: (i) which fundamental and enhancing qualitative characteristics does the proposal advance, and which does it impair; (ii) which of the Watts-Zimmerman hypotheses (bonus plan, debt/equity, political cost) would you expect to generate the most active managerial discretion in implementing the standard; and (iii) on balance, would you support the proposal? Limit your response to approximately 500 words.
