Note that the year-ten expense is forced to $3{,}422 (rather than $2{,}684, i.e., 20% of the beginning carrying amount) so that the ending carrying amount equals the salvage value of $10{,}000. In practice, firms often switch from DDB to straight-line in the year in which the straight-line expense on the remaining depreciable base exceeds the DDB expense; the switch produces a slightly different schedule but the same ten-year-end carrying amount.
Units-of-production. Suppose the asset is expected to produce 1{,}000{,}000 units over its life, and actual output in the first three years is 150{,}000, 130{,}000, and 110{,}000 units, respectively. The per-unit depreciation rate is \[
\text{Rate} = \frac{\$100{,}000 - \$10{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ units}} = \$0.09 \text{ per unit}.
\] Year-one depreciation is $13{,}500 (i.e., \(150{,}000 \times \$0.09\)); year-two is $11{,}700; year-three is $9{,}900. The pattern tracks production and is not pre-specified.
The three methods produce identical lifetime depreciation (the depreciable base of $90{,}000) but very different annual patterns. Under straight-line, expense is constant and earnings are higher in early years (and lower in late years) than under DDB. Under DDB, expense is front-loaded and earnings are lower in early years (and higher in late years) than under straight-line. The choice affects the level of reported earnings, the carrying amount on the balance sheet at any intermediate date, and the timing — but not the total — of the expense recognized over the asset’s life. The cumulative cash flows are identical across methods, suggesting that, in efficient markets, the choice should not affect firm value. The empirical evidence is consistent with this prediction for most depreciation-method differences, although tax-method choices (which do produce real cash differences) are more value-relevant.






Common pitfall



Depreciation is sometimes confused with cash outflows. The cash outflow associated with PP&E occurs at acquisition (and at any subsequent capitalized expenditure); the depreciation expense recognized over the asset’s life is non-cash. The reconciliation in the indirect-method cash flow statement (Section 7.6) makes this explicit by adding depreciation back to net income. A firm whose earnings are depressed by depreciation charges may have substantial operating cash flow even when reported earnings are low.
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice
text:
8.4 Impairment of long-lived tangible assets
The depreciation schedule established at acquisition implicitly assumes that the asset’s pattern of economic benefits will follow the predetermined allocation. The world frequently does not cooperate. A factory expected to produce widgets for fifteen years may become obsolete after eight when the relevant technology shifts. A retail location expected to be productive may become unproductive when the surrounding neighborhood changes. A piece of equipment specific to a particular product line may lose its value if the product is discontinued. In these cases, the carrying amount on the balance sheet — historical cost less accumulated depreciation — overstates the asset’s economic value.
The institutional response is the impairment test. Under ASC 360, a long-lived tangible asset is tested for impairment whenever events or circumstances indicate that the asset’s carrying amount may not be recoverable. Triggering events include (i) a significant decrease in the asset’s market price, (ii) a significant adverse change in the way the asset is used, (iii) a significant adverse change in legal factors or business climate, (iv) an accumulation of costs significantly in excess of the amount originally expected, (v) operating or cash-flow losses associated with the asset, and (vi) a more-likely-than-not expectation that the asset will be sold or disposed of before the end of its previously estimated useful life.
The test has two steps. Step one is the recoverability test: the firm compares the asset’s carrying amount to the undiscounted cash flows expected to be derived from the asset over its remaining life. If the undiscounted cash flows exceed the carrying amount, no impairment is recognized. If the undiscounted cash flows fall short of the carrying amount, the firm proceeds to step two. Step two is the fair-value writedown: the firm computes the asset’s fair value (typically the present value of expected cash flows or, where available, an observable market price) and writes the asset down to fair value. The writedown is recognized as a loss on the income statement, and the post-impairment carrying amount becomes the new cost basis for subsequent depreciation.
The two-step structure is conservative in a specific sense. The test triggers impairment only when undiscounted cash flows fall below carrying amount, which is a high bar: an asset whose discounted cash flows are below carrying amount but whose undiscounted cash flows are above carrying amount escapes impairment under step one. The asymmetry reflects the FASB’s reluctance to permit upward revaluations and is consistent with the asymmetric verifiability standard of Watts (2003), who argues that contracting parties demand higher verification for gains than for losses precisely because gain-inflation is the opportunism most costly to creditors and other stewards.
A canonical example is Boeing’s PP&E impairment associated with the 737 MAX. Following the global grounding of the 737 MAX in 2019 and the subsequent reduction in expected production rates, Boeing recognized substantial charges related to the asset base supporting 737 MAX production — including inventory writedowns, accelerated depreciation on tooling, and impairment charges on related fixed assets. The Boeing case illustrates the standard impairment trigger pattern: an external event (the grounding) materially changed the expected cash flows from a defined asset group, the recoverability test was failed, and the resulting fair-value writedown ran through earnings in the period of recognition. The size of the charges, and their concentration in a small number of reporting periods, also illustrates the lumpy character of impairment under U.S. GAAP relative to a continuous fair-value remeasurement regime.
The Watts–Zimmerman framework predicts that the timing of impairment recognition is itself a managerial choice. A manager reluctant to take a loss may delay impairment as long as the undiscounted-cash-flow test can be argued to not yet be triggered. A manager seeking to clean the slate (the “big bath” pattern from Chapter 4) may accelerate impairment in a period in which other negative news is being absorbed. The empirical literature has documented both behaviors, and the timing of impairment is a contested area of accounting discretion to which we return in Section 8.7.
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice
text:
8.5 Intangible assets
The U.S. GAAP treatment of intangible assets is, on first encounter, surprising. The central asymmetry is the distinction between acquired and internally developed intangibles.
Intangibles that are acquired in arms-length transactions — through purchase, license, or business combination — are recognized as assets at acquisition cost (or, in the case of business combinations, at fair value at the acquisition date). Acquired intangibles include patents, copyrights, trademarks, customer lists, and licensing rights. Once recognized, acquired intangibles are treated in one of two ways depending on their useful life. Finite-lived intangibles (e.g., a ten-year patent or a five-year customer contract) are amortized over their useful lives on a straight-line basis and tested for impairment when triggering events arise. Indefinite-lived intangibles (e.g., a perpetual trademark or a broadcast license with no expiration) are not amortized under ASC 350; they are instead tested for impairment annually, with the test performed at the level of the reporting unit or asset group.
Intangibles that are internally developed — through R&D spending, marketing expenditures, training programs, or the accumulation of human capital — are, in most cases, expensed as incurred. Research and development costs are expensed under ASC 730 with narrow exceptions. The principal exception is internally-developed software: costs incurred after technological feasibility is established and before general release may be capitalized under ASC 985-20 (for software sold to third parties) or ASC 350-40 (for internal-use software). Marketing expenditures, training costs, and most product-development costs outside the software exception are expensed in the period incurred, regardless of the magnitude or expected duration of their economic benefits.
The asymmetry produces measurement gaps that are large in modern intangibles-intensive industries. Apple’s roughly $30 billion in annual R&D, Alphabet’s roughly $45 billion, and Microsoft’s roughly $30 billion are recorded as period expenses, even though substantial portions of these expenditures presumably create assets with multi-year economic lives. A firm that acquires a competitor with valuable patents recognizes the patents on its balance sheet; a firm that produces equally valuable patents through its own R&D spending does not. Two firms with identical underlying economic activity can therefore have very different balance sheets depending on the legal form through which their intangibles were obtained.
The asymmetry is not arbitrary. The verifiability concern is the same one that motivates historical cost for PP&E: an arms-length transaction establishes a verifiable price; an internally-developed asset requires the firm to estimate the asset’s value by reference to expected future cash flows. The FASB’s reluctance to permit capitalization of internally-developed intangibles reflects, in part, the construct-validity worry that any internally-developed estimate sits at the manager’s discretion. The conservatism principle, in the Watts (2003) formulation, tilts the balance toward expense recognition for internally-developed intangibles even at the cost of producing balance sheets that systematically understate the firm’s economic asset base.
IFRS departs from U.S. GAAP in one notable respect. Under IAS 38, development costs (i.e., expenditures incurred after the project has reached a defined feasibility threshold) are required to be capitalized when six specific conditions are met, while research costs are expensed. The IFRS distinction is operationally narrow — most R&D spending across the IFRS-reporting universe is still expensed because it does not meet the IAS 38 development criteria — but the conceptual posture is different from U.S. GAAP: IFRS permits, and indeed requires, capitalization in defined circumstances, while U.S. GAAP does not. The empirical literature has examined whether the IAS 38 distinction produces capitalized intangible balances that are economically meaningful, with mixed findings.
The cost of the asymmetric treatment, on the Lev and Gu (2016) view, is that financial statements have lost much of their usefulness for valuation purposes as the economy has shifted toward intangible-intensive production. A balance sheet that omits the assets that produce most of the firm’s economic value is an increasingly poor representation of the firm’s economic position. The Lev–Gu argument has not produced a change in U.S. GAAP, but it has been influential in the academic literature and has informed ongoing FASB deliberations on the treatment of intangibles.






Why this matters



The asymmetric treatment of intangibles is not a technicality. It is, on Lev–Gu’s evidence, the single largest source of measurement error in modern U.S. GAAP balance sheets. Empirical work in the value-relevance tradition has documented that book-value information has lost predictive power over time, while non-financial information about innovation activity, brand strength, and human capital has gained predictive power. The pattern is consistent with the claim that the current GAAP rules are systematically failing to capture what matters most for the modern firm.
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice
text:
8.6 Goodwill
Goodwill is a special category of intangible asset that arises only in business combinations. When one firm acquires another, U.S. GAAP requires the acquirer to allocate the purchase price across the identifiable assets and liabilities of the acquired firm at their fair values at the acquisition date. The residual — the excess of the purchase price over the fair value of identifiable net assets — is recorded as goodwill on the acquirer’s balance sheet.
Mechanically, the computation is as follows. Suppose firm A acquires firm B for $1{,}000 million. The fair values of B’s identifiable assets and liabilities at the acquisition date are: tangible assets $400 million, identifiable intangibles (patents, customer lists, trademarks) $300 million, and liabilities assumed $100 million. The fair value of identifiable net assets is $400 + 300 - 100 = \(600\) million. Goodwill recorded by A is the residual: \(1{,}000 - 600 = \$400\) million. The $400 million captures items that did not qualify for separate recognition — the assembled workforce, the going-concern value of the business, the synergies expected from combining the two firms, and any element of A’s overpayment for B. It is, in practice, a heterogeneous bundle of items whose only commonality is that they did not fit into any of the other categories of recognized assets.
Until 2001, goodwill was amortized over a period of up to forty years. Statement of Financial Accounting Standards No. 142 (i.e., SFAS 142, now codified at ASC 350), issued in 2001, eliminated goodwill amortization and replaced it with an annual impairment test. Under the impairment-only model, goodwill is carried on the balance sheet at its acquisition-date amount until and unless it is determined to be impaired, at which point it is written down with the loss recognized on the income statement. The current impairment test (post-2017 simplification under ASU 2017-04) compares the carrying amount of the reporting unit, including goodwill, to its fair value. If the carrying amount exceeds the fair value, an impairment loss is recognized for the difference, capped at the carrying amount of goodwill.
The 2001 change was controversial when it was made, and remains so. The proponents argued that arbitrary amortization bore no necessary relationship to the underlying economic erosion of acquired goodwill — a one-size-fits-all schedule that destroyed information rather than producing it. The critics argued that the impairment-only model created an opportunity for managers to delay loss recognition: a manager reluctant to admit that an acquisition had been overpriced could, under the impairment-only model, decline to declare the impairment and rely on the discretion embedded in the impairment test to support that determination.
The canonical example is the AOL–Time Warner merger. In 2001, AOL acquired Time Warner in a stock-for-stock transaction valued at approximately $165 billion, generating an enormous goodwill balance on the combined entity’s books. In 2002, the firm recognized a goodwill impairment charge of approximately $99 billion — at the time the largest accounting loss in U.S. corporate history — when the post-merger economic reality failed to match the acquisition-date expectations. The AOL–Time Warner case illustrates both the scale of the goodwill that can accumulate in large business combinations and the lumpy, after-the-fact character of impairment recognition under the post-2001 regime: the loss was real, the loss was large, and the loss was concentrated in a single reporting period after a multi-quarter delay during which the underlying economic deterioration was already visible to the capital markets.
The empirical literature has produced evidence on both sides of the 2001 change. Beatty and Weber (2006) document patterns in goodwill impairment timing consistent with managerial discretion, suggesting that impairments are clustered in periods in which other negative news is being absorbed and are systematically delayed in firms whose managers face contractual or career consequences from the loss recognition. Li and Sloan (2017), in a paper titled “Has Goodwill Accounting Gone Bad?,” argue that the impairment-only model has produced systematic overstatement of goodwill on U.S. balance sheets and that the cumulative magnitude of unrecognized goodwill impairments is substantial. The literature has not converged on a definitive verdict, but the broad pattern is that the 2001 change did, as the critics anticipated, give managers more discretion over the timing of loss recognition, and the use of that discretion has not always been benign.






A subtle implication



Goodwill is, in the framework of Chapter 5, a high-relevance, low-faithful-representation account. It is highly relevant because it captures a large fraction of the value of acquired businesses. It is low in faithful representation because the underlying components of goodwill — workforce value, synergies, going-concern value — are inherently difficult to verify. The post-2001 impairment-only model is a particular institutional choice in the trade-off, and the choice has both vindicators and critics.
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice
section: 8.7 The value-relevance debate and the empirical evidence on impairment
text:
8.7 The value-relevance debate and the empirical evidence on impairment
The 2001 special issue of the Journal of Accounting and Economics contained two papers that have framed the academic debate over the value relevance of accounting numbers. Holthausen and Watts (2001), in a paper titled “The Relevance of the Value-Relevance Literature for Financial Accounting Standard Setting,” argue that the value-relevance literature has limited usefulness for standard-setting decisions. They identify several reasons. First, value-relevance studies typically demonstrate associations between accounting numbers and prices but do not establish that the accounting numbers cause the price reactions; the associations could reflect common dependence on underlying economic events that the accounting numbers and prices both reflect. Second, value-relevance studies frequently use aggregated measures that conflate the relevance of the accounting standard with the relevance of the underlying economic events being reported, making it difficult to extract guidance for specific standard-setting choices. Third, value-relevance studies are typically conducted on samples that pre-date the standard-setting decision being evaluated, raising concerns about external validity. Holthausen and Watts argue, in light of these concerns, that the standard setter should rely on a broader and more conceptual analysis rather than on value-relevance evidence alone.
Barth, Beaver, and Landsman (2001), in a paper published in the same issue, defend the value-relevance literature. They argue that the literature does provide useful evidence for standard setters, particularly when the empirical design isolates the effects of specific accounting choices and when the inference is appropriately qualified. Value-relevance evidence is one input into the standard-setting decision, on their view, not the sole basis for it. The Barth–Beaver–Landsman position has been more influential within the empirical-accounting subfield, while the Holthausen–Watts position has been more influential among standard setters and in policy-oriented discussions. The two papers do not principally disagree about the empirical evidence; they disagree about the inferences that can be drawn from it and about the role of empirical evidence in the standard-setting process.
The impairment literature speaks directly to this debate. Empirical work has documented that the timing of impairment recognition is, on average, partially driven by the underlying economics of the asset (i.e., impairments cluster in periods of adverse industry shocks and operating losses, consistent with the standard’s stated purpose) but also partially driven by managerial discretion. Beatty and Weber (2006) examine goodwill impairment decisions immediately following SFAS 142 and report that firms with debt covenants tied to net worth, firms with earnings-based bonus plans, and firms whose CEOs face career consequences from loss recognition are systematically slower to impair, consistent with the prediction that contracting incentives delay loss recognition. Li and Sloan (2017) extend the analysis through more recent vintages and document that the cumulative undisclosed goodwill impairments under the post-2001 regime are economically meaningful at the aggregate level, suggesting that delayed recognition produces a stock of impaired goodwill that is not reflected on contemporaneous balance sheets.
The empirical pattern is consistent with both interpretations of the regime. The impairment standard does identify some impairments in a timely way (the standard works), and the impairment standard also leaves substantial room for managerial discretion that is, on average, used to delay loss recognition (the standard is incomplete). The evidence is suggestive but not definitive on the question of whether the post-2001 regime is superior to the pre-2001 amortization regime. The amortization model had its own problems — arbitrary allocations, no responsiveness to the underlying economics of individual goodwill balances — and the empirical record on a regime-versus-regime comparison is mixed. The literature has not produced a consensus recommendation for a return to amortization, but it has produced sustained scrutiny of the post-2001 model and continued attention to the topic in FASB deliberations.
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice
section: 8.8 Mechanics: an extended Acme Corp. example
text:
8.8 Mechanics: an extended Acme Corp. example
The Acme Corp. example continues. Recall that on January 2 of its first year of operations, Acme purchased a delivery truck for $50{,}000 financed with a $50{,}000 bank loan. We did not, at that point, depreciate the truck because the chapter had not yet introduced depreciation. We can now do so.
Suppose Acme estimates the truck’s useful life at five years and its salvage value at \(5{,}000. Under straight-line depreciation, the annual depreciation expense is\)$ = = $9{,}000 . $$ Monthly depreciation is $750. The journal entry to record one month’s depreciation is:



Account
Debit
Credit




Depreciation expense
750



Accumulated depreciation

750



The expense reduces reported income; the accumulated depreciation account is a contra-asset that offsets the truck’s gross balance on the balance sheet. After one year (twelve months), Acme’s truck would appear as:



Item
Amount




Truck, at cost
$50,000


Less: accumulated depreciation
(9,000)


Truck, net
$41,000



Suppose, three years into the truck’s life, Acme replaces the truck’s transmission for $3{,}000. The expenditure extends the truck’s expected useful life by one year. Under U.S. GAAP, the expenditure is a capital improvement — it is added to the truck’s cost basis, and the depreciation schedule is revised prospectively to reflect the new useful life and the new depreciable base.
Suppose, alternatively, that in year four Acme determines that the truck has become obsolete due to a shift in the firm’s business and is no longer expected to generate cash flows sufficient to recover its remaining carrying amount. Acme would test the truck for impairment under ASC 360. Assume the carrying amount at the end of year four is $14{,}000 (cost of $50{,}000 less four years of straight-line depreciation at $9{,}000 per year). Suppose the truck’s remaining undiscounted cash flows are estimated at $11{,}000 and its fair value (the present value of expected future cash flows) is estimated at $8{,}000. The recoverability test fails because the undiscounted cash flows ($11{,}000) are below the carrying amount ($14{,}000). Step two writes the truck down to fair value, producing a $6{,}000 impairment loss ($14{,}000 less $8{,}000). The new carrying amount becomes the basis for subsequent depreciation over the truck’s remaining useful life.
The example is straightforward. It is included here because the underlying mechanics — capitalize at cost, depreciate over useful life, capitalize subsequent improvements, test for impairment when triggered — are the workhorse of long-term asset accounting under U.S. GAAP. Deeper applications (component depreciation under IFRS, complex leasing arrangements, asset retirement obligations) are available in any intermediate accounting textbook, and the SEC’s staff accounting bulletins are the most authoritative source for narrow interpretive questions.
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice
text:
8.9 Key takeaways






Key takeaways




Historical cost is a stewardship choice, not an oversight. U.S. GAAP carries most long-lived operating assets at historical cost (less accumulated depreciation) because verifiability and stewardship considerations outweigh the relevance gain from continuous fair-value remeasurement.
Depreciation allocates cost, not value. Straight-line, double-declining-balance, and units-of-production methods produce identical lifetime depreciation on the same depreciable base, but they differ in the timing of expense recognition and therefore in the year-to-year pattern of reported earnings and carrying amount.
Impairment is a one-way ratchet. Long-lived tangible assets are written down to fair value when undiscounted cash flows fall below carrying amount, but they are not written back up when the asset’s economics improve — an asymmetry consistent with the conservatism principle of Watts (2003).
Intangibles are treated asymmetrically. Acquired intangibles are recognized at cost or fair value and either amortized or tested for impairment annually under ASC 350; internally-developed intangibles are largely expensed as incurred under ASC 730, leaving a measurement gap that has grown with the intangibles-intensive economy.
Goodwill is the residual that captures what survives. Goodwill arises only in business combinations as the excess of purchase price over the fair value of identifiable net assets, is not amortized post-SFAS 142, and is tested for impairment annually — a regime that gives managers discretion that the empirical literature finds is sometimes used to delay loss recognition.
The empirical record on impairment is mixed. Impairments are sometimes timely and economically driven (e.g., Boeing’s 737 MAX charges); impairments are sometimes delayed in ways consistent with managerial incentives (e.g., the empirical patterns in Beatty and Weber (2006) and Li and Sloan (2017)).
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice
text:
8.10 Discussion questions

The asymmetric treatment of acquired versus internally-developed intangibles is justified, in part, by the verifiability concern: arms-length transactions establish prices that can be verified, while internally-developed intangibles require management estimates. Is this justification persuasive in the context of modern technology firms whose value is dominated by R&D and brand-development expenditures? Defend your answer.
The 2001 change from goodwill amortization to impairment-only testing was justified, in part, by the argument that arbitrary amortization destroys information. Does the empirical evidence on goodwill impairment timing support or undermine this justification? What additional evidence would shift your view?
The Holthausen–Watts critique of the value-relevance literature has been more influential among standard setters than within the academic literature. Why might that be? What does the divergence imply about the role of academic research in standard setting?
Suppose the FASB were to permit the capitalization of all R&D expenditures, with subsequent amortization over a period of, say, ten years. Using the qualitative characteristics framework of Chapter 5, identify the costs and benefits of the proposed change. Would the change be a net improvement to U.S. GAAP?
The chapter argued that the rising book-to-market gap reflects the asymmetric treatment of intangibles. Identify an alternative explanation for the gap and explain how an empirical researcher might distinguish between the two.
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice
text:
8.11 Problems

(Numerical, depreciation methods.) On January 1, Acme Corp. acquires a piece of equipment for $100{,}000. The equipment has an estimated useful life of ten years and a salvage value of $10{,}000. (a) Compute the annual depreciation expense for each year of the asset’s life under straight-line. (b) Compute the annual depreciation expense for each year of the asset’s life under double-declining-balance, forcing the year-ten expense to bring the carrying amount to salvage value. (c) Assume the equipment is expected to produce 1{,}000{,}000 units over its life and produces 150{,}000, 130{,}000, 110{,}000, 100{,}000, 100{,}000, 90{,}000, 90{,}000, 80{,}000, 80{,}000, and 70{,}000 units in years one through ten, respectively; compute the annual depreciation expense under units-of-production. (d) Verify that the three methods produce the same lifetime depreciation. (e) In approximately 150 words, discuss how the choice of method affects the income statement and balance sheet over time and identify a setting in which each method may be preferable.
(Numerical, impairment.) After four years of straight-line depreciation, Acme determines that the equipment from Problem 1 has become obsolete. The remaining undiscounted cash flows are estimated at $50{,}000, and the equipment’s fair value (the present value of expected future cash flows) is estimated at $42{,}000. (a) Compute the carrying amount of the equipment at the end of year four under straight-line. (b) Apply the two-step impairment test under ASC 360. (c) If impairment is triggered, compute the impairment loss and the new carrying amount. (d) Briefly explain what the new carrying amount implies for depreciation expense in year five.
(Numerical, goodwill from an acquisition.) On January 1, Firm A acquires 100% of Firm B for $2{,}000 million in cash. The fair values of B’s identifiable assets and liabilities at the acquisition date are: cash and receivables $300 million, inventory $250 million, PP&E $700 million, identifiable intangibles (patents, customer lists, trademarks) $450 million, current liabilities $200 million, and long-term debt assumed $400 million. (a) Compute the fair value of B’s identifiable net assets. (b) Compute the goodwill recognized by A at the acquisition date. (c) Two years later, A’s relevant reporting unit (which includes B’s assets and the goodwill from the acquisition) has a carrying amount of $1{,}900 million, including $700 million of remaining goodwill, and a fair value of $1{,}500 million. Apply the goodwill impairment test under ASC 350 (post-ASU 2017-04) and compute the impairment loss, if any.
(Conceptual, fair-value remeasurement of PP&E.) In approximately 350 words, argue for or against permitting U.S. GAAP firms to elect a fair-value remeasurement regime for PP&E along the lines of IAS 16. Address (i) the relevance argument, (ii) the verifiability and stewardship counter-arguments, and (iii) the empirical evidence on the use of the IAS 16 revaluation option by IFRS-reporting firms. The Holthausen–Watts versus Barth–Beaver–Landsman framing should appear somewhere in the response.
(Conceptual, impairment incentives.) The chapter argues that managers may have incentives to under-impair (to avoid loss recognition) or to over-impair (a “big-bath” pattern that accelerates losses into a period in which other bad news is absorbed). In approximately 300 words, describe a setting in which under-impairment is the predominant incentive and a setting in which over-impairment is the predominant incentive. Identify, for each setting, the contracting or career-incentive feature that drives the prediction.
(Applied, intangibles intensity.) Choose a publicly traded firm whose business is intangible-intensive — a technology firm, a pharmaceutical firm, a consumer-brand firm. Look up the firm’s most recent 10-K. (a) Compute the firm’s book equity and its market capitalization at the most recent fiscal year-end. (b) Compute the firm’s market-to-book ratio. (c) Identify the firm’s R&D expense and marketing or selling-general-and-administrative (SG&A) expense for the most recent year. (d) In approximately 300 words, comment on what your computations suggest about the role of unrecognized intangibles in the firm’s valuation.
(Short writing exercise.) In approximately 500 words, take a position on the following claim: “U.S. GAAP’s expensing of internally-developed R&D is the single largest source of measurement error in modern financial statements, and the FASB should respond by permitting capitalization under conditions analogous to IAS 38.” The response should engage the Lev and Gu (2016) critique, the verifiability rationale, and at least one specific firm example (e.g., Apple, Alphabet, Pfizer).
(Lab preparation.) When Lab 3 becomes available on Canvas, open the template notebook and run the data-pull section. Verify that you can produce a panel of R&D expense and SG&A expense by industry over the past ten years. Bring any errors or unexpected behaviors to the in-class lab session.
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice
text:
8.12 Lab
The in-class activity for the second class meeting of this week is the Asset Scavenger Hunt (activities/Asset Scavenger Hunt*), in which you will dissect the long-term asset and intangibles disclosures of an assigned firm and identify the most consequential measurement choices the firm has made. The activity will surface the kinds of judgment calls — useful life estimates, impairment triggers, intangible recognition decisions — that the chapter has discussed in abstract terms.
The out-of-class lab for this week is WRDS Lab 3: Intangibles intensity by industry, building on the existing data examples/intangibles.R script (which you will adapt to Python and extend). The lab walks you through (i) the construction of cross-industry measures of R&D and SG&A intensity in the spirit of Lev and Gu (2016), (ii) the comparison of capitalized to expensed intangible expenditures, and (iii) the interpretation of the resulting cross-sectional distribution. The deliverable is a Colab notebook that produces (a) an industry-level plot of intangible intensity over time and (b) a brief written discussion of what the cross-industry distribution implies about the GAAP treatment of intangibles.
Quiz 2 (covering Chapters 4–7 of the Companion) is administered at the start of the second class meeting this week. The quiz covers stewardship and moral hazard, standard setting, efficient markets and value relevance, and the accrual anomaly.
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice
text:
8.13 Further reading
The four readings below are the assigned readings for Week 8. You should read all of them.

Holthausen and Watts (2001), “The Relevance of the Value-Relevance Literature for Financial Accounting Standard Setting.” Read for the central critique and the four reasons the authors offer.
Barth, Beaver, and Landsman (2001), “The Relevance of the Value Relevance Literature for Financial Accounting Standard Setting: Another View.” The companion paper that defends the value-relevance literature. Read alongside Holthausen–Watts; the contrast is the more useful pedagogical tool.
Lev and Gu (2016), Chapters 2 and 3 (selected sections). The contemporary case that GAAP is failing to capture intangibles in a way that has consequences for the usefulness of financial statements.
Watts (2003), “Conservatism in Accounting Part I: Explanations and Implications.” Read for the four economic explanations of conservatism (contracting, litigation, regulation, taxation) and their connection to the asymmetric verifiability standard that underlies the U.S. GAAP impairment regime.
Optional, for those who want more: Li and Sloan (2017) on goodwill impairment delays. Empirical evidence on the post-2001 goodwill regime.
Optional, for those who want more: Beatty and Weber (2006) on the timing of goodwill impairments and the role of debt-covenant and compensation incentives in impairment decisions.







Where Arc II goes from here



Chapter 8 has examined the asset side of the balance sheet for long-lived items. Chapter 9 turns to the liability side. The contracting role of accounting — already introduced in passing in Chapters 4 and 5 — becomes the central theme of Chapter 9, where debt covenants, conservatism, and the bondholder-shareholder conflict all reduce to questions about how accounting numbers enter the contracting environment of the firm. The framework of relevance versus faithful representation will return as the analytical engine, but with a different emphasis: faithful representation, and specifically conservative measurement, plays a more prominent role in liabilities accounting than it does in asset accounting.








Barth, Mary E., William H. Beaver, and Wayne R. Landsman. 2001. “The Relevance of the Value Relevance Literature for Financial Accounting Standard Setting: Another View.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 31 (1–3): 77–104.


Beatty, Anne, and Joseph Weber. 2006. “Accounting Discretion in Fair Value Estimates: An Examination of SFAS 142 Goodwill Impairments.” Journal of Accounting Research 44 (2): 257–88.


Holthausen, Robert W., and Ross L. Watts. 2001. “The Relevance of the Value-Relevance Literature for Financial Accounting Standard Setting.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 31 (1–3): 3–75.


Lev, Baruch, and Feng Gu. 2016. The End of Accounting and the Path Forward for Investors and Managers. Hoboken, NJ: Wiley.


Li, Kevin K., and Richard G. Sloan. 2017. “Has Goodwill Accounting Gone Bad?” Review of Accounting Studies 22 (2): 964–1003.


Watts, Ross L. 2003. “Conservatism in Accounting Part i: Explanations and Implications.” Accounting Horizons 17 (3): 207–21.
crumbs: 8  Long-Term Assets and Measurement Choice

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.1 Opening question
Hertz filed for Chapter 11 protection on May 22, 2020. The collapse of the rental-car business during the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic was the proximate trigger, but Hertz had been operating with elevated leverage for years, and the firm’s debt agreements — like those of most highly-leveraged firms — contained covenants (i.e., contractual side-conditions) tied to accounting numbers: minimum interest-coverage ratios, maximum debt-to-EBITDA ratios, restrictions on capital expenditures, and restrictions on asset dispositions. The COVID shock pushed Hertz’s accounting metrics through the relevant thresholds, the covenant violations cascaded, the lenders accelerated, and Hertz’s ability to operate independently was effectively over.
The Hertz case is a predictable consequence of how modern debt contracts work. A creditor lending money to a firm has, in effect, sold an option that the firm will repay; the option is in the money when the firm is healthy and out of the money when the firm is distressed. The creditor faces the same information problem we have studied throughout Arc I: the manager knows more about the firm’s prospects than the creditor does, and the manager’s incentives in distress diverge sharply from the creditor’s. Debt covenants are the contractual response. They give the creditor a measurable trigger, typically tied to accounting numbers, that activates creditor rights — acceleration, renegotiation, or the installation of an outside controller — when the firm’s condition deteriorates.
This chapter develops a framework for thinking about how liabilities are measured and how debt contracts use accounting numbers. We proceed in four steps. We begin with the bondholder-shareholder conflict articulated by Smith and Warner (1979), who classified the covenants found in U.S. bond indentures into four categories that remain the standard reference forty-five years on. We then introduce conservatism — a long-recognized but contested feature of U.S. GAAP — as a contracting solution to that conflict, drawing on Watts (2003), who argues that asymmetric measurement (i.e., requiring more evidence for gains than for losses) is functional in a creditor-protective accounting regime. We work through the mechanics of bond accounting, lease accounting under ASC 842, pension and other post-employment benefit (OPEB) accounting, and contingent liabilities. We close with the empirical literature on covenants, focusing on Roberts and Sufi (2009), who document that covenant violations produce sharp reductions in subsequent investment.
The chapter sits squarely in Arc II’s empirical investigation but leans toward the internal-information theme of Arc III. The contracting role of accounting — visible most clearly in debt covenants but also present in compensation, regulatory, and supplier-customer contracts — is the long-arc parallel to the capital-markets role developed in Chapters 6 and 7. Both roles motivate accounting standards and accounting practice, and the empirical literature on each role has independently documented that accounting numbers affect real corporate outcomes.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.2 The bondholder-shareholder conflict
Consider a stylized firm financed with a mix of debt and equity. The shareholders own the residual claim on the firm’s cash flows and receive whatever is left after the creditors are paid. The creditors own a fixed claim and receive their promised payments if the firm is solvent and a fraction of liquidation value if the firm is not. The two claims have very different sensitivities to the firm’s underlying decisions.
Three particular conflicts have been identified. The first is the asset substitution problem (i.e., the post-debt incentive to take on riskier projects). After the debt is in place, the shareholders can increase the value of their claim by inducing the firm to substitute high-variance projects for low-variance projects: the upside accrues to them, while the downside falls disproportionately on the creditors. The second is the underinvestment problem (i.e., the debt-overhang incentive to forgo positive-NPV investment). The shareholders may decline to fund positive-NPV projects if much of the payoff would accrue to existing creditors rather than to the new equity invested. The third is the claim dilution problem (i.e., the post-debt incentive to issue further senior debt). The shareholders may increase the firm’s leverage by issuing additional debt, diluting the claim of existing creditors.
Each of the three conflicts is a moral-hazard problem of the kind introduced in Chapter 4. The agent (i.e., the manager, acting on behalf of the shareholders) has private information about the firm’s investment opportunities and capital-structure choices, and the principal (i.e., the creditor) cannot directly observe the agent’s actions. Each conflict produces an ex-ante deadweight loss. Anticipating the shareholders’ incentives, creditors demand a higher coupon to compensate for the expected loss; anticipating the higher coupon, shareholders may forgo value-creating debt issuances.
The contractual response is to condition the debt on observable measures that proxy for the underlying behaviors. Asset substitution can be partially controlled by covenants restricting investment in risky assets and by negative-pledge covenants (i.e., covenants that prevent the firm from collateralizing other debt with the firm’s existing assets). Underinvestment can be partially controlled by covenants permitting accelerated repayment if the firm’s measured capacity to invest is impaired. Claim dilution can be partially controlled by covenants restricting additional debt issuance, dividend payments, and stock repurchases.
Accounting numbers enter the contracting framework because they are the most readily available and most credibly verifiable summary of the firm’s economic position. A creditor cannot easily observe the firm’s investment opportunities, the riskiness of its individual projects, or the manager’s private information. The creditor can observe — at low cost, with reasonable verification, and at standardized intervals — the firm’s reported earnings, leverage, liquidity, and operating cash flow. The covenant structure is the institutional response to the limited verifiability of the underlying economic conditions: the contract conditions on what is observable and verifiable, and the verifiable proxies are accounting numbers.






Why this matters for accounting



The bondholder-shareholder framework gives accounting a role distinct from its capital-markets role. Capital markets use accounting numbers to value the firm’s residual claim (Arc II’s main theme so far). Debt contracts use accounting numbers to determine when contractual rights have been triggered. The two uses make different demands on the accounting numbers. Capital markets value information that is forward-looking and economically meaningful; debt contracts value information that is conservative, verifiable, and timely. The two demands sometimes pull in the same direction and sometimes in opposite directions, and FASB standard-setting decisions navigate the tension on a standard-by-standard basis.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.3 The Smith and Warner typology
Smith and Warner (1979) classified the covenants found in U.S. bond indentures into four broad categories, organized by the type of behavior the covenant restricts. The classification has proven durable.
Production-investment covenants restrict the firm’s investment activity. Examples include (i) limits on capital expenditures, (ii) restrictions on asset sales, (iii) prohibitions on investments in unrelated businesses, and (iv) prohibitions on mergers and acquisitions absent creditor consent. These covenants address the asset-substitution and underinvestment problems by giving the creditor a direct say over the firm’s investment portfolio.
Dividend covenants restrict the firm’s distributions to shareholders. Examples include restrictions on dividend payments above a stated cumulative ceiling, restrictions on stock repurchases, and “payment-in-kind” provisions that limit the firm’s ability to redeem subordinated securities. These covenants address the claim-dilution problem by preventing the firm from withdrawing equity capital that protects the creditors.
Financing covenants restrict the firm’s capital-structure choices. Examples include limits on additional debt issuance, restrictions on liens that would subordinate the existing debt, and prohibitions on sale-leaseback transactions that would synthetically increase leverage. These covenants address the claim-dilution problem more comprehensively.
Bonding covenants require the firm to provide specific information or to take specific actions that signal the absence of opportunism. Examples include requirements to provide audited financial statements at stated intervals, requirements to maintain insurance coverage, and requirements to maintain particular financial ratios.
The fourth category — bonding covenants — is the one that most directly conditions on accounting numbers. The financial-ratio covenants in this category impose constraints such as (i) a minimum interest-coverage ratio, (ii) a maximum debt-to-EBITDA ratio, (iii) a minimum current ratio, (iv) a maximum leverage ratio, or (v) a minimum tangible net worth. Each ratio is computed from the firm’s reported financial statements at the relevant measurement date. A covenant violation occurs when the ratio crosses the contractual threshold; the violation gives the creditor the right to accelerate the loan, to demand additional collateral, to renegotiate terms, or — in the most severe cases — to force the firm into bankruptcy.
Bonding covenants in modern debt agreements are extensive. The typical leveraged loan in the U.S. private credit market contains a dozen or more financial-ratio covenants; the typical investment-grade bond contains a smaller but still substantive set. The number, structure, and tightness of covenants vary systematically with the borrower’s credit quality and with the lender’s information environment.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.4 Conservatism as a contracting solution
Watts (2003) developed the argument that the conservative biases observable in U.S. GAAP are a contracting solution to the bondholder-shareholder conflict. The argument runs as follows. A creditor with a fixed claim cares more about downside risk than about upside potential — the creditor receives at most the promised payment and may receive less, but cannot receive more. Accounting that systematically biases toward conservative measurement — recognizing losses when they become probable but recognizing gains only when they become realized, carrying inventory at the lower of cost or market, requiring more verifiable evidence for income recognition than for loss recognition — produces accounting numbers that overstate the downside scenarios the creditor cares about while understating the upside scenarios that primarily benefit the shareholders.
Conservatism, on this view, is asymmetric (i.e., more demanding of evidence for good news than for bad news). The asymmetry is contracting-functional in two distinct ways. First, conservative accounting produces numbers that tilt toward triggering the covenant earlier than they would under symmetric accounting, giving the creditor protection against the shareholder’s incentive to delay loss recognition. Second, conservative accounting reduces the manager’s discretion over the timing of loss recognition. A manager whose compensation depends on reported earnings has an incentive to delay losses; conservative standards reduce the scope for that delay by requiring the manager to recognize losses on triggered events rather than at discretionary times.
The Watts argument has both empirical and conceptual implications. Empirically, it predicts that firms with more debt in their capital structure, with more covenants in their debt contracts, or with more concentrated creditors should have more conservative accounting; the prediction has been broadly supported. Conceptually, it suggests that the FASB’s reluctance to permit upward revaluations of long-lived assets, the lower-of-cost-or-market rule for inventory, the impairment-only goodwill model under ASC 350, and the asymmetric treatment of contingent gains and losses are not arbitrary holdovers from a less sophisticated era of accounting but are functional responses to the contracting environment.
The argument is contested. The Conceptual Framework (Chapter 5) treats neutrality as an aspect of faithful representation and is wary of systematic biases in either direction. Critics argue that the contracting benefits of conservative accounting are smaller than its costs in distorting investor perceptions, particularly for firms with substantial public equity. Recent FASB decisions — the move from incurred-loss to expected-loss credit accounting under CECL, for example — have, on the margin, shifted some areas of GAAP toward more symmetric measurement. The Watts argument is best understood as a description of one role accounting plays, not a normative endorsement of conservatism in all settings.






A subtle implication



Conservatism is not synonymous with prudence in some general moral sense. Watts’s notion of conservatism is specifically asymmetric — it requires more evidence for good news than for bad news. A standard that requires equally rigorous evidence for both gains and losses is neutral, not conservative. The distinction matters in standard-setting deliberations because debates over neutrality versus conservatism are often confused with debates over rigor versus laxity.

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
section: 9.5 Mechanics: bonds, the effective-interest method, and a worked example
text:
9.5 Mechanics: bonds, the effective-interest method, and a worked example
Bond accounting under U.S. GAAP follows the amortized cost model (i.e., a model in which the carrying amount is updated each period by accruing interest at the historical market rate, rather than by remeasuring to current market value) for most non-trading bond obligations. The structure sits at the heart of the financial reporting of corporate debt.
When a firm issues a bond, the bond promises a stream of fixed cash payments — a periodic coupon and a principal repayment at maturity. The cash the issuer receives is determined by the market interest rate at issuance (i.e., the yield demanded by investors for bonds of comparable risk and duration), not by the bond’s stated coupon rate. Three issuance cases arise:

At par. If the market rate equals the coupon rate, the bond issues at par, and the cash received equals the face value.
At a discount. If the market rate exceeds the coupon rate, the bond issues at a discount, and the cash received is less than the face value. The discount is prepaid interest that the issuer recognizes over the life of the bond.
At a premium. If the market rate is below the coupon rate, the bond issues at a premium, and the cash received exceeds the face value. The premium reduces interest expense recognized over the life of the bond.

The discount or premium is amortized over the life of the bond using the effective-interest method. In any period, (i) interest expense equals the carrying amount at the start of the period multiplied by the market rate at issuance, (ii) the cash coupon payment equals the face value multiplied by the coupon rate, and (iii) the difference between interest expense and the coupon payment adjusts the discount or premium account, moving the carrying amount toward face value. At maturity, the carrying amount equals the face value, and the firm pays the face value to retire the bond.

A worked example: $100,000 face, 10-year, 6% coupon, issued to yield 8%
Suppose Acme Corp. issues, on January 1, a ten-year bond with a face value of $100{,}000 and a coupon rate of 6% paid annually. The market rate for bonds of comparable risk and duration at issuance is 8%. The cash the bond will fetch — the present value of the bond’s cash flows discounted at 8% — is the sum of (i) the present value of a ten-year annuity of $6{,}000 (i.e., 6% of $100{,}000) and (ii) the present value of the $100{,}000 face repayment at the end of year 10:
\[
\text{Issue price} \;=\; \$6{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08} \;+\; \frac{\$100{,}000}{(1.08)^{10}} \;\approx\; \$40{,}260 + \$46{,}319 \;=\; \$86{,}580.
\]
The bond issues at a discount of approximately $13{,}420 ($100{,}000 − $86{,}580). The journal entry at issuance is:

The discount on bonds payable is a contra-liability account (i.e., a balance-sheet account that reduces the gross face value of the bonds to the net carrying amount). The net liability presented on the balance sheet at issuance is $100{,}000 − $13{,}420 = $86{,}580.
The amortization schedule for the first three periods is:

Interest expense ($6{,}926) is the economically meaningful charge — it equals the carrying amount at the start of the period times the market rate at issuance. Cash interest paid ($6{,}000) is smaller because the coupon rate is below the market rate. The difference ($926) reduces the discount account; the carrying amount rises to $87{,}506 and the bond moves closer to its face value. The pattern repeats with the carrying amount compounding upward each period until, at the end of year 10, the discount has been fully amortized and the carrying amount equals the face value of $100{,}000.
At maturity, the firm retires the bond by paying the face value:

The amortized-cost model differs from a fair-value model in an important respect. If market interest rates change after issuance, the bond’s market price will change correspondingly — but the carrying amount on the issuer’s balance sheet does not move. The bond is reported at the historical issuance value, amortized toward face. The amortized-cost basis is an example of a measurement choice that prioritizes faithful representation (the carrying amount is verifiable from the original transaction) over relevance (the carrying amount may be a poor proxy for the bond’s current market value).






An instructive case: Apple’s 2013 debut



Apple held no public debt for nearly two decades before issuing $17 billion in bonds across six tranches on April 30, 2013 — at the time the largest non-bank corporate bond offering in history. The proceeds funded a share-repurchase and dividend program rather than operating investment, an example of how leverage decisions interact with shareholder distributions. The accounting was standard: each tranche was recorded at its issue price (with small discounts and premiums relative to par), and interest expense has been recognized at the effective yield in each period since. The case is a reminder that bond accounting mechanics apply equally to a firm with no operating need to borrow and to a firm in deep distress; the mechanics are silent on why the firm chose to issue.

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.6 Mechanics: leases under ASC 842
The most consequential single change in U.S. liabilities accounting in the past decade was the FASB’s adoption of ASC 842, effective for U.S. public companies in 2019. The standard replaced a framework under which operating leases were essentially off-balance-sheet — the firm reported lease expense as a periodic operating cost but did not record any asset or liability associated with the lease — with a framework requiring all leases of more than twelve months to appear on the balance sheet.
Under ASC 842, the lessee recognizes, at lease inception, (i) a right-of-use (ROU) asset (i.e., the lessee’s contractual right to use the leased asset over the lease term) and (ii) a corresponding lease liability (i.e., the lessee’s obligation to make the lease payments). Both are measured at the present value of the unavoidable lease payments, discounted at the lessee’s incremental borrowing rate (i.e., the rate the lessee would pay to borrow on a collateralized basis over a similar term) or the rate implicit in the lease, if determinable.
ASC 842 distinguishes two categories of leases for income-statement purposes. Finance leases are those that transfer substantially all of the economic benefits and burdens of ownership to the lessee — for example, (i) leases that transfer title at the end of the term, (ii) leases with bargain purchase options, (iii) leases of substantially all of the asset’s useful life, and (iv) leases whose present value of payments equals substantially all of the asset’s fair value. Finance leases produce a depreciation expense on the ROU asset and a separately reported interest expense on the lease liability, just as if the lessee had purchased the asset and financed it with debt. Operating leases are all other leases. They produce a single straight-line lease expense over the lease term, preserving the income-statement pattern of the pre-2019 framework while introducing the balance-sheet recognition the prior framework lacked.

Why ASC 842 was a big deal
The pre-2019 framework was widely understood — by sophisticated analysts and creditors — as an accounting fiction. A firm that leased a building had the same economic obligations as a firm that financed the building with debt and recorded depreciation, but only the latter showed up on the balance sheet. Analysts had for decades constructed adjusted leverage ratios that capitalized the discounted stream of footnoted operating-lease payments, but less sophisticated readers of financial statements — including some creditors writing covenants — relied on the unadjusted GAAP numbers.
The 2019 transition moved approximately $2 trillion of operating-lease right-of-use assets and corresponding liabilities onto U.S. corporate balance sheets — the largest single change in U.S. financial reporting in a generation. The change was concentrated in industries with heavy reliance on operating leases: retail (store leases), airlines (aircraft and gate leases), restaurants (location leases), and shipping (vessel and container leases). It reduced the cross-firm variation in apparent leverage that had been driven by the choice between leasing and buying, and it produced an empirical natural experiment in the renegotiation of covenants that had been written before the change.


A concrete example: Boeing in the 737 MAX era
Boeing’s pre-2019 footnote disclosures showed substantial operating-lease commitments associated with its leased buildings, its aircraft-test infrastructure, and its supplier-financing arrangements. The 2019 adoption of ASC 842, coinciding with the 737 MAX grounding, brought these obligations onto the balance sheet as ROU assets and lease liabilities at the same time that Boeing’s underlying credit was deteriorating. The two changes are conceptually distinct — one is an accounting representation change, the other is a real-economic change — but both showed up in the same financial statements, and the resulting compounded effect on reported leverage produced a meaningful change in how creditors and rating agencies viewed the firm. The Boeing case is an instructive reminder that an accounting representation change, although not changing the underlying economics, can interact with concurrent real shocks in ways that affect contractual outcomes.






Common pitfall



The lease accounting change did not change the underlying economics of leasing. A firm that leased a building before 2019 had the same economic obligations as a firm that leased the building after 2019. What changed was the accounting representation: the off-balance-sheet treatment under the prior framework was, on most analyses, an accounting fiction that the change merely corrected. Sophisticated investors and creditors had for decades adjusted for off-balance-sheet leases through footnote analysis. The institutional value of ASC 842 is that the adjustment is no longer required.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
section: 9.7 Mechanics: pensions, OPEB, and off-balance-sheet history
text:
9.7 Mechanics: pensions, OPEB, and off-balance-sheet history

Defined-benefit versus defined-contribution plans
Employer-sponsored retirement plans fall into two broad categories that differ sharply in who bears the residual risk. A defined-contribution plan (e.g., a 401(k)) specifies the employer’s periodic contribution; the employee bears the investment risk on the accumulated balance, and the employer’s accounting is simple — the contribution is an expense in the period made. A defined-benefit plan (e.g., a traditional corporate pension) specifies the retirement benefit the employee will receive — typically a function of years of service and final salary — and the employer bears the risk that the assets set aside to fund the obligation may or may not be sufficient when the benefits come due. Other post-employment benefits (OPEB), most notably retiree health care, are accounted for similarly to defined-benefit pensions.
The accounting for defined-benefit plans is governed by ASC 715. The economic obligation — the projected benefit obligation (i.e., the present value of benefits earned to date, with future salary increases factored in) — is computed by an actuary using assumptions about (i) the discount rate, (ii) the expected long-term return on plan assets, (iii) future salary growth, (iv) employee turnover, and (v) mortality. The plan’s funded status is the difference between the fair value of plan assets and the projected benefit obligation. The funded status is reported on the balance sheet, and the period’s pension expense is reported on the income statement.
The role of actuarial assumptions is consequential. A higher discount rate produces a lower obligation; a higher assumed return on plan assets produces a lower pension expense. Both assumptions are at the firm’s discretion, subject to broad professional-judgment standards and auditor review. The empirical literature has documented that firms appear to choose actuarial assumptions in ways correlated with the firm’s incentives — for example, raising the assumed return on plan assets in periods when pension expense pressure is binding (e.g., periods of weak earnings or near covenant thresholds).


The gap between funded status and pension expense
A distinctive feature of defined-benefit pension accounting is that the funded status reported on the balance sheet and the pension expense reported on the income statement are not tightly linked. The funded status reflects the current present-value gap between obligations and assets. The pension expense, by contrast, smooths several components — service cost, interest cost, expected return on plan assets, and amortization of prior gains and losses — across periods. Actuarial gains and losses (i.e., changes in the funded status from actual-versus-expected investment performance or assumption changes) are not flowed through the income statement in the period of change; they are deferred in accumulated other comprehensive income (AOCI) and amortized into pension expense over time using the “corridor” mechanism.
The result is that a firm can carry a substantial underfunded pension liability on the balance sheet while reporting modest pension expense on the income statement. The smoothing was a deliberate FASB choice — actuarial estimates are noisy, and quarter-to-quarter remeasurement was viewed as producing more variation than information — but the smoothing produces a divergence between the cash-flow reality of the pension obligation and the period-by-period reported expense.


The legacy-pension overhang: GM and Ford
The U.S. auto industry has long carried defined-benefit pension and OPEB obligations of a magnitude that meaningfully affects firm value. General Motors and Ford each carried obligations measured in the tens of billions of dollars during the 2000s and 2010s, with funded-status gaps that varied with discount rates and asset performance. The 2009 GM bankruptcy was driven in substantial part by the unsustainability of legacy retiree benefits relative to the firm’s operating cash flows. The case is consistent with the broader point that off-balance-sheet or partially-smoothed obligations can mask leverage that becomes binding when business conditions deteriorate — a pattern with the same structural shape as the ASC 842 lease case, although the institutional response (smoothing versus capitalization) has been different.


Special-purpose entities and the Enron episode
A historical thread that ties together the chapter’s treatment of off-balance-sheet items is the history of special-purpose entities (SPEs) and variable-interest entities (VIEs). Before the 2003 issuance of FIN 46 (later codified as ASC 810), firms could sponsor SPEs — separately incorporated entities holding assets and liabilities — without consolidating the SPE’s balance sheet into the sponsor’s, provided that a small minority equity interest (3% under the prior guidance) was held by an outside party. Enron exploited the structure aggressively, using SPEs to hold assets at high carrying values and to hold liabilities off the sponsor’s balance sheet. The 2001 Enron collapse revealed that the consolidation threshold had become a mechanism for evasion rather than a substantive test of control.
ASC 810 (i.e., the consolidation guidance issued in 2003 and revised subsequently) shifted the consolidation test from a quantitative equity threshold to a qualitative analysis of who bears the entity’s risks and rewards. Under the revised guidance, the primary beneficiary — the party with the power to direct the entity’s most significant activities and the obligation to absorb losses or right to receive benefits — consolidates the entity. The Enron-era SPE structures would not survive the ASC 810 test, and the broader pattern of using off-balance-sheet vehicles to disguise leverage has been substantially curtailed. The post-2003 environment is not friction-free — securitization structures and certain operating-lease and joint-venture arrangements remain analytically demanding — but the consolidation perimeter is now substantially broader than it was before the Enron episode.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.8 Mechanics: contingent liabilities
A contingent liability (i.e., a potential obligation that depends on the outcome of a future event) is accounted for under ASC 450 according to (i) the probability that the obligation will be confirmed and (ii) the firm’s ability to estimate the amount. The three-tier framework is:

Probable and reasonably estimable. The firm recognizes a liability on the balance sheet and an expense on the income statement.
Reasonably possible but not probable. The firm does not recognize a liability but discloses the contingency in the footnotes, including the nature of the contingency and an estimate of the possible loss (or a statement that no estimate can be made).
Remote. The firm need not disclose the contingency at all.

The framework is asymmetric in the same direction as the rest of conservatism: a contingent gain (e.g., a probable favorable outcome in pending litigation in which the firm is the plaintiff) is not recognized until the gain is realized, while a contingent loss is recognized once it is probable. The asymmetry is consistent with the Watts argument of Section 9.4.
The probability thresholds are stated qualitatively, and the firm’s classification of a given contingency requires substantial judgment. Litigation contingencies, product warranties, environmental remediation obligations, and tax disputes are common settings in which the classification has been contested. The empirical literature on contingency disclosure has documented (i) that firms with binding accounting concerns tend to disclose less specifically than firms without such concerns, (ii) that the realized losses on disclosed contingencies are systematically larger than the disclosed estimates, and (iii) that the asymmetric treatment of gains and losses produces footnote disclosures that are useful but incomplete summaries of the firm’s exposure.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.9 Empirical evidence
The empirical literature on liabilities and contracting is large; Beyer et al. (2010) review the financial-reporting environment broadly, and Dechow, Ge, and Schrand (2010) review earnings quality. Three findings are particularly relevant.
First, the contracting role of accounting has been documented through natural-experiment designs. Roberts and Sufi (2009), in a paper titled “Control Rights and Capital Structure,” document that covenant violations produce sharp reductions in subsequent investment by violating firms — investment falls by approximately 13% on average in the four quarters following a violation. The reduction is concentrated in firms with greater contracting flexibility (i.e., firms with single-lender relationships, where the lender can implement the covenant remedy directly) and is largest when the violation is the firm’s first. The result is consistent with the framework of Section 9.2: covenants are not merely passive performance metrics but active mechanisms through which creditors influence the firm’s real decisions. The Roberts-Sufi result has been replicated and extended, and it forms the empirical foundation of this week’s lab.
Second, the empirical literature on conservatism has documented that conservative accounting is more prevalent in firms with more debt in their capital structure and with more concentrated creditors. The pattern is consistent with Watts (2003), who argues that conservatism has a contracting function. The literature has also documented that conservatism has, on the margin, declined over time as the FASB has moved toward more neutral standards and as firms have become more equity-financed; the relative importance of the contracting role versus the capital-markets role has shifted.
Third, the empirical literature on the ASC 842 lease accounting change is still developing, but early evidence suggests that the change has produced statistically significant changes in firms’ apparent leverage as measured by traditional ratios and corresponding changes in covenant slack. Renegotiations of debt covenants in response to the change have been substantial, suggesting that — even though sophisticated creditors had presumably already adjusted for off-balance-sheet leases — the formal change in measurement basis carried contractual weight.
The literature does not claim that all aspects of GAAP liability accounting are well-designed. Specific concerns — around the measurement of complex derivatives, the recognition of contingent obligations, and the treatment of pension and OPEB obligations — remain contested. The general claim that emerges is that accounting numbers play an active role in the contracting environment of the modern firm, and that the design of the accounting standards has consequences for that environment.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.10 Key takeaways






Key takeaways




Debt contracts condition on accounting numbers because direct economic conditions are not verifiable. Reported earnings, leverage, and cash-flow ratios are the most credibly verifiable summaries of the firm’s position, and covenants tie creditor rights to thresholds on those numbers.
The Smith-Warner typology organizes covenants by what they restrict. Production-investment, dividend, financing, and bonding covenants each address a specific component of the bondholder-shareholder conflict.
Conservatism is asymmetric, not merely prudent. Asymmetric measurement is functional in a creditor-protective regime by triggering covenants earlier and reducing the manager’s discretion over the timing of loss recognition.
The effective-interest method ties accounting interest expense to the market rate at issuance. Interest expense equals the carrying amount times the historical yield; the discount or premium is amortized so that the carrying amount converges to face value at maturity.
ASC 842 moved approximately $2 trillion of operating leases onto U.S. balance sheets without changing the underlying economics. The change replaced an accounting fiction with substantive recognition, with the largest effects concentrated in lease-intensive industries.
Pension and OPEB accounting smooths actuarial volatility through the corridor mechanism, producing a divergence between the funded status on the balance sheet and the pension expense on the income statement. The smoothing was a deliberate FASB choice but creates a gap between cash-flow reality and reported expense that is consequential for legacy-pension-heavy firms.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.11 Discussion questions

The chapter argued that conservative accounting can be understood as a contracting solution to the bondholder-shareholder conflict. The Conceptual Framework (Chapter 5) treats neutrality as an aspect of faithful representation. Are these positions in tension? If so, how should the FASB resolve the tension when designing a specific standard?
The Smith and Warner typology classifies covenants by the type of behavior they restrict. Identify a covenant from each of the four categories that you would expect to see in a leveraged loan agreement, and explain briefly which of the bondholder-shareholder conflicts the covenant addresses.
The 2019 lease accounting change moved approximately $2 trillion of operating-lease right-of-use assets and corresponding liabilities onto U.S. corporate balance sheets. Did the change produce more useful financial statements? Defend your answer using the relevance-versus-faithful-representation framework of Chapter 5.
Roberts and Sufi (2009) document that covenant violations produce sharp reductions in subsequent investment. Is this a case where accounting numbers are causing real corporate behavior, or a case where the accounting numbers are correlated with underlying economic conditions that themselves cause the behavior? How might one distinguish the two interpretations empirically?
Suppose a creditor and a debtor are negotiating a new loan agreement. The creditor proposes a covenant requiring the debtor to maintain a minimum interest-coverage ratio of 3.0×. The debtor counters with a proposal to instead provide weekly cash-flow reports to the creditor. From the creditor’s perspective, which proposal is preferable? Use the framework of this chapter to defend your answer.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.12 Problems

Mechanical

(Bond issued at a discount; effective-interest amortization.) Acme Corp. issues a four-year bond with a face value of $200{,}000 and a coupon rate of 5% paid semi-annually. The market interest rate at issuance is 6%. (a) Compute the cash received at issuance. (b) Construct an amortization schedule showing the carrying amount, interest expense, coupon payment, and discount amortization for each of the eight semi-annual periods. (c) Verify that the carrying amount at the end of period 8 equals the face value. (d) Record the journal entry at issuance, the journal entry at the end of period 1, and the journal entry at the retirement of the bond.
(Bond issued at a premium; effective-interest amortization.) Repeat Problem 1 under the assumption that the market interest rate at issuance is 4% (i.e., the bond issues at a premium). Show how the premium account behaves over the life of the bond, and contrast the income-statement pattern with that of Problem 1.
(ASC 842 lease, operating classification.) On January 1, Acme Corp. enters into a five-year lease of office space. The lease requires annual payments of $24{,}000 at the end of each year. Acme’s incremental borrowing rate is 7%. The lease is classified as an operating lease under ASC 842. (a) Compute the lease liability and right-of-use asset at lease inception.
