(b) Construct the journal entries for the first two years of the lease, separately reporting the components of the single straight-line lease expense (i.e., interest accretion on the liability and amortization of the ROU asset). (c) Compute the carrying amount of the ROU asset and the lease liability at the end of year 2.
(Pension expense decomposition.) Acme Corp.’s defined-benefit pension plan reports the following at the start of the year: projected benefit obligation $400{,}000; fair value of plan assets $360{,}000; discount rate 5%; expected long-term return on plan assets 7%; current-year service cost $30{,}000; benefits paid during the year $20{,}000; actual return on plan assets for the year $36{,}000. Assume no amortization of prior gains or losses. (a) Compute the components of pension expense (i.e., service cost, interest cost on the obligation, expected return on plan assets, and any amortization). (b) Compute the projected benefit obligation and fair value of plan assets at year-end. (c) Report the funded status at year-end and identify whether the plan is over- or under-funded.



Conceptual

(On-balance-sheet versus off-balance-sheet judgment.) A firm enters into a fifteen-year agreement to purchase 80% of the output of a specialized manufacturing facility being constructed by a counterparty. The agreement is structured so that the firm does not own the facility, does not bear residual value risk on the facility, and is not obligated to make payments if the counterparty fails to deliver. Should the agreement give rise to a recognized liability on the firm’s balance sheet? Apply the consolidation framework of ASC 810 and the lease framework of ASC 842, identify the relevant judgment calls, and defend your conclusion.
(Evaluation of contingent-liability disclosure.) Choose a publicly traded firm with substantial pending litigation disclosed in its most recent 10-K (large pharmaceutical firms and large industrial firms are usually good candidates). In approximately 400 words, evaluate the firm’s contingency disclosure. Address (i) the classification of the contingencies under ASC 450 (probable, reasonably possible, remote), (ii) whether the firm provides a numerical estimate of possible loss, (iii) what the disclosure conveys about the firm’s exposure relative to its market capitalization, and (iv) whether the disclosure is, in your view, useful to a reader trying to assess the firm’s economic position.



Writing exercise

(Short writing exercise on conservatism and standard-setting trade-offs.) In approximately 500 words, evaluate the following claim: “The FASB’s recent move toward expected-loss credit accounting under CECL is a retreat from conservatism that will, on net, produce less useful financial statements for creditors.” Engage the Watts (2003) contracting argument, the Conceptual Framework’s treatment of neutrality, and the empirical literature you have read. State your position and defend it; do not pretend the question has an obvious answer.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.13 Lab
The out-of-class lab for this week is WRDS Lab 4: Covenant Slack and Investment. The lab walks through the construction of standardized covenant-slack measures from Compustat for a sample of leveraged firms and the replication of the Roberts and Sufi (2009) finding that covenant violations predict reductions in subsequent investment. The lab is the most empirically intensive of the WRDS labs in this course and builds directly on the methodologies of Labs 1–3.
The deliverable is a Colab notebook that produces (i) the cross-sectional distribution of covenant slack in your sample, (ii) a regression of subsequent investment on covenant slack and standard controls, and (iii) a brief written discussion of what the replication implies about the contracting role of accounting. The lab is due at the end of the week.
The GenAI policy permits the use of AI coding assistants with disclosure. As with prior labs, the disclosure must specify what the tool was used for and whether any AI-generated text appears in the final submission.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism
text:
9.14 Further reading
The four readings below are the assigned readings for Week 9. Students should read all of them.

Smith and Warner (1979), “On Financial Contracting.” The foundational paper, which articulates the bondholder-shareholder conflict and the four-category typology of covenants. Read for the conflict, the typology, and the contracting argument.
Watts (2003), “Conservatism in Accounting Part I: Explanations and Implications.” Read for the contracting interpretation of conservatism and the four explanations the author offers. The companion paper “Part II” is optional.
Roberts and Sufi (2009), “Control Rights and Capital Structure.” The empirical paper that documents that covenant violations sharply reduce subsequent investment. Read for the empirical design and the headline result; the technical appendix on covenant slack measures is useful for the lab.
Optional, for those who want more: The text of a real credit agreement from EDGAR. The agreements are dense and unfamiliar at first, but working through the financial-covenant section of one — for any leveraged firm whose 10-K incorporates its credit facility by reference — is one of the more useful exercises a student can do for understanding what bonding covenants actually look like in practice.







Where Arc II goes from here



Chapter 9 has examined the liability side of the balance sheet and the contracting role of accounting numbers in debt agreements. Chapter 10 closes Arc II by turning to the equity side: stockholders’ equity, basic and diluted earnings per share, stock-based compensation, and the empirical literature on earnings management. The Chapter 4 framework on moral hazard returns explicitly: managers respond to the incentives created by their compensation contracts, and the accruals discussed in Chapter 7 are the workhorse mechanism through which the response is implemented.








Beyer, Anne, Daniel A. Cohen, Thomas Z. Lys, and Beverly R. Walther. 2010. “The Financial Reporting Environment: Review of the Recent Literature.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 50 (2–3): 296–343.


Dechow, Patricia, Weili Ge, and Catherine Schrand. 2010. “Understanding Earnings Quality: A Review of the Proxies, Their Determinants and Their Consequences.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 50 (2–3): 344–401.


Roberts, Michael R., and Amir Sufi. 2009. “Control Rights and Capital Structure: An Empirical Investigation.” Journal of Finance 64 (4): 1657–95.


Smith, Clifford W., and Jerold B. Warner. 1979. “On Financial Contracting: An Analysis of Bond Covenants.” Journal of Financial Economics 7 (2): 117–61.


Watts, Ross L. 2003. “Conservatism in Accounting Part i: Explanations and Implications.” Accounting Horizons 17 (3): 207–21.
crumbs: 9  Liabilities, Debt Covenants, and Conservatism

title: 10  Equity Compensation
text:
10.1 Opening question
In 2005, just before the FASB’s new stock-option expensing rule took effect, the median S&P 500 CEO held option grants whose Black-Scholes value was approximately $5 million per year. The same firms reported the cost of those grants — under the pre-2005 framework — almost entirely in a footnote, with no charge to net income. The largest single line item in many executives’ annual pay packages produced, on the income statement, no expense at all. Earnings under the prior framework treated stock options as economically free.
The accounting was contested for two decades. Standard-setters, academic researchers, and a long line of SEC chairs argued that options had real economic value at grant and that the value should be recognized as compensation expense over the period the employee earned the award. A coalition of corporate issuers — concentrated in the technology sector, where option-heavy pay packages were standard — argued that the value at grant was speculative, that volatility-based valuation was unreliable, and that recognizing the speculation as expense would understate reported earnings of innovative firms. The fight produced a Congressional override of an earlier FASB proposal in 1994, a deferral of mandatory expensing for more than a decade, and a final standard — Statement of Financial Accounting Standards 123R (now codified at ASC 718) — that took effect in 2005. The standard required firms to recognize stock-based compensation at grant-date fair value, ratably over the vesting period, as an expense in the income statement.
The episode is a useful entry point into this chapter for two reasons. First, it shows that accounting standards for compensation are not technical adjustments at the margin of the income statement. They determine how much of executive pay enters the reported earnings number, and they shape the incentives the contracts create. Second, it foregrounds the connection between compensation accounting and the broader stewardship framework introduced in Chapter 4. The Holmstrom informativeness principle (Holmstrom 1979) argued that any signal informative about managerial effort should enter the contract; the design of equity compensation determines which signals enter and with what weight. The accounting for equity compensation determines, in turn, how those contracts are reported to investors.
This chapter closes Arc II by examining equity compensation as a contracting and measurement problem. The chapter develops the historical fight over options expensing (i.e., the standard-setting episode that produced FAS 123R), the mechanics of grant-date fair value for options, restricted stock units, and performance shares, and the empirical literature on whether equity-pay-sensitive executives are more likely to misreport. The literature on misreporting incentives (Bergstresser and Philippon 2006; Burns and Kedia 2006; Erickson, Hanlon, and Maydew 2006) connects directly to the moral hazard framework of Chapter 4 and to the earnings-management mechanisms that the Companion will revisit when Arc III turns to internal information systems.

title: 10  Equity Compensation
section: 10.2 A short history of stock-option accounting
text:
10.2 A short history of stock-option accounting
The accounting treatment of stock options in the United States passed through three regimes between 1972 and 2005. The trajectory is itself a useful illustration of how standard-setting interacts with industry incentives and Congressional politics.
The first regime, governing from 1972 to 1995, was set by Accounting Principles Board Opinion No. 25 (APB 25). APB 25 measured compensation expense as the option’s intrinsic value at grant date — the excess of the stock price over the strike price. Almost all options were granted at the money (i.e., with a strike price equal to the grant-date stock price), which produced an intrinsic value of zero and therefore an accounting expense of zero. A firm could grant an option worth several million dollars on Black-Scholes terms and record no compensation expense for the grant. The treatment was widely understood, by the 1980s, as inconsistent with the economic reality of option grants. Stock options have positive value at grant because of their time value — the probability that the stock price will rise above the strike before expiration — and APB 25’s intrinsic-value rule recognized only one component of that value.
The second regime began with Statement of Financial Accounting Standards 123 (SFAS 123), issued in 1995. The FASB’s original 1993 proposal would have required firms to recognize compensation expense at grant-date fair value, measured using an option-pricing model. The proposal was met with substantial opposition from the technology sector and from a bipartisan coalition in Congress, which argued that fair-value expensing would understate the reported earnings of high-growth firms and could damage the competitiveness of the U.S. innovation economy. The Senate passed a non-binding resolution (the Lieberman-Bumpers amendment of 1994) urging the FASB to abandon the proposal. The FASB, in response, issued SFAS 123 as a compromise: firms were permitted but not required to recognize options at fair value. Almost all firms chose to retain APB 25’s intrinsic-value rule for income-statement recognition and instead provided fair-value disclosure in a footnote. By 2002, only a small minority of S&P 500 firms had voluntarily adopted fair-value recognition.
The third regime arrived with SFAS 123R (now ASC 718), issued in December 2004 and effective for most U.S. public firms in fiscal 2006. SFAS 123R mandated recognition of stock-based compensation at grant-date fair value over the vesting period. The standard was issued against a substantially changed political backdrop. The Enron and WorldCom accounting scandals of 2001 and 2002 had eroded the technology sector’s political position, and the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 had positioned Congress on the side of stronger financial reporting. The International Accounting Standards Board had adopted a fair-value framework in IFRS 2 (2004), and pressure for convergence ran in the same direction. The Securities and Exchange Commission supported the change. The FASB issued SFAS 123R with limited Congressional resistance, and within two years, virtually all U.S. public firms were expensing stock-based compensation at fair value.






What kind of episode is this?



The FAS 123R fight is a useful case study in the political economy of standard-setting. The FASB is nominally independent of Congress, but Congress retains the ability to override the FASB through statute (it has done so once, in 1994, with the Lieberman-Bumpers resolution producing the SFAS 123 compromise). The episode suggests that standard-setting is not a purely technical exercise — the FASB chooses standards subject to a political constraint, and the constraint binds when affected industries can organize politically. Chapter 5’s discussion of the FASB’s institutional design speaks to the underlying tension.


The argument for the pre-2005 framework — that options are not “real” expenses — does not, on most economic readings, survive scrutiny. The firm that grants an option has transferred a contingent claim on its future cash flows; the recipient has received something of positive market value; the transfer is unambiguously a compensation event. The accounting question is how to measure the value of the transfer, not whether the transfer occurred. The contested issue was construct validity: whether the Black-Scholes model and its variants produce reliable estimates of the option’s value at grant, given that the options are non-tradable, contingent on continued employment, and subject to substantial early-exercise behavior. The FASB’s view, broadly shared in the academic literature (Core and Guay 2002), was that grant-date fair-value estimates were imperfect but sufficiently reliable for recognition.

title: 10  Equity Compensation
text:
10.3 Institutional response: ASC 718
The current U.S. GAAP framework for stock-based compensation is codified at ASC 718 (the codification of SFAS 123R, plus subsequent amendments). The framework has four essential elements.

Grant-date measurement at fair value. The fair value of the award is determined at the date the grant is communicated to the employee, using an option-pricing model for options and the grant-date stock price for restricted stock units. The grant-date measurement is locked in for accounting purposes — subsequent changes in the stock price do not alter the accounting expense for the original award.
Ratable recognition over the requisite service period. The grant-date fair value is recognized as compensation expense over the period during which the employee earns the award (i.e., the vesting period). For a four-year cliff-vested award, the expense is recognized in equal amounts over the four years. For a four-year graded-vesting award (e.g., 25% per year), the expense is loaded toward the early years because each tranche has its own service period.
Performance and market conditions. Awards with performance conditions (e.g., earnings or revenue targets) recognize expense only if achievement of the target is probable; if the target is missed, previously recognized expense is reversed. Awards with market conditions (e.g., a total shareholder return target) are measured at grant-date fair value that incorporates the probability of achieving the market condition; the expense is recognized regardless of whether the market condition is met. The asymmetry reflects the FASB’s view that market conditions can be priced into the grant-date fair value through a valuation model, while performance conditions cannot.
Forfeitures. Awards that the employee never vests in (e.g., because of termination) reduce the cumulative compensation expense. Firms may estimate expected forfeitures and adjust expense recognition prospectively, or recognize forfeitures as they occur.

The framework treats different categories of awards consistently in their accounting representation while distinguishing them by their economic characteristics. The next section develops the mechanics of the three principal categories.

title: 10  Equity Compensation
section: 10.4 Mechanics: options, RSUs, and performance shares
text:
10.4 Mechanics: options, RSUs, and performance shares

Stock options
A stock option grants the employee the right to buy a fixed number of shares at a fixed strike price during a specified term, conditional on remaining employed through the vesting date. The grant-date fair value of an option depends on six inputs:

Stock price at grant. The current market price of the underlying stock.
Strike price. The price at which the employee may purchase the shares. Most employee options are granted at the money (strike equal to grant-date stock price), so this input typically equals the stock price.
Time to expiration. The maximum term of the option, often ten years, though the model typically uses an expected term shorter than the contractual maximum because employees frequently exercise early.
Volatility. The expected standard deviation of the stock’s returns over the option’s life. The input is typically estimated from historical stock-price volatility, sometimes supplemented with implied volatility from listed options.
Risk-free rate. The yield on a Treasury security with a maturity matching the option’s expected term.
Expected dividend yield. The expected dividends the firm will pay over the option’s life. Dividends paid before exercise reduce the option’s value because the option holder does not receive them.

The Black-Scholes-Merton formula combines these inputs into a closed-form value. The Companion does not develop the formula in detail (the derivation is the subject of derivatives courses). What matters for the accounting is that the model produces a positive fair value at grant for any at-the-money option with positive volatility and a positive expected term, and that the value is increasing in volatility, in the expected term, in the risk-free rate, and (under most parameterizations) in the stock price. The value is decreasing in the strike price and in the expected dividend yield.
Consider a stylized example. Suppose Acme Corp. grants its CFO 5,000 options on January 1 with a strike of $50 (the grant-date stock price), a ten-year contractual term, an expected term of six years, expected annualized volatility of 30%, a risk-free rate of 4%, and an expected dividend yield of 2%. A Black-Scholes calculation produces a fair value per option of approximately $15.20. The total grant-date fair value of the award is $76,000. With four-year cliff vesting, Acme recognizes $19,000 of compensation expense per year for four years.
The journal entry at the end of year 1 is:



Account
Debit
Credit




Compensation expense
19,000



Additional paid-in capital

19,000



The entry is repeated in years 2, 3, and 4. At the end of year 4, cumulative APIC for the award is $76,000, and the options are fully vested. If the CFO exercises the options when the stock price has risen to $80, she pays the strike price of $250,000 (5,000 × $50) and receives 5,000 shares. The journal entry at exercise is:



Account
Debit
Credit




Cash
250,000



Additional paid-in capital
76,000



Common stock (par)

50


Additional paid-in capital

325,950



The previously recognized APIC of $76,000 is reclassified, and the new APIC reflects the issuance proceeds in excess of par. No additional compensation expense is recognized at exercise — the entire compensation cost was captured at the grant-date fair value, recognized over the vesting period.


Restricted stock units
A restricted stock unit (RSU) is a promise to deliver one share of stock at vesting, conditional on continued employment. The grant-date fair value of an RSU is the stock price at grant — there is no option to exercise, so the time value of an option does not apply. RSUs have, since the FAS 123R reform, displaced options as the dominant form of equity compensation at most large U.S. public firms; survey evidence from major proxy advisors suggests that RSUs now account for approximately 60% of the typical S&P 500 equity grant.
The mechanics are simpler than for options. Suppose Acme grants its CFO 1,000 RSUs on January 1 with a grant-date stock price of $50 and four-year ratable vesting. The grant-date fair value is $50,000. Acme recognizes $12,500 of compensation expense per year for four years. The journal entry at the end of year 1 is:



Account
Debit
Credit




Compensation expense
12,500



Additional paid-in capital

12,500



When the RSUs vest, the shares are issued. The journal entry at vesting (assuming all 1,000 vest) is:



Account
Debit
Credit




Additional paid-in capital
50,000



Common stock (par)

10


Additional paid-in capital

49,990



The par value of the issued shares is $10 (1,000 × $0.01), and the remainder is APIC. No cash changes hands at vesting (RSUs do not require an exercise price), so the entry reclassifies the previously recognized APIC into the issuance-related equity accounts.


Performance shares
A performance share delivers stock at vesting only if a specified performance condition is achieved. The condition is typically defined relative to (i) an internal accounting metric — for example, three-year cumulative earnings per share, return on invested capital, or revenue growth — or (ii) a market-based metric — most commonly relative total shareholder return (rTSR) against a peer group or an index.
The accounting depends on which type of condition applies. For an accounting-target performance share, the firm estimates the probability of achieving the target at each reporting date and recognizes expense based on the probability-weighted grant-date stock price. If the target is missed at the measurement date, previously recognized expense is reversed. For an rTSR performance share, the firm computes a grant-date fair value using a Monte Carlo simulation that incorporates the probability distribution of the relative TSR outcome; the expense is recognized over the service period regardless of the realized outcome.
Performance shares have become a substantial fraction of senior-executive equity grants over the past decade, in part because compensation consultants and proxy advisors have promoted them as more closely aligned with shareholder interests than time-vested RSUs. Whether the alignment claim holds up empirically is contested. Performance shares introduce a target-proximity incentive — the executive is more sensitive to actions that move the firm across the threshold than to actions that move the firm far from the threshold — that is itself a potential source of misreporting incentives.






A subtle implication



The accounting asymmetry between performance conditions and market conditions has unintended consequences for contract design. A firm that designs a relative-TSR performance share locks in its compensation expense at grant. A firm that designs an EPS-target performance share has a residual chance of reversing the expense (if the target is missed). The asymmetry creates a marginal accounting incentive to favor market-condition awards, even when the contract designer might prefer an accounting-target award on incentive grounds. The empirical literature on the rise of rTSR awards has not, to our knowledge, isolated the contribution of this accounting asymmetry.

title: 10  Equity Compensation
section: 10.5 Mechanics: tax treatment and diluted EPS
text:
10.5 Mechanics: tax treatment and diluted EPS
Two further mechanical issues warrant attention because they affect both reported financial statements and the cash cost of the awards.

Tax treatment
U.S. tax law distinguishes two categories of stock options. Incentive stock options (ISOs) qualify for preferential tax treatment under Section 422 of the Internal Revenue Code: the employee recognizes no taxable income at grant or exercise, and the gain on subsequent sale of the shares is taxed at capital-gains rates (provided certain holding-period requirements are met). The corresponding employer receives no deduction. Non-qualified stock options (NQSOs) produce ordinary-income treatment for the employee at exercise (taxed on the spread between the strike and the market price) and a corresponding deduction for the employer. ISOs are restricted by statute to grants of at most $100,000 of strike value vesting in any year and are subject to other technical limits; for senior executives at large firms, NQSOs are the dominant category.
The tax treatment creates a wedge between the GAAP expense and the cash compensation cost. A firm that grants $76,000 of NQSO fair value will recognize $76,000 of GAAP compensation expense over the vesting period and, separately, will receive a tax deduction at exercise equal to the spread between strike and market price (which may be substantially more or substantially less than $76,000, depending on stock-price realization). The difference produces an excess tax benefit or tax deficiency that, under current ASC 718 guidance, is recognized in the income statement at exercise. RSUs produce ordinary-income treatment to the employee at vesting and a corresponding employer deduction; performance shares are treated analogously upon achievement of the performance condition.


Diluted earnings per share
Equity-compensation awards enter the firm’s diluted EPS computation under ASC 260. The mechanics introduced in earlier chapters of the Companion apply: the treasury stock method is used for stock options and similar awards, and the if-converted method is used for convertible securities. For stock options, the treasury stock method assumes that the options are exercised at the start of the period, that the firm uses the proceeds (strike price times number of options) to buy back shares at the average market price during the period, and that the difference between the issued shares and the repurchased shares is the dilutive effect.
Consider an example. Suppose Acme has 1,000,000 weighted-average common shares outstanding, 100,000 stock options outstanding with a strike of $30, and an average stock price during the year of $50. The treasury stock method assumes the 100,000 options are exercised, producing $3,000,000 of cash proceeds (100,000 × $30). At the average market price of $50, Acme can repurchase 60,000 shares with the proceeds. The net dilutive effect is 100,000 − 60,000 = 40,000 shares. The diluted share count is 1,040,000.
Anti-dilutive securities — those whose inclusion would increase EPS rather than decrease it — are excluded from the diluted computation. Out-of-the-money options (strike above the average market price) are anti-dilutive and do not enter the diluted EPS. RSUs enter the diluted EPS at the full number of unvested units, since they have no exercise price and produce no offsetting cash proceeds for the treasury stock method.

title: 10  Equity Compensation
text:
10.6 The connection to stewardship
Chapter 4 introduced the principal-agent framework and the Holmstrom informativeness principle as the analytical foundation for thinking about compensation contracts. The equity-compensation mechanics developed above are a specific institutional instantiation of that framework, and a step back is useful before turning to the empirical literature.
The Holmstrom principle holds that any signal informative about the agent’s effort, conditional on the existing signals, should enter the contract. Equity compensation effectively makes the stock price a contract signal — the executive’s wealth becomes a function of the stock price through the equity holdings — and the structure of the equity award determines the slope of the wealth-price relationship. Options have a convex payoff in the stock price, which produces a non-linear sensitivity: the executive’s wealth rises sharply as the stock moves above the strike but is insensitive to declines below it. RSUs have a linear payoff (one share per unit), which produces a constant sensitivity. Performance shares introduce a step function at the performance threshold.
Each award structure produces a distinct incentive profile. Options reward upside risk-taking but provide limited downside discipline. RSUs reward shareholder-value creation in either direction (price increases or restraint from price-destroying actions). Performance shares reward proximity to the threshold and may, on standard incentive arguments, produce target-proximity manipulation similar to the bonus-plan kinks that Healy (1985) documented.
A second connection to Chapter 4 is the verifiability requirement. The Holmstrom principle requires that the contract condition on observable signals; for equity compensation, the relevant signal is the stock price, which is observable and verifiable in real time. For performance shares, the relevant signal is the achievement of the performance target, which is typically computed from the firm’s audited financial statements. The verification structure of the closing process and the external audit (Section 4.5) determines whether the performance target is reliably measured, which in turn determines whether the contract is incentive-compatible.
The connection runs in the other direction as well. The structure of the equity contract creates incentives for the manager to influence the verifiable signal — the reported earnings, the audited performance target, the stock price at grant. Section 10.7 turns to the empirical literature on whether equity-pay-sensitive executives in fact respond to the contract by exercising discretion over the signal.

title: 10  Equity Compensation
section: 10.7 Empirical evidence: equity pay and misreporting
text:
10.7 Empirical evidence: equity pay and misreporting
The empirical literature on the relation between equity-based compensation and financial misreporting is, as Bergstresser and Philippon characterize it, “a literature with a clear theoretical prediction and a contested empirical record.” The theoretical prediction is straightforward: an executive whose wealth is sensitive to the stock price has an incentive to take actions that increase the stock price, including misreporting actions (i.e., the manipulation of reported earnings through accruals or other discretionary choices) that can fool the market temporarily. The contested empirical question is whether the marginal effect of equity pay on misreporting is detectable in the cross-section of firms, conditional on the substantial confounding variation in firm characteristics and contract design.
Three foundational papers in the 2006 vintage of the literature established the empirical findings the field continues to engage.

Bergstresser and Philippon (2006) examined the cross-section of U.S. public firms and documented that firms with CEOs whose total compensation is more sensitive to share price — measured by the dollar change in CEO wealth per percentage-point change in stock price, scaled by the CEO’s annual salary plus bonus — exhibit higher levels of discretionary accruals as measured by the modified Jones model. The economic magnitudes the authors report are meaningful: a one-standard-deviation increase in equity-pay sensitivity is associated with approximately 0.5 standard deviations of additional discretionary accruals in their sample. The paper also documents that high-sensitivity firms are more likely to experience subsequent earnings restatements, suggesting that the discretionary-accrual pattern is not merely a noise-fitting artifact.
Burns and Kedia (2006) focused specifically on misreporting events — SEC-identified accounting restatements — and examined whether the structure of CEO compensation predicts restatement probability. The authors find that the sensitivity of CEO wealth to stock price is significantly associated with restatement probability, while the sensitivity to firm value (i.e., the linear stock-holdings component) is not. The asymmetry is consistent with options’ convex payoff: out-of-the-money options gain value disproportionately from positive stock-price surprises, and the executive holding such options has a larger expected payoff from a temporary stock-price boost than the executive holding linear claims of equivalent grant-date value.
Erickson, Hanlon, and Maydew (2006) examined a smaller but more targeted sample — firms whose financial statements were the subject of SEC enforcement actions for accounting fraud during 1996–2003. The authors compared the equity-incentive structure of fraud firms to a matched control sample and found that the wealth-performance sensitivity of fraud-firm CEOs is not statistically distinguishable from the wealth-performance sensitivity of control-firm CEOs. The result stands in tension with the Bergstresser-Philippon and Burns-Kedia findings, and the paper concludes that the link between equity incentives and accounting fraud — at least in the fraud-firm sample — is “not particularly strong.”

The three papers’ divergent conclusions reflect real differences in research design. Bergstresser and Philippon use a broad cross-section and a continuous measure of misreporting (discretionary accruals). Burns and Kedia use restatement events and emphasize the convexity of options as a separate channel. Erickson, Hanlon, and Maydew use a more extreme outcome (SEC fraud enforcement) and a matched-pair design that absorbs more cross-sectional variation. The three approaches together suggest that the relation between equity pay and misreporting may exist on average (consistent with Bergstresser-Philippon and Burns-Kedia) but may be hard to detect in the smaller, more selected sample of fraud-firm cases (consistent with Erickson-Hanlon-Maydew).
Two further strands of the literature are worth flagging.
The first strand concerns option grant timing. Aboody and Kasznik (2000) documented that CEOs receive disproportionately favorable stock-price movements around the time of option grants — stock prices tend to decline before scheduled grant dates and rise after, suggesting that managers may be timing voluntary disclosures to favor their option grants. Subsequent work by Heron and Lie (2007) examined the more aggressive practice of options backdating (i.e., the retrospective dating of option grants to a date with a lower stock price), which became the focus of an SEC enforcement wave in the mid-2000s. The Brocade Communications case (CEO Gregory Reyes was convicted of securities fraud in 2007) and the Apple matter (in which CFO Fred Anderson settled with the SEC over the backdating of options to former-CEO Steve Jobs) were among the high-profile enforcement actions. Heron and Lie estimate that approximately 30% of the abnormal stock-price pattern around option grants documented in earlier work is attributable to backdating rather than to legitimate grant-timing decisions.
The second strand concerns target-proximity incentives. The Healy bonus-plan literature (Healy 1985) established that managers respond to discontinuities in the compensation function — the lower threshold below which no bonus is paid, the upper cap above which additional earnings produce no additional bonus. Performance-share awards introduce analogous discontinuities at the performance-target threshold, and the empirical literature has documented that firms whose actual performance is close to a performance-share threshold exhibit patterns consistent with target-proximity manipulation. The mechanism connects to the broader earnings-management literature surveyed in Dechow, Ge, and Schrand (2010): managers exercise discretion over reported numbers in directions consistent with the discontinuities in their compensation function.






What kind of finding is this?



The literature on equity pay and misreporting is an example of a finding that is robust on average but contested in detail. The cross-sectional regression results in Bergstresser-Philippon and Burns-Kedia are consistent with the theoretical prediction, but the matched-sample design in Erickson-Hanlon-Maydew, applied to the most extreme cases of misreporting, fails to find a comparable pattern. Honors students should be careful in summarizing such literatures: the headline finding (“equity pay is associated with more misreporting”) is more secure than the specific magnitude or the specific mechanism, and the contested findings in the literature are themselves informative about the strength of the underlying effect.








Key takeaways




Equity compensation is a real economic cost. A grant of options or RSUs transfers a positive-value contingent claim on the firm’s future cash flows from existing shareholders to the recipient, and the FAS 123R reform of 2005 recognized this cost on the income statement at grant-date fair value, ratably over the vesting period.
The pre-2005 framework was politically contested rather than analytically defensible. APB 25’s intrinsic-value rule recognized zero expense for at-the-money options; the FASB’s 1993 fair-value proposal was overridden by a Congressional coalition and survived only as voluntary disclosure under SFAS 123 until the post-SOX standard-setting environment permitted SFAS 123R.
The mechanics differ across award types. Options are valued at grant-date Black-Scholes fair value with six inputs (i.e., stock price, strike, expected term, volatility, risk-free rate, dividend yield); RSUs are valued at the grant-date stock price; performance shares are valued by Monte Carlo simulation for market-condition awards and by probability-weighted intrinsic value for accounting-condition awards.
RSUs and performance shares now dominate options in S&P 500 equity grants. The shift reflects, in part, the post-2005 accounting reform (which eliminated the accounting advantage of options) and, in part, the spread of governance norms favoring share-based pay with explicit performance conditions.
Equity pay creates measurable misreporting incentives, on average. Bergstresser-Philippon and Burns-Kedia document that wealth-performance sensitivity is positively associated with discretionary accruals and with restatement probability; Erickson-Hanlon-Maydew, examining SEC fraud cases specifically, finds a more limited association, suggesting that the relation is robust on average but may be hard to detect in the smallest and most selected samples.
Connections to Arc III. The contracting framework developed in Chapter 4 and applied here to equity compensation will recur in Arc III’s treatment of performance measurement and capital budgeting. The same Holmstrom logic — condition on informative signals; balance risk and effort — underlies the internal-information systems that will occupy the remainder of the course.

title: 10  Equity Compensation
text:
10.8 Discussion questions

The FAS 123R fight was, in important respects, a dispute about whether stock options have an “economic” cost or only a “potential” cost. Take a position on the dispute and defend it. What does the position imply about the appropriate accounting treatment for other compensation forms whose value is contingent on uncertain future events?
Performance shares introduce a step function at the performance threshold, creating an incentive for executives to manage the firm’s reported performance toward the threshold. Suppose you are designing the compensation contract for a CEO of a large U.S. public firm. Would you (i) use performance shares with a smoothly increasing payout function, (ii) use performance shares with discrete payout tiers, or (iii) avoid performance shares in favor of time-vested RSUs? Defend your choice using the framework of Chapter 4.
The empirical literature on equity pay and misreporting has produced divergent findings — Bergstresser-Philippon and Burns-Kedia document positive associations, while Erickson-Hanlon-Maydew does not. What features of the research designs might explain the divergence, and which design produces evidence you find more compelling?
The Holmstrom informativeness principle (Chapter 4) holds that any signal informative about managerial effort should enter the compensation contract. The principle is silent on the form of the signal’s incorporation. Options, RSUs, and performance shares all condition on the stock price but produce different sensitivities of executive wealth to the price. Which form of conditioning best implements the Holmstrom logic? Why?
Some technology firms continue to report non-GAAP earnings measures that exclude stock-based compensation. Engage the argument for and against such exclusion. Does the framework of this chapter give you a reason to prefer one position over the other?

title: 10  Equity Compensation
text:
10.9 Problems

(Numerical, grant-date fair value.) Acme Corp. grants its CEO 10,000 stock options on January 1 with a strike of $40 (the grant-date stock price). The options have an expected term of seven years, expected annualized volatility of 35%, a risk-free rate of 4.5%, and an expected dividend yield of 1.5%. A Black-Scholes calculation produces a fair value per option of $14.50. (a) Compute the total grant-date fair value of the award. (b) Assuming four-year ratable vesting, compute the annual compensation expense. (c) Construct the journal entry Acme will record at the end of year 1.
(Numerical, RSU vesting and exercise.) On January 1, Acme grants its CFO 8,000 RSUs with a grant-date stock price of $60 per share and three-year cliff vesting. (a) Compute the grant-date fair value of the award. (b) Compute the annual compensation expense. (c) Construct the journal entry Acme will record on the day the RSUs vest, assuming all 8,000 vest and the stock price at vesting is $90 per share.
(Numerical, diluted EPS, treasury stock method.) Acme reports net income of $5,000,000 and has 2,000,000 weighted-average common shares outstanding for the year. Acme also has 200,000 stock options outstanding with a strike of $25, and the average market price during the year is $50. (a) Compute basic EPS. (b) Apply the treasury stock method to compute the dilutive effect of the options. (c) Compute diluted EPS.
(Numerical, performance share with market condition.) On January 1, Acme grants its CEO 5,000 performance shares that vest only if Acme’s three-year total shareholder return exceeds the median TSR of a 20-firm peer group. A Monte Carlo simulation conducted at grant date produces a fair value of $48 per share. (a) Compute the total grant-date fair value of the award. (b) Compute the annual compensation expense, assuming a three-year service period. (c) Explain why, under ASC 718, the compensation expense is recognized regardless of whether the market condition is ultimately met.
(Conceptual.) Read Bergstresser and Philippon (2006), focusing on Sections 1, 2, and 5. In approximately 350 words, summarize the empirical methodology, the headline finding on equity-pay sensitivity and discretionary accruals, and the authors’ interpretation of the result. Identify one alternative explanation for the cross-sectional pattern (other than the misreporting-incentive interpretation) and discuss whether the alternative is supported by the empirical evidence in the paper.
(Conceptual.) The FAS 123R episode produced a final standard that required fair-value expensing of stock options, over the objection of a substantial coalition of corporate issuers. Construct the argument both sides made: (i) the case for footnote-only disclosure under the pre-2005 framework, and (ii) the case for income-statement recognition at grant-date fair value. In approximately 400 words, identify the strongest version of each argument and indicate which side you find more persuasive and why.
(Writing exercise.) In approximately 600 words, describe a compensation contract you would design for the CEO of a hypothetical mid-cap U.S. public firm. Specify the mix of fixed salary, cash bonus, time-vested RSUs, and performance shares. For each component, indicate the rationale (referencing the framework of Chapter 4 and this chapter). Identify the misreporting incentive each component creates, and describe how you would mitigate it through contract design or through the firm’s internal-control environment.

title: 10  Equity Compensation
text:
10.10 Lab
The out-of-class lab for this week is WRDS Lab 5: Equity Compensation and Discretionary Accruals. The lab walks you through (i) the construction of a CEO-level wealth-performance-sensitivity measure from Execucomp, (ii) the computation of firm-level discretionary accruals using the modified Jones model on a panel of Compustat firms, and (iii) a cross-sectional regression of discretionary accruals on equity-pay sensitivity along the lines of Bergstresser and Philippon (2006). The deliverable is a Colab notebook that produces (a) the cross-sectional distribution of wealth-performance sensitivity in your sample, (b) the cross-sectional distribution of discretionary accruals, and (c) a brief written discussion of what your replication implies about the link between equity pay and misreporting in the modern sample.
Quiz 3 (covering Chapters 8 and 9 of the Companion) is administered at the start of the second class meeting this week. The quiz covers long-term assets and measurement choice and the contracting role of accounting in debt agreements. Bring a writing implement, a calculator, and the willingness to apply the framework to questions you have not seen before.
The GenAI policy permits the use of AI coding assistants on the lab, with disclosure. The policy continues to prohibit GenAI on quizzes and exams.

title: 10  Equity Compensation
text:
10.11 Further reading
The four readings below are the assigned readings for Week 10. You should read all of them.

Holmstrom (1979), “Moral Hazard and Observability.” Re-read for the informativeness principle that underlies the equity-compensation logic in this chapter. (You will have read this paper for Chapter 4.)
Bergstresser and Philippon (2006), “CEO Incentives and Earnings Management.” Read for the empirical methodology, the cross-sectional finding on equity-pay sensitivity and discretionary accruals, and the authors’ interpretation of the result.
Burns and Kedia (2006), “The Impact of Performance-Based Compensation on Misreporting.” Read for the focus on options’ convexity and the connection to restatement probability.
Optional, for those who want more: Erickson, Hanlon, and Maydew (2006) on the fraud-firm sample and the more cautious empirical conclusion; Heron and Lie (2007) on the options-backdating phenomenon; Core and Guay (2002) on the construct validity of grant-date fair-value estimates.







Closing Arc II



Chapter 10 closes Arc II of the course. Across five chapters we have moved from the abstract framework of Arc I into the empirical question of how capital markets actually use accounting numbers. We have seen that markets respond to earnings releases (Chapter 6), that the components of earnings are not equally informative (Chapter 7), that long-term asset measurement is a contested space where the relevance-faithfulness trade-off plays out (Chapter 8), that accounting plays a contracting role distinct from its capital-markets role (Chapter 9), and that equity compensation extends the contracting framework to the employer-employee relationship — with measurable consequences for managerial incentives and reported earnings (this chapter). Arc III moves inside the firm. The same framework that has been applied to external reporting and capital markets in Arcs I and II will be applied to internal reporting and management accounting: cost behavior, performance measurement, and capital budgeting will all be examined as instances of decentralization, agency, and the design of internal information systems.








Aboody, David, and Ron Kasznik. 2000. “CEO Stock Option Awards and the Timing of Corporate Voluntary Disclosures.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 29 (1): 73–100.


Bergstresser, Daniel, and Thomas Philippon. 2006. “CEO Incentives and Earnings Management.” Journal of Financial Economics 80 (3): 511–29.


Burns, Natasha, and Simi Kedia. 2006. “The Impact of Performance-Based Compensation on Misreporting.” Journal of Financial Economics 79 (1): 35–67.


Core, John E., and Wayne R. Guay. 2002. “Estimating the Value of Employee Stock Option Portfolios and Their Sensitivities to Price and Volatility.” Journal of Accounting Research 40 (3): 613–30.


Dechow, Patricia, Weili Ge, and Catherine Schrand. 2010. “Understanding Earnings Quality: A Review of the Proxies, Their Determinants and Their Consequences.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 50 (2–3): 344–401.


Erickson, Merle, Michelle Hanlon, and Edward L. Maydew. 2006. “Is There a Link Between Executive Equity Incentives and Accounting Fraud?” Journal of Accounting Research 44 (1): 113–43.


Healy, Paul M. 1985. “The Effect of Bonus Schemes on Accounting Decisions.” Journal of Accounting and Economics 7 (1–3): 85–107.


Heron, Randall A., and Erik Lie. 2007. “Does Backdating Explain the Stock Price Pattern Around Executive Stock Option Grants?” Journal of Financial Economics 83 (2): 271–95.


Holmstrom, Bengt. 1979. “Moral Hazard and Observability.” Bell Journal of Economics 10 (1): 74–91.

title: 11  Decentralization and Cost Behavior
text:
11.1 Opening question
Berkshire Hathaway owns roughly ninety wholly-owned operating subsidiaries spanning railroads, insurance, energy, retail, candy, paint, and underwear. The Omaha headquarters employs about thirty people. The thirty people do not run those businesses. They are run by the executives Berkshire acquired with each subsidiary, who make essentially all decisions about pricing, product mix, capital expenditure, hiring, and operations. Buffett’s standing instruction to subsidiary CEOs is to manage the business well and remit excess cash to the parent for capital allocation.
The structure is unusual in its extremity but not in its general orientation. Almost every large U.S. firm decentralizes substantial decision authority to operating units below the corporate level — divisions, subsidiaries, business units, regional groups — that report up through layered structures of accountability. Pricing, hiring, operational, and most investment decisions are made not by a single central authority but by an array of subordinate units.
The prevalence of decentralization raises a question Arc III takes up. Why do firms decentralize, and what role does accounting play in supporting decentralized decisions? The economic case for decentralization is articulated in Hayek (1945), who argued that the knowledge needed to make economic decisions is dispersed across the people who interact with local conditions and is difficult to aggregate at a central authority. The countervailing case for centralization comes from agency theory (Jensen and Meckling 1976, who modeled the firm as a nexus of contracts among parties with conflicting interests): decentralized managers may not act in the principal’s interest, and the costs of monitoring them can be substantial. The accounting system is the institutional infrastructure that makes the decentralization-versus-centralization trade-off workable. It supplies (i) local managers with the cost and revenue information they need to make decisions and (ii) the principal with monitoring information about the local managers.
This chapter introduces the conceptual frame and the workhorse mechanics. Section 11.2 develops the Hayek argument and pairs it with the agency-cost counterargument. Section 11.3 describes the institutional response: responsibility centers, cost allocation, and transfer pricing. Section 11.4 introduces cost behavior. Section 11.5 develops cost allocation through a worked numerical example. Section 11.6 introduces the contribution-margin income statement and cost-volume-profit analysis. Section 11.7 examines transfer pricing in detail and the Boeing operating-leverage case study.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information

title: 11  Decentralization and Cost Behavior
section: 11.2 The Hayek argument and the agency counterargument
text:
11.2 The Hayek argument and the agency counterargument
Hayek (1945), in “The Use of Knowledge in Society,” articulated the central informational case for decentralized decision-making. The paper was framed as a contribution to the debate over centrally planned versus market-based economies, but its logic applies equally to the internal organization of firms.
Hayek’s central observation was that the knowledge needed to make economic decisions is dispersed. The price of a particular good in a particular location at a particular time depends on local supply and demand conditions observable to the people who interact with the market directly. The technical possibilities for production depend on the specific equipment, materials, and workforce available at a particular site. The opportunities for cost reduction depend on the detailed operational knowledge of the people who do the work. None of this knowledge is, in any formal sense, secret. Its aggregation into a single picture a central authority could act on is itself a costly undertaking, and the cost grows rapidly with the volume of decisions and the geographic dispersion of the activity.
Hayek’s response was the price system. Markets, in his framing, are an information-aggregation mechanism (i.e., prices summarize the distributed knowledge of buyers and sellers in a form everyone can act on, even though no individual participant has access to the underlying information directly). The central planner who tries to replace prices with administrative directives faces the impossible task of aggregating the dispersed information without the help of the mechanism markets use to do the work.
The Hayek argument applies directly to the internal organization of firms. A corporate CEO faces the same dispersed-knowledge problem as a central economic planner. The information needed to make local decisions is available to the local managers but is costly to communicate completely upward. A firm that centralizes too many decisions sacrifices the local information advantage of the people closest to the work. This is the affirmative case for decentralization.
The counterargument is the agency problem introduced in Chapter 4. Decentralized decision-makers have private information and private preferences that may not align with the principal’s. A local manager may pursue empire-building, may shade reported results, may consume perquisites the principal would not approve, or may simply exert less effort than the principal would prefer. These are not hypothetical concerns: prior research (Jensen and Meckling 1976, who decomposed agency cost into monitoring, bonding, and residual loss) suggests that the deadweight loss from imperfect monitoring can be economically meaningful, particularly when local managers’ actions are difficult to observe.
The trade-off is the central problem of organizational design. Decentralization permits decisions to be made with better local information but creates agency problems. Centralization mitigates the agency problems but imposes information losses that may exceed the agency cost savings. The optimal organizational structure depends on the relative magnitudes of these two effects, which vary across firms and across types of decisions.






Why accounting sits at the center of the trade-off



Internal accounting is the institutional infrastructure that lets a firm decentralize without giving up the principal’s monitoring claim. The system performs two functions at once: (i) it supplies local managers with the cost, revenue, and performance information they need to make decisions on the principal’s behalf, and (ii) it supplies the principal with comparable measures of how each local manager is performing. The two functions can come into conflict — information designed to inform a local decision may not be the right information for monitoring the manager who made it — and the design of internal accounting systems is, in important respects, the management of that conflict.


The classic illustration is General Electric under Jack Welch (CEO from 1981 to 2001). Welch operated GE as a portfolio of more than a dozen large business units in disparate industries — aircraft engines, medical imaging, financial services, lighting, plastics — and gave each unit’s CEO substantial decision authority over its operations. The corporate parent set the strategic frame (“number one or number two in every business”), provided capital, and enforced an aggressive performance-evaluation system that ranked managers and divisions against one another. The structure was Hayekian in its delegation of operating decisions to people with local knowledge, and it was simultaneously agency-theoretic in its insistence on quantitative performance reporting against corporate-set standards. The combination is not unique to GE, but the Welch-era GE is a useful reference point for how the trade-off can be operationalized.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information

title: 11  Decentralization and Cost Behavior
text:
11.3 The institutional response
The institutional response to the decentralization problem has three principal components, all of which are realized through the firm’s internal accounting system.
The first is the responsibility center: an organizational unit with defined boundaries within which a single manager has authority and is held accountable for performance. The literature distinguishes four types.
A cost center is a unit whose manager is responsible for the costs of operating the unit but not for revenues or capital. Production departments, central administrative functions, and shared service centers (e.g., a payroll department or a corporate IT group) are commonly organized as cost centers. The performance metric is typically a comparison of actual costs to a budget or to a flexible standard.
A revenue center is a unit whose manager is responsible for the revenues generated by the unit but not for the costs of producing what the unit sells. Sales territories and individual store locations within a retail chain are commonly organized as revenue centers. The performance metric is typically a comparison of actual revenues to a budget or to prior-period results.
A profit center is a unit whose manager is responsible for both revenues and costs but not for the capital deployed in the unit. Product divisions within a multidivisional firm are commonly organized as profit centers. The performance metric is operating profit, often presented in a contribution-margin format.
An investment center is a unit whose manager is responsible for revenues, costs, and the capital deployed in the unit. The operating subsidiaries of Berkshire Hathaway, for example, are commonly organized as investment centers. The performance metric is some form of return on capital, such as ROI, residual income, or EVA, all of which we examine in Chapter 12.
The choice among these four forms is itself a Hayekian decision. A firm decentralizes more decision authority by establishing investment centers; it centralizes more by relying on cost centers. The choice depends on (i) the firm’s information environment, (ii) the heterogeneity of its operating units, and (iii) the costs of monitoring at different levels. The optimal mix typically varies across the firm — some functions are more naturally centralized, others more naturally decentralized — and the firm’s organizational chart is, in this sense, a snapshot of how the trade-off has been resolved across the firm’s activities.
The second component is cost allocation: the assignment of indirect costs (i.e., costs that cannot be traced directly to a specific product, service, or unit) to the responsibility centers or products that consume them. Section 11.5 develops cost allocation through a worked example. The general issue is that any allocation method requires choosing an allocation base (e.g., direct labor hours, machine hours, square footage), and the choice of base affects both the reported profitability of the receiving unit and the incentives of the manager who runs it.
The third component is transfer pricing: the price at which goods and services are exchanged between divisions of the same firm. Transfer prices serve two functions simultaneously. They provide divisional buyers with cost information that should influence their sourcing decisions, and they provide divisional sellers with revenue information that should influence their production and pricing decisions. The two functions can come into conflict. Section 11.7 develops transfer pricing in detail.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information

title: 11  Decentralization and Cost Behavior
text:
11.4 Cost behavior
The mechanical workhorse of internal accounting is cost behavior: the way costs change as the firm’s activity level changes. Most internal-accounting analyses begin with a classification of costs into three categories.
A variable cost is one that changes proportionally with activity. The cost of raw materials in a manufacturing setting is the canonical example: producing twice as many units requires twice as much material, and the raw material cost per unit is roughly constant across activity levels. At an automotive assembly plant, the steel, aluminum, glass, and purchased components that go into each vehicle are variable: each additional Ford F-150 rolling off the Dearborn line consumes a roughly constant bill of materials.
A fixed cost is one that does not change with activity within a relevant range. The rent on a factory building is the canonical example: the rent is the same whether the factory operates at fifty percent capacity or ninety percent. The depreciation on an automotive stamping press is fixed: the press’s annual cost is the same whether it stamps one shift or three. Fixed costs are not strictly fixed in the long run — a firm that consistently operates at fifty percent capacity might choose to relocate to a smaller building — but within the time horizon of most internal-accounting analyses, the cost is invariant to short-run activity changes.
A mixed cost (or semi-variable cost) is one that has both fixed and variable components. The total electricity bill of a factory typically includes a fixed monthly service fee plus a variable component proportional to consumption. The total compensation of a salesperson on a base-plus-commission contract is fixed in the base portion and variable in the commission portion.
The three-fold classification is a simplification. Many costs that appear fixed within a narrow range exhibit step behavior — they jump up to a new level at a particular activity threshold and remain constant within the next range. The cost of supervisors in a manufacturing facility is often step-fixed: one supervisor per fifteen workers, with the count jumping up as the workforce grows. The classification framework is sufficient for most analytical purposes; the step-cost refinement is introduced where it is operationally relevant.
A separate empirical observation worth noting is cost stickiness: the tendency of selling, general, and administrative costs to fall less in response to revenue declines than they rise in response to revenue increases. Anderson, Banker, and Janakiraman (2003) documented this asymmetry in a large U.S. sample and found that the empirical pattern is robust across industries and time periods. The standard interpretation is that cost-cutting decisions are themselves costly (e.g., terminating employees, breaking leases, disposing of equipment), so managers delay cost reductions in the hope that revenue declines are temporary. The cost-stickiness pattern qualifies the simple variable-fixed dichotomy by suggesting that the distinction may be asymmetric in the direction of revenue change.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information

title: 11  Decentralization and Cost Behavior
text:
11.5 Cost allocation: a worked example
Indirect costs — costs that benefit more than one product, division, or activity but cannot be traced directly to any one of them — are pervasive in multi-product and multi-divisional firms. Corporate headquarters expense, central IT, the CEO’s salary, the cost of the legal department, and depreciation on a shared manufacturing facility are all indirect from the perspective of the operating units that benefit from them. Internal accounting allocates these costs to the units that consume them using an allocation base: an observable quantity, such as direct labor hours or square footage, that is taken to proxy for the unit’s consumption of the indirect resource.
The choice of allocation base matters. Different bases produce different reported profitability for the same underlying operations, and the manager whose unit absorbs a larger share of overhead may face different incentives than the manager whose unit absorbs a smaller share.
Consider a stylized example. Apex Manufacturing operates two divisions, Consumer and Industrial. Each division generates its own revenue, incurs its own direct costs, and is supported by a shared corporate office that incurs $4,000,000 of overhead per year. The two divisions report:









Item
Consumer
Industrial
Total




Revenue
$30,000,000
$20,000,000
$50,000,000


Direct variable costs
$18,000,000
$10,000,000
$28,000,000


Direct fixed costs
$4,000,000
$3,000,000
$7,000,000


Direct labor hours
200,000
100,000
300,000


Floor space (sq. ft.)
50,000
150,000
200,000



Apex must allocate the $4,000,000 of corporate overhead to the two divisions. Three commonly used allocation bases would produce three different results.
Allocation by revenue. The Consumer division generates 60 percent of total revenue, the Industrial division 40 percent. Allocating overhead in proportion to revenue assigns $2,400,000 to Consumer and $1,600,000 to Industrial.
Allocation by direct labor hours. The Consumer division uses two-thirds of total direct labor hours, the Industrial division one-third. Allocating overhead in proportion to direct labor hours assigns $2,666,667 to Consumer and $1,333,333 to Industrial.
Allocation by floor space. The Consumer division occupies 25 percent of total floor space, the Industrial division 75 percent. Allocating overhead in proportion to floor space assigns $1,000,000 to Consumer and $3,000,000 to Industrial.
The three methods yield the same total ($4,000,000) but very different divisional results. Under revenue-based allocation, the Industrial division’s operating profit is $5,400,000 ($20,000,000 − $10,000,000 − $3,000,000 − $1,600,000). Under floor-space-based allocation, the Industrial division’s operating profit is $4,000,000. The choice of allocation base shifts $1,400,000 of reported profitability between the two divisions, without changing anything about their underlying operations.
The example illustrates two general points. First, an allocation base is defensible to the extent that it proxies for the receiving unit’s causal consumption of the indirect resource — the resource that drives the cost (e.g., a factory’s shared utilities are arguably caused by floor space; corporate legal expense is arguably caused by transaction volume; the CEO’s time is arguably caused by managerial attention rather than by any easily observed input). The choice is rarely clean. Second, the allocation choice has incentive consequences. A division manager who is evaluated on operating profit after allocation has an incentive to influence the choice of base in a direction that reduces the unit’s allocated overhead, and the firm’s senior management may face lobbying that has little to do with the true causal structure of cost.
The widely discussed alternative, activity-based costing (ABC), attempts to mitigate the distortion by identifying a separate allocation base for each activity that consumes the indirect resource — machine setups, purchase orders, customer service calls, engineering change requests — and allocating cost in proportion to each unit’s consumption of each activity. ABC produces more disaggregated and arguably more causally defensible allocations, but it is also costly to implement and to maintain, and the additional information value is not always worth the cost. The choice between traditional allocation and ABC is itself an instance of the broader cost-benefit calculation that pervades internal accounting design.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information

title: 11  Decentralization and Cost Behavior
section: 11.6 The contribution-margin income statement and CVP
text:
11.6 The contribution-margin income statement and CVP
The income statement format taught in Chapter 3 — revenues less expenses, with expenses organized by their financial-reporting category — is the external income statement format used in financial reports. For internal decision-making purposes, a different format is usually more useful. The contribution-margin income statement organizes expenses by their behavior (variable versus fixed) rather than by their reporting category, and produces a measure called the contribution margin that is central to internal-accounting analysis.
The structure is:



Line
Computation




Revenue
Sales price × Quantity


Variable costs
Variable cost per unit × Quantity


Contribution margin
Revenue − Variable costs


Fixed costs
(constant within the relevant range)


Operating profit
Contribution margin − Fixed costs



The contribution margin is the revenue available to cover fixed costs and contribute to profit after the variable costs of production have been paid. It is a measure of the marginal profitability of the firm’s activity: an additional unit of sales adds the contribution margin to operating profit, because the variable cost of production is paid out of the additional revenue and the fixed costs do not change. The contribution margin per unit (revenue per unit minus variable cost per unit) and the contribution-margin ratio (contribution margin divided by revenue) are the workhorse metrics for thinking about pricing, product mix, and capacity decisions.
The contribution-margin framework supports a particular form of analysis called cost-volume-profit (CVP) analysis, which examines how operating profit responds to changes in volume, prices, variable costs, and fixed costs, holding the rest of the firm’s operations constant. The central CVP equation is:
\[
\text{Operating profit} = (\text{Contribution margin per unit}) \times \text{Quantity} - \text{Fixed costs}.
\]
Setting operating profit equal to zero and solving for quantity yields the break-even quantity: the level of activity at which the firm’s revenues exactly cover its costs.
\[
\text{Break-even quantity} = \frac{\text{Fixed costs}}{\text{Contribution margin per unit}}.
\]
Consider an example. Acme Corp. operates a subscription software business with revenue of $200 per subscription per year, variable cost of $40 per subscription (server, payment processing, support), and fixed costs of $4,000,000 per year (headquarters, R&D, marketing). The contribution margin per subscription is $160 ($200 − $40). The break-even quantity is $4,000,000 / $160 = 25,000 subscriptions. At 25,000 subscriptions Acme exactly covers its costs; at higher volumes it earns operating profit at the rate of $160 per additional subscription; at lower volumes it operates at a loss at the same rate.
CVP analysis can be extended in several directions: (i) target-profit analysis computes the volume needed to achieve a specified operating profit, (ii) price-sensitivity analysis asks how break-even volume changes as the firm raises or lowers prices, (iii) cost-sensitivity analysis asks the analogous question for changes in variable or fixed costs, and (iv) multi-product analysis extends the framework to firms with multiple product lines using a weighted-average contribution margin based on the firm’s product mix.
The CVP framework also provides the right tool for thinking about operating leverage: the sensitivity of operating profit to changes in revenue. A firm with a high fraction of fixed costs has high operating leverage, so a one-percent change in revenue produces a more-than-one-percent change in operating profit. The formal measure is:
\[
\text{Degree of operating leverage} = \frac{\text{Contribution margin}}{\text{Operating profit}}.
\]
A firm whose contribution margin equals five times its operating profit has a degree of operating leverage of five: each one-percent change in revenue produces an approximately five-percent change in operating profit, holding the cost structure constant.






Common pitfall



The external (GAAP) income statement and the internal (contribution-margin) income statement do not, in general, agree on operating profit. Under GAAP, fixed manufacturing overhead is allocated to inventory and recognized only when the inventory is sold — an absorption costing treatment that cuts against the variable-fixed distinction the internal format requires. A firm that produces more than it sells in a period absorbs some of its fixed manufacturing costs into ending inventory, so the GAAP cost of goods sold is lower (and operating profit higher) than the contribution-margin operating profit would be. The discrepancy is one of the well-known sources of confusion between external and internal accounting.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information

title: 11  Decentralization and Cost Behavior
section: 11.7 Transfer pricing and the Boeing operating-leverage case
text:
11.7 Transfer pricing and the Boeing operating-leverage case
When a firm is decentralized into investment or profit centers, goods and services routinely move from one division to another. The price at which an internal transfer is recorded — the transfer price — is consequential. It allocates revenue between the selling and buying divisions, influences each manager’s reported performance, shapes each manager’s incentive to source internally rather than externally, and (for multinational firms) determines how taxable income is split across jurisdictions. The standard approaches are three.
A market-based transfer price sets the internal price equal to the price the selling division could obtain in an arms-length external market for the same good or service. The approach is defensible when a competitive external market exists: it puts each division on the same footing as an external counterparty and gives the buying division the right “make-or-buy” incentive. The approach is harder to apply when the transferred good has no clean external equivalent, as is common for specialized intermediate components.
A cost-based transfer price sets the internal price equal to some measure of the selling division’s cost — variable cost, full absorption cost, or full cost plus a markup. Cost-based pricing is administratively simple and is used widely when no external market price exists. The well-known distortion is that pricing at full cost gives the selling division no margin and a weak incentive to supply, while pricing at variable cost shifts all of the contribution to the buying division and gives the buying division no incentive to economize on the internally supplied input.
A negotiated transfer price is one the two divisions bargain over directly, with corporate headquarters intervening only when they cannot agree. Negotiation can in principle find a price that respects both divisions’ incentives, but it absorbs managerial time and can produce outcomes that depend on the divisions’ relative bargaining power rather than on the underlying economics.
Each method has known distortions, and the literature is consistent that no transfer-pricing system is uniformly first-best (Demski 2008, who develops the formal trade-offs in detail). Two further considerations sharpen the choice. First, incentive alignment: the transfer price should give both the buying and selling divisions decisions that the principal would endorse if she could see the underlying information. Second, taxation: for multinational firms, the transfer price determines how income is allocated across tax jurisdictions, and tax authorities (e.g., the U.S. Internal Revenue Service under Section 482, and the OECD’s transfer-pricing guidelines) require that intercompany prices approximate arms-length terms. Firms therefore face a constraint that the transfer prices they use for internal decision-making must also be defensible to tax authorities, which can pull the choice toward market-based prices even when other considerations point elsewhere.
A concrete illustration of operating leverage in a decentralized setting is Boeing. The firm’s commercial-aircraft business has enormous fixed costs — research and development, manufacturing infrastructure, supply-chain investments, certifications — that must be absorbed regardless of demand. Variable costs (raw materials, direct labor on individual aircraft) are smaller. The cost structure produces a high degree of operating leverage: when commercial aviation is robust and Boeing’s order book is full, the firm earns substantial operating profit; when commercial aviation contracts and orders fall, operating profit falls disproportionately fast — and, in extreme cases, becomes substantially negative. The 737 MAX grounding in 2019 and the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 produced consecutive operating losses at Boeing of historic magnitude, despite revenue declines that, by industrial-firm standards, were not particularly large. The amplification of those revenue declines into substantial losses is consistent with what a high-operating-leverage cost structure predicts.
The in-class activity for the second meeting of this week works through Boeing’s cost structure using the contribution-margin format, computes the degree of operating leverage at different revenue levels, and assesses what the high operating leverage implies for the volatility of Boeing’s reported earnings.






Key takeaways




Decentralization rests on dispersed knowledge. Hayek (1945) argued that the information needed to make economic decisions is held by the people who interact with local conditions, and a central authority cannot easily aggregate it.
Agency theory provides the counterweight. Decentralized managers may not act in the principal’s interest, suggesting that decentralization works only when the firm can also monitor what local managers do (Jensen and Meckling 1976).
