Summing the present values gives a project NPV of approximately $298{,}274. The project has a positive NPV at Acme’s cost of capital and, under the textbook decision rule, should be accepted.
The IRR is the discount rate at which the project’s NPV equals zero. Trial-and-error narrows the IRR for this project to approximately 17.0 percent. At a 15 percent discount rate the NPV is approximately $63{,}000 (positive); at a 20 percent rate the NPV is approximately $(140{,}000) (negative). Linear interpolation between the two points gives an estimate near 16.5 percent; a finer computation places the rate closer to 17.0 percent. The IRR comfortably exceeds Acme’s 10 percent cost of capital, consistent with the positive NPV.
The (undiscounted) payback period is the time required for cumulative net cash flows to equal the initial investment. The cumulative cash inflows after years 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 are $400{,}000, $900{,}000, $1{,}400{,}000, $1{,}800{,}000, and $2{,}400{,}000 respectively. The investment is recovered between years 3 and 4: the project has recovered $1{,}400{,}000 of the $1{,}500{,}000 investment by the end of year 3, and recovers the remaining $100{,}000 from the year-4 inflow of $400{,}000. Linear interpolation within year 4 gives a payback period of approximately \(3 + (100{,}000 / 400{,}000) = 3.25\) years. If Acme uses a four-year payback threshold, the project passes; if it uses a three-year threshold, the project fails despite its positive NPV.
The profitability index is \(1 + (298{,}274 / 1{,}500{,}000) \approx 1.199\). The project produces approximately $1.20 of present value per dollar of initial investment.
The four computations agree on the qualitative recommendation (accept), but they differ in the strength of the signal. NPV is comfortably positive but not large in relation to the investment. IRR is approximately 700 basis points above the cost of capital, providing a reasonable margin for estimation error. Payback at 3.25 years is short enough to clear most firm thresholds but not dramatically so. PI of 1.20 gives Acme a useful number for comparing this project against alternatives competing for the same capital. The Acme CFO, faced with this set of metrics, has more information than NPV alone would provide — exactly the pattern the Graham-Harvey survey documented.






NPV-IRR conflicts on mutually exclusive projects



Consider two mutually exclusive variants of the Acme project. Project A is the project just analyzed: $1{,}500{,}000 initial investment, NPV of $298{,}274 at 10 percent, IRR of approximately 17 percent. Project B is a smaller variant: $400{,}000 initial investment, expected cash flows that produce an NPV of $120{,}000 at 10 percent and an IRR of approximately 25 percent. Project B has the higher IRR; Project A has the higher NPV. The IRR rule, applied naively, would select Project B; the NPV rule selects Project A. The NPV rule is the appropriate criterion for the choice between mutually exclusive projects, because firm value is increased by the dollar amount of NPV created, not by the rate of return per dollar invested. The IRR ranking confounds project scale with project profitability, and the firm that ranks mutually exclusive projects by IRR will systematically underinvest in large, value-creating projects in favor of small, high-return ones.
Non-conventional cash-flow patterns produce a related complication. A project that requires $100{,}000 at \(t = 0\), returns $400{,}000 at \(t = 1\), and requires a $400{,}000 remediation outlay at \(t = 2\) has two IRRs — one near 25 percent and another near 400 percent — because the NPV function changes sign twice. The IRR rule cannot identify “the” rate of return; the analyst who relies on a single IRR will reach incorrect conclusions. NPV, applied at the firm’s cost of capital, gives an unambiguous answer.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information
13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information

title: 13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information
section: 13.5 Antle-Eppen: capital rationing and organizational slack
text:
13.5 Antle-Eppen: capital rationing and organizational slack
Antle and Eppen (1985) developed an analytical model of capital budgeting under asymmetric information that connects directly to the framework Arc III has developed. The model considers a corporate headquarters allocating capital across divisions, where each division has private information about the productivity of its investment opportunities. Headquarters cannot directly observe divisional productivity; it can observe only the project proposals divisions submit, and divisions have incentives to overstate the productivity of their proposals to obtain more capital than they truly need. Antle and Eppen showed that, under a wide class of information conditions, the optimal capital allocation policy involves capital rationing: corporate headquarters provides each division with a capital budget below the level that the division’s reported productivity would justify. The rationing serves a screening function: divisions with truly high-productivity projects will, on the margin, prefer to receive smaller capital allocations on terms that prevent overstatement, while divisions with low-productivity projects will be unable to extract excessive capital because the rationing constraint binds.
The Antle-Eppen framework also explains why firms often impose hurdle rates (i.e., the minimum return a project must promise to receive funding) that exceed the firm’s cost of capital. A divisional manager who must convince headquarters that her project clears a hurdle rate of, say, fifteen percent — even though the firm’s cost of capital is only ten percent — will have less ability to obtain capital for projects of marginal quality. The hurdle-rate-above-cost-of-capital pattern, widely observed in U.S. firms, is, on this analysis, a rational response to the asymmetric-information environment of capital allocation, not a textbook error.
The Antle-Eppen analysis has implications that extend beyond the model’s specific structure. The deeper point is that the capital budgeting process is a mechanism-design problem: corporate headquarters is designing the rules under which divisions submit project proposals, and the rules must accommodate the divisions’ incentives to misrepresent project information. The textbook NPV framework, taken in isolation, ignores this dimension. The actual capital budgeting practices of firms — capital rationing, hurdle rates above cost of capital, formal post-completion audits, multi-stage approval processes — are, in important respects, the institutional response to the mechanism-design problem.






Why this matters



The Antle-Eppen framework links capital budgeting to the broader Arc III theme. Internal accounting is the institutional response to information asymmetry between corporate headquarters and decentralized operating units, and the framework applies to capital budgeting as much as it applies to performance measurement. The capital allocation process is one of the most important single decisions the modern firm makes, and the analytical framework for thinking about it is the same framework we have used for cost behavior, performance measurement, and (in Arc I) external reporting.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information
13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information

title: 13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information
section: 13.6 Influence costs and internal capital markets
text:
13.6 Influence costs and internal capital markets
The Antle-Eppen model treats divisional managers as informationally private but otherwise non-strategic. A separate strand of the literature emphasizes that managers spend real resources lobbying for capital — preparing slide decks, building political coalitions, cultivating relationships with corporate officers — and that the lobbying activity itself is costly to the firm.
The classic statement is Milgrom (1988), “Employment Contracts, Influence Activities, and Efficient Organization Design,” which analyzed influence activities (i.e., effort that an employee expends to alter a decision in her favor, rather than to produce value for the firm) as a central organizational problem. Milgrom’s point is structural. Whenever a central decision maker exercises discretion over the allocation of valuable resources — capital budgets, promotions, project authority — the parties affected by the decision have an incentive to expend effort attempting to influence the decision in their favor. The effort is, from the firm’s perspective, pure waste: the manager who spends a week building a deck designed to make a marginal project look attractive is not spending that week producing value. The firm bears the influence cost twice — once in the direct effort the manager diverts from productive work, and again in the distortion the lobbying introduces into the eventual capital allocation.
Milgrom’s framework predicts that firms will adopt organizational rules that limit discretion precisely to reduce influence costs. Bright-line hurdle rates, formal capital-budgeting committees with documented criteria, requirements that projects be supported by external benchmarks rather than divisional advocacy, and even the deliberate use of capital rationing all serve to constrain headquarters’ discretion and thereby reduce the marginal payoff to influence activity. The textbook NPV framework, on this view, is not just an analytical tool but an institutional commitment device: by binding itself to a relatively mechanical procedure, headquarters reduces the incentive for divisional managers to invest in lobbying.
Stein (1997), “Internal Capital Markets and the Competition for Corporate Resources,” extended the analysis to the question of whether internal capital markets (i.e., the corporate-headquarters process by which capital is allocated across divisions of a multidivisional firm) function better or worse than the external capital market. Stein developed a model in which corporate headquarters’ active role generates two offsetting effects. The bright-side effect is winner-picking: headquarters has authority to reallocate capital from low-productivity divisions to high-productivity ones, an authority the external market lacks because external investors cannot force one firm to redirect capital to another. When headquarters has good information about relative project quality across divisions, the winner-picking advantage of internal capital markets is real. The dark-side effect — developed more fully in Scharfstein and Stein (2000), “The Dark Side of Internal Capital Markets” — is that the same allocation authority creates the influence-cost problem Milgrom identified. Divisional managers lobby for capital they do not deserve, and weak headquarters (perhaps under pressure to maintain peace among divisions, perhaps facing political constraints) ends up cross-subsidizing low-productivity divisions at the expense of high-productivity ones. Scharfstein and Stein described this pattern as corporate socialism: capital flows toward the divisions that lobby hardest rather than toward the divisions that would generate the most value.
The empirical literature on diversified firms has provided evidence that speaks to both effects. Berger and Ofek (1995) documented a diversification discount — diversified firms trade at approximately 13 to 15 percent below the sum of the imputed values of their stand-alone segments — consistent with internal capital markets that, on average, destroy value rather than create it. Lamont (1997) showed, using the 1986 oil-price collapse as a shock to oil-company cash flows, that diversified oil firms cut investment in their non-oil divisions when oil prices fell, suggesting that internal capital markets in those firms were less effective than external markets would have been. Rajan, Servaes, and Zingales (2000) documented that the diversification discount is larger in firms whose divisions have more dispersed investment opportunities, consistent with the prediction that the influence-cost problem is most severe when divisions differ substantially in true productivity. The combined evidence is consistent with the Scharfstein-Stein dark-side mechanism: in many diversified firms, the internal capital market appears to socialize capital across divisions rather than to pick winners.
The interpretation is, however, contested. Maksimovic and Phillips (2002) argued that the diversification discount partly reflects selection — the kinds of firms that diversify differ in unobservable ways from the kinds that do not — and showed that, in plant-level data, diversified firms transfer assets toward their more productive divisions in patterns broadly consistent with winner-picking. The empirical literature has not converged on a single verdict on whether internal capital markets, on average, create or destroy value, and the most defensible reading is that the answer depends on the firm: well-governed diversified firms with strong corporate finance functions appear to allocate capital reasonably, while diversified firms with weak governance and strong divisional politics exhibit the cross-subsidization pattern.
Two concrete examples illustrate the range. General Electric under Jack Welch (CEO 1981–2001) was widely cited in business literature as an example of an effective internal capital market: the firm’s “Session C” reviews, in which each division’s investment proposals were stress-tested against firm-wide alternatives, and the explicit policy of exiting any business that could not credibly target a top-two position in its industry, were designed (whether or not they fully succeeded) to combat the cross-subsidization tendency by binding headquarters to allocation criteria divisions could not easily lobby around. Berkshire Hathaway under Warren Buffett exemplifies a different model: the holding company allocates capital from operating subsidiaries to wholly new investments based on Buffett’s centralized judgment, deliberately keeping the operating subsidiaries’ role in the allocation process minimal. Both arrangements are responses to the same underlying problem — the influence-cost and winner-picking trade-off — but they resolve the problem in different ways.






The winner-picking / socialism trade-off in one sentence



Stein’s model captures the central tension: corporate headquarters has the authority to pick winners across divisions, an authority the external market lacks, but the same authority creates the opportunity for divisional managers to lobby for capital they do not deserve, and the empirical question is whether headquarters uses the authority to pick winners or is captured by the lobbying.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information
13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information

title: 13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information
text:
13.7 Real options
A separate qualification to the standard NPV framework concerns the treatment of flexibility in long-term investments. The standard NPV computation assumes a fixed cash flow profile to which the firm commits at the time of the investment. Real-world projects often have substantial flexibility: the firm can scale up if early results are favorable, scale down or abandon if they are unfavorable, defer the project to a later date, or modify the project’s scope in response to new information. The standard NPV technique fails to capture this flexibility, and projects with substantial flexibility are often undervalued by NPV alone.
The real options framework — developed in McDonald and Siegel (1986) on the value of waiting and synthesized in Dixit and Pindyck (1994) — addresses the concern by treating embedded flexibility as a financial option. Three option types recur. An option to delay allows the firm to postpone a project until uncertainty about its value resolves; when the project is irreversible and information will arrive over time, the option to delay has positive value, and a project with positive NPV today may rationally be deferred. An abandonment option allows the firm to discontinue a project and recover residual value if conditions turn unfavorable; the option limits downside losses and raises the project’s true value above its NPV-as-committed estimate. An expansion option allows the firm to make a small initial investment that creates the right (but not the obligation) to make a larger follow-on investment if early results are favorable; such projects have characteristic call-option payoffs and are systematically undervalued by NPV.
Consider a stylized pharmaceutical example. A drug-development firm is considering a Phase II clinical trial that costs $50 million and is expected to fail with probability 0.6 (producing no further cash flows) and succeed with probability 0.4 (allowing the firm to invest $400 million in Phase III, after which the expected discounted value of commercialization is $1.2 billion conditional on success). The standalone NPV of the Phase II trial, computed mechanically, treats Phase III as a planned outlay: expected total investment of $50M + 0.4 $400M = $210M against expected payoff of $0.4 $1.2B = $480M, for an expected NPV of $270M (ignoring discounting for simplicity). The mechanical NPV is positive but understates the project’s value. The correct framing is that Phase II purchases an option to invest in Phase III. The firm will exercise the option only if Phase II succeeds, in which case the Phase III investment has positive NPV. If Phase II fails, the firm abandons the project and avoids the Phase III outlay entirely. The optionality is what makes drug development economically viable in the face of high failure rates: each early-stage trial purchases the right, not the obligation, to continue. NPV treatments that ignore the optionality systematically undervalue R&D-intensive investments and partly explain why R&D-intensive firms maintain looser capital allocation processes than the standard textbook framework would prescribe.
The mechanical computation of real-option values is, in general, more complex than the standard NPV computation. The valuation of a real option requires assumptions about the volatility of the project’s underlying cash flows, the timing of available decisions, and the cost of the option’s exercise. In practice, firms often use simplified rules of thumb (e.g., adding a fixed premium to NPV for projects with substantial flexibility, or applying decision-tree analysis with explicit branch probabilities) rather than full option-pricing computations. The conceptual framework, however, is correct: flexibility has value, and NPV alone undervalues flexible projects.
The real-options framework intersects with the influence-cost framework in interesting ways. A capital-rationing constraint that prevents a division from undertaking a real-option-rich project is more costly than a constraint that prevents an inflexible project of the same NPV: the option value, which the rationing constraint forecloses, is part of the cost of the constraint. The empirical literature has documented patterns consistent with this reasoning, with firms in industries where real options are particularly important (e.g., R&D-intensive industries, resource-extraction industries, industries exposed to commodity-price volatility) maintaining looser capital allocation processes than firms in less option-rich industries.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information
13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information

title: 13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information
text:
13.8 Empirical evidence
The empirical literature on capital budgeting has grown substantially since the Graham-Harvey survey. Four findings are particularly relevant.
First, the use of multiple techniques documented by Graham and Harvey (2001) has been replicated in subsequent surveys and across other countries. Firms continue to use NPV alongside IRR and payback, and the use of multiple techniques is positively associated with firm size, sophistication of the finance function, and the complexity of the firm’s investment opportunities. The pattern is consistent with the framework of Section 13.5: the techniques serve different functions, and using multiple techniques captures information that any single technique would miss.
Second, the use of hurdle rates above cost of capital is widely documented. Graham and Harvey (2001) and the subsequent literature have found that the typical large U.S. firm uses a hurdle rate that exceeds its cost of capital by approximately three to five percentage points, with substantial variation across firms and industries. The pattern is broadly consistent with the Antle-Eppen analysis: hurdle rates serve a screening function in environments with asymmetric information, and the optimal screening intensity depends on the firm’s information environment.
Third, the diversification discount documented by Berger and Ofek (1995) — diversified firms trade at approximately 13 to 15 percent below the sum of the imputed values of their stand-alone segments — has been replicated and extended in numerous subsequent studies. Lamont (1997) provided complementary evidence using the 1986 oil-price shock, and Rajan, Servaes, and Zingales (2000) showed that the discount is larger in firms with greater dispersion in divisional investment opportunities. The pattern is consistent with the Scharfstein-Stein dark-side mechanism of internal capital markets, though the Maksimovic and Phillips (2002) evidence using plant-level transfers suggests that the average effect masks substantial heterogeneity, with some firms allocating capital well and others poorly.
Fourth, the capital allocation process in multidivisional firms has been extensively studied in the empirical literature. The literature has documented that capital allocations within firms are systematically related to divisional performance metrics and to the strategic priorities of corporate headquarters, but also that allocations frequently deviate from what the textbook NPV framework would prescribe. Some of the deviation reflects the asymmetric-information environment of the Antle-Eppen analysis; some reflects the political economy of capital allocation within firms (i.e., managerial influence and the relative bargaining position of divisions, in the sense of Milgrom (1988)); and some reflects the explicit consideration of real-option value that NPV alone undervalues.






Key takeaways




NPV is theoretically correct but practically incomplete. The technique is appropriate under symmetric information and frictionless capital, conditions that approximate few real firms; the supplementary techniques firms actually use respond to features of the practical environment that NPV alone ignores.
IRR and payback survive because they are informative on dimensions NPV omits. IRR conveys a margin-of-safety relative to the cost of capital, payback conveys a liquidity recovery time, and the profitability index conveys value created per dollar of constrained capital — each useful when the textbook NPV assumptions fail.
Capital rationing and hurdle rates above the cost of capital are not textbook errors. Consistent with Antle and Eppen (1985), they are optimal screening devices in environments where divisional managers have private information and incentives to overstate project quality.
Influence costs make centralized capital allocation expensive even when headquarters is well-intentioned. Consistent with Milgrom (1988), the lobbying effort divisional managers expend on capital is, from the firm’s perspective, a real social cost — both directly and through the distortions it introduces into the allocation.
Internal capital markets pick winners or cross-subsidize losers depending on governance. Consistent with Stein (1997) and Scharfstein and Stein (2000), the same allocation authority that enables winner-picking creates the opportunity for socialism, and the empirical evidence on the diversification discount suggests that, on average, the dark side is non-trivial.
Real options raise the value of flexibility above the NPV baseline. Projects with embedded options to delay, abandon, or expand are systematically undervalued by mechanical NPV, and the practical capital-budgeting processes of R&D-intensive and resource-extraction firms reflect the importance of preserving optionality.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information
13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information

title: 13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information
text:
13.9 Discussion questions

The Graham-Harvey survey found that approximately seventy-five percent of CFOs use IRR and roughly the same percentage use NPV — even though the two techniques are theoretically equivalent for projects with conventional cash flows. Why might a CFO use both? What information does each technique provide that the other does not?
Payback period ignores cash flows beyond the payback horizon and ignores the time value of money. Yet it remains in widespread use. Identify three plausible reasons for its persistence and rank them by which you find most compelling.
The Antle-Eppen framework predicts that hurdle rates above cost of capital are an optimal response to asymmetric information about project quality. Suppose a firm uses a hurdle rate ten percentage points above its cost of capital. What does the choice imply about the firm’s information environment and about the productivity of its divisional managers? Be specific.
The Milgrom (1988) framework predicts that firms will adopt rules limiting headquarters’ discretion in order to reduce influence costs. Identify a specific capital-budgeting rule that a real firm uses (e.g., a published hurdle rate, a requirement for external benchmarking, a multi-stage gating process) and explain how it constrains influence activities.
The Scharfstein-Stein “corporate socialism” mechanism predicts that internal capital markets in diversified firms cross-subsidize low-productivity divisions at the expense of high-productivity ones. The Maksimovic-Phillips evidence suggests that, in plant-level data, diversified firms transfer assets toward more productive divisions. How would you reconcile the two findings, and what would you want to see in additional evidence to take a stronger position?
The real-options framework predicts that firms in R&D-intensive industries should maintain looser capital allocation processes than firms in less option-rich industries. Suppose you observe a pharmaceutical firm with a strict payback-based capital allocation rule. What does the choice suggest, and what would you advise the firm to consider?
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information
13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information

title: 13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information
text:
13.10 Problems

(Numerical, NPV and IRR.) A five-year project requires an initial investment of $1{,}200{,}000 and is expected to produce after-tax cash flows of $250{,}000, $350{,}000, $400{,}000, $400{,}000, and $300{,}000 in years 1 through 5, plus a terminal value of $200{,}000 received at the end of year 5. The firm’s cost of capital is 9 percent. (a) Compute the project’s NPV. (b) Compute the project’s IRR using trial-and-error or a spreadsheet’s IRR function. (c) Compute the project’s undiscounted payback period. (d) Compute the profitability index. (e) Apply each of the four decision rules — NPV, IRR (with hurdle equal to cost of capital), payback (with a three-year threshold), and PI — and discuss whether they agree.
(Numerical, capital rationing with PI.) A division of Acme Corp. has identified five candidate projects with the following NPVs and capital requirements: Project A, NPV $80{,}000, capital $200{,}000; Project B, NPV $60{,}000, capital $150{,}000; Project C, NPV $50{,}000, capital $100{,}000; Project D, NPV $40{,}000, capital $200{,}000; Project E, NPV $20{,}000, capital $50{,}000. The division’s capital budget is $400{,}000. (a) Compute the profitability index for each project. (b) Identify the value-maximizing combination of projects subject to the capital constraint, using the PI ranking as a starting point and confirming the result by checking the aggregate NPV of each feasible combination. (c) Compute the opportunity cost of capital for the marginal accepted project and compare it to the firm’s nominal cost of capital. Discuss the implications for the divisional hurdle rate.
(Numerical, real options — the option to wait.) A firm is considering a project that requires an immediate $10 million investment. If undertaken today, the project produces a perpetual after-tax cash flow whose present value is, with probability 0.5, $14 million (favorable state) and, with probability 0.5, $8 million (unfavorable state). The state will be revealed in one year if the firm waits; the $10 million investment can be made then if the state is favorable, or not made at all if the state is unfavorable. The risk-free rate is 5 percent. (a) Compute the project’s NPV if undertaken immediately. (b) Compute the expected NPV if the firm waits one year and invests only in the favorable state (treat the cash flows as risk-neutral for simplicity, and discount all year-1 values at the risk-free rate). (c) Compute the value of the option to wait and explain what it represents.
(Numerical, NPV-IRR conflict on mutually exclusive projects.) A firm must choose between two mutually exclusive projects of different scale. Project Small requires $100{,}000 and produces a single year-1 cash flow of $130{,}000. Project Large requires $1{,}000{,}000 and produces a single year-1 cash flow of $1{,}200{,}000. The firm’s cost of capital is 10 percent. (a) Compute each project’s NPV and IRR. (b) Identify the project the IRR rule selects and the project the NPV rule selects. (c) Explain which rule is correct and why.
(Conceptual, influence costs.) Read Milgrom (1988), focusing on the introduction and the central definition of influence activities. In approximately 350 words, summarize the central argument and apply the framework to a specific internal-capital-market setting (e.g., capital budgeting at a large multidivisional firm, faculty hiring at a research university, project funding at a federal agency). Identify two specific organizational rules that would, on Milgrom’s framework, reduce the influence costs in your chosen setting.
(Conceptual, diversification discount.) Read the introductions and central results of Berger and Ofek (1995), Lamont (1997), and Rajan, Servaes, and Zingales (2000). In approximately 500 words, evaluate the empirical case for the proposition that internal capital markets, on average, destroy value. What is the evidence’s strongest piece, what is the weakest, and what additional evidence would you want before forming a confident view? Consider in particular the selection-based critique in Maksimovic and Phillips (2002).
(Conceptual, winner-picking vs. socialism.) The chapter contrasted GE under Jack Welch with Berkshire Hathaway as two responses to the winner-picking / socialism trade-off. Identify a third public company whose capital-allocation arrangements are publicly documented (in annual reports, investor presentations, or business journalism). Describe the firm’s approach, classify it relative to the GE and Berkshire models, and assess whether the approach appears to address the influence-cost problem.
(Writing exercise.) In approximately 600 words, take a position on the following claim: “The widespread practice of using hurdle rates substantially above the firm’s cost of capital is an institutional error that destroys value by causing firms to reject positive-NPV projects.” Marshal evidence from this chapter — including the Antle-Eppen, Milgrom, and Stein analyses — and from your own reading. Acknowledge the strongest counterargument and respond to it. Your essay should take a position rather than survey the literature.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information
13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information

title: 13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information
text:
13.11 Lab
The in-class activity for this week is a capital budgeting simulation in which students play roles as divisional managers proposing projects to a corporate headquarters that must allocate a fixed capital budget across competing proposals. Each project proposal embeds private information about the project’s true productivity that the divisional manager knows but corporate headquarters does not. Divisional managers may also invest “lobbying effort” — a costly action that increases the probability headquarters funds the project but produces no value for the firm — capturing the influence-cost mechanism of Milgrom (1988). The simulation makes the mechanism-design framework concrete: students experience, in real time, the trade-off between providing capital to high-productivity projects, avoiding overpayment to managers who overstate their project quality, and limiting the wasteful expenditure of effort on internal politics.
Quiz 4 (covering Chapters 10–12 of the Companion) is administered at the start of the second class meeting this week. The quiz covers earnings management, the equity and compensation framework, decentralization and cost behavior, and performance measurement. Bring a writing implement, a calculator, and the willingness to apply the framework to questions you have not seen before.
There is no out-of-class lab for this week. The Lab 6 group empirical project has been ongoing through Arc III; preliminary findings are due in Week 14.
crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information
13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information

title: 13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information
text:
13.12 Further reading
The readings below are the assigned readings for Week 13. You should read all of them.

Antle and Eppen (1985), “Capital Rationing and Organizational Slack in Capital Budgeting.” Read for the model setup and the central result on capital rationing as an optimal screening device. The proofs can be skimmed.
Graham and Harvey (2001), “The Theory and Practice of Corporate Finance: Evidence from the Field.” Read for the survey methodology and the findings on capital budgeting practice. The other sections of the paper (capital structure, dividends, etc.) are useful background.
Milgrom (1988), “Employment Contracts, Influence Activities, and Efficient Organization Design.” Read for the central definition of influence activities and the implications for organizational design. The model section can be skimmed.
Stein (1997), “Internal Capital Markets and the Competition for Corporate Resources.” Read for the winner-picking mechanism. The companion Scharfstein and Stein (2000) develops the dark-side complement and is worth skimming.
A short selection from Demski (2008) on capital budgeting and decentralized decision-making (chapter assignment to be specified on Canvas).
Optional, for those who want more: Berger and Ofek (1995), Lamont (1997), and Rajan, Servaes, and Zingales (2000) on the diversification discount; Maksimovic and Phillips (2002) on the selection-based critique; and Dixit and Pindyck (1994) (book-length) for a thorough treatment of real options.







Closing Arc III



Chapter 13 closes the substantive content of Arc III. Across three chapters we have moved from the abstract framework of decentralization (Chapter 11), through the design of performance metrics (Chapter 12), to the mechanism-design problem of capital allocation (Chapter 13). The thread connecting all three chapters is that internal accounting is the institutional infrastructure through which firms manage the trade-off between local information advantages and corporate-level coordination — the same framework Arc I and Arc II developed for external reporting, applied inside the firm. Chapter 14 closes the course by returning to a small set of contested conceptual questions and asking you to argue both sides of each.

Berger, Philip G., and Eli Ofek. 1995. “Diversification’s Effect on Firm Value.” Journal of Financial Economics 37 (1): 39–65.


Demski, Joel S. 2008. Managerial Uses of Accounting Information. 2nd ed. New York: Springer.


Dixit, Avinash K., and Robert S. Pindyck. 1994. Investment Under Uncertainty. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press.


Graham, John R., and Campbell R. Harvey. 2001. “The Theory and Practice of Corporate Finance: Evidence from the Field.” Journal of Financial Economics 60 (2–3): 187–243.


Lamont, Owen. 1997. “Cash Flow and Investment: Evidence from Internal Capital Markets.” Journal of Finance 52 (1): 83–109.


Maksimovic, Vojislav, and Gordon Phillips. 2002. “Do Conglomerate Firms Allocate Resources Inefficiently Across Industries? Theory and Evidence.” The Journal of Finance 57 (2): 721–67. https://doi.org/10.1111/1540-6261.00440.


McDonald, Robert, and Daniel Siegel. 1986. “The Value of Waiting to Invest.” Quarterly Journal of Economics 101 (4): 707–27.


Milgrom, Paul. 1988. “Employment Contracts, Influence Activities, and Efficient Organization Design.” Journal of Political Economy 96 (1): 42–60.


Rajan, Raghuram, Henri Servaes, and Luigi Zingales. 2000. “The Cost of Diversity: The Diversification Discount and Inefficient Investment.” Journal of Finance 55 (1): 35–80.


Scharfstein, David S., and Jeremy C. Stein. 2000. “The Dark Side of Internal Capital Markets: Divisional Rent-Seeking and Inefficient Investment.” Journal of Finance 55 (6): 2537–64.

crumbs: Arc III — Contracting and Internal Information
13  Capital Budgeting Under Asymmetric Information

title: 14  Synthesis: The Capstone Debates
text:
14.1 Where we have been
The framework is finished. Across the previous thirteen chapters, the Companion has assembled a single integrated apparatus for thinking about financial and managerial accounting, anchored in information economics.
Arc I introduced the four foundational problems. The modern economy requires owners to delegate control of capital to managers they cannot directly observe (Berle and Means 1932; Jensen and Meckling 1976); the resulting information asymmetry produces (i) adverse selection, which mandatory disclosure helps to mitigate (Akerlof 1970); (ii) voluntary disclosure incentives, which partially unravel separating equilibria (Verrecchia 1983; Dye 1985); (iii) moral hazard (i.e., unobservable managerial action), which the institutional response of audits, internal controls, and accounting-based compensation contracts is designed to address (Holmstrom 1979); and (iv) a standard-setting process that is itself a political-economic equilibrium subject to the same incentives the rules attempt to discipline (Watts 2003).
Arc II asked how capital markets use the numbers the institutional infrastructure produces. Markets respond to earnings announcements (Ball and Brown 1968; Beaver 1968) but do not always price the components of earnings correctly (Sloan 1996); long-term asset measurement is a contested space where the relevance-faithfulness trade-off plays out (Holthausen and Watts 2001; Barth, Beaver, and Landsman 2001); accounting plays a contracting role in debt agreements that is distinct from its capital-markets role (Smith and Warner 1979; Roberts and Sufi 2009); and managers respond to the incentives created by their compensation contracts in measurable ways that produce predictable patterns in reported earnings (Healy 1985; Burgstahler and Dichev 1997).
Arc III turned inside the firm. The same framework that motivates external reporting motivates internal reporting: decentralization (i.e., the delegation of decision rights to local managers) creates the information conditions under which internal accounting becomes valuable (Hayek 1945); performance measurement is the design problem that arises once central authority delegates those rights (Holmstrom and Milgrom 1991); capital budgeting is the mechanism-design problem of allocating capital across divisions whose private information about project quality cannot be directly verified (Antle and Eppen 1985).
The framework is not a complete theory of accounting. It does not, in itself, settle the contested questions that practitioners and standard-setters wrestle with in their daily work. It does, however, provide a vocabulary and a set of analytical tools that can structure those wrestling matches. The capstone debates that occupy Weeks 14 and 15 of the course are designed to put the framework to work on a small set of currently contested questions in financial reporting.

title: 14  Synthesis: The Capstone Debates
text:
14.2 How to argue both sides
The debates that follow are not exercises in expressing personal opinions. They are exercises in steelmanning (i.e., constructing the strongest available version of a position one may not personally hold). The structure of each debate is identical, and we lay it out here so that the reader can use the same structure when preparing for class.
For each contested question, the chapter walks through five elements in order.

The question. Stated as a resolution, in a single sentence, with the contested term defined parenthetically where necessary.
The strongest argument for the affirmative. Built from the framework — adverse selection, signaling, moral hazard, agency cost, decentralization, market efficiency — rather than from extemporaneous opinion.
The strongest argument for the negative. Built from the same framework, drawing on the verifiability, political-economy, or contracting concerns the framework also articulates.
The kind of evidence that would resolve the disagreement, and what the empirical literature currently shows. Most contested questions in accounting are contested precisely because the available evidence is incomplete or contradictory; we say so when that is the case.
A tentative conclusion, stated in hedged voice. Not a verdict. The point is to model the calibrated-uncertainty posture that the rest of the Companion has tried to instill.

The five-element structure is the analytical scaffold. The classroom format that wraps around it — opening arguments, cross-examination, rebuttal, audience question and answer, evaluation by non-presenting students — is described in Section 14.4 below. Both structures are designed to do the same thing: force each side to articulate its strongest case and to engage seriously with the strongest case for the opposing side.






Why the random assignment matters



Assigning positions randomly is a deliberate pedagogical choice. Students who are forced to argue for a position they personally find unpersuasive must do the work of constructing the best argument from a perspective they did not begin with. The resulting argument is, almost always, more careful and more rigorously grounded than the argument those same students would have produced if they had been assigned the position they already held. The exercise teaches that contested questions usually have more support on the side the student initially rejected than the student initially supposed, and that the case for the student’s own initial view is often weaker than the student initially thought.

title: 14  Synthesis: The Capstone Debates
text:
14.3 The four 2027 debate prompts
The four debate prompts for Spring 2027 are listed below. Each connects to specific chapters of the Companion, and the connections are noted explicitly so that students preparing their constructive arguments can locate the analytical tools they need. Each prompt is developed using the five-element structure introduced in Section 14.2.

14.3.1 Debate 1: Mandatory climate disclosure

Resolved: Mandatory disclosure of climate-related financial information will improve capital allocation in the U.S. economy.

Framework anchors. Chapter 2 (mandatory disclosure as a response to adverse selection); Chapter 5 (the political economy of standard setting and the cost-benefit trade-off in mandatory regulation); Chapter 6 (capital-market consequences of new disclosure regimes).
The affirmative case. If climate-related risks are economically material and currently asymmetrically known to issuers, the standard adverse-selection logic of Akerlof (1970) — in which buyers who cannot distinguish high-quality from low-quality assets apply a pooled discount that drives the high-quality assets out of the market — implies that mandatory disclosure should reduce the climate-related lemons discount and reallocate capital toward firms with favorable climate exposures. The cross-country evidence in Daske et al. (2008), who study the capital-market effects of mandatory IFRS adoption and find liquidity improvements concentrated in countries with strong enforcement, is consistent with the more general proposition that mandatory disclosure regimes can produce measurable capital-market benefits when enforcement is credible.
The negative case. Three concerns qualify the affirmative case. First, compliance costs of climate disclosure are large and are concentrated on issuers; the benefits are diffuse and may not exceed the costs, particularly for smaller firms whose climate footprint is itself small. Second, climate-related information is highly uncertain (i.e., the underlying quantities being disclosed are themselves estimates with wide confidence intervals), and may be more meaningfully provided through alternative channels — sustainability reports, third-party ratings, voluntary frameworks — than through mandatory SEC filings whose content is constrained by litigation risk. Third, the political-economy framework of Stigler (1971), in which regulation is captured by the regulated industry, predicts that mandatory standards in newly-regulated areas often serve the interests of incumbent regulated parties who can shape the rules, which qualifies the case for mandatory adoption.
What would resolve the disagreement. A credible test would compare capital allocation outcomes — cost of capital, investment in climate-favored versus climate-unfavored firms, the cross-sectional pricing of climate risk — before and after a mandatory regime, ideally exploiting a regime that is exogenous to the firms it covers. The evidence on existing regimes (the EU CSRD, the California climate disclosure laws, the SEC’s proposed rules) is mixed and is developing rapidly; no single study yet provides a clean read on the U.S. allocation question.
Tentative conclusion. The theoretical case for mandatory climate disclosure is consistent with the adverse-selection framework, but is not equivalent to a case for any particular regime. Whether a particular set of rules will improve allocation depends on whether the rules require information that is verifiable, decision-useful, and material — three conditions that, as Chapter 5 makes clear, are difficult to satisfy simultaneously. The evidence is suggestive but not definitive.


14.3.2 Debate 2: Annual fair-value remeasurement of goodwill

Resolved: Goodwill should be remeasured annually at fair value rather than tested for impairment.

Framework anchors. Chapter 8 (the impairment-only model, the relevance-faithfulness trade-off, the empirical evidence on impairment timing); Chapter 5 (the standard-setting process and the qualitative characteristics framework).
The affirmative case. The post-2001 impairment-only model has produced systematic delays in loss recognition and an accumulating overstatement of goodwill on U.S. balance sheets. Li and Sloan (2017), who examine the timing of goodwill impairments under SFAS 142, document that impairments tend to lag economically observable declines in performance — consistent with the view that managers exercise discretion to delay loss recognition. Annual fair-value remeasurement would address the delay problem by removing the discretion in impairment timing and forcing recognition on a regular cycle. The relevance dimension of the relevance-faithfulness trade-off, articulated in Barth, Beaver, and Landsman (2001), supports a measurement basis that updates the carrying amount of goodwill to its current economic value.
The negative case. The verifiability concern of Chapter 5 — articulated forcefully in Holthausen and Watts (2001), who argue that the value-relevance literature underweights the role of accounting in contracting and stewardship — cuts against annual remeasurement. Fair value of goodwill is highly subjective: it varies widely depending on assumptions about discount rates, expected cash flows, and comparable transactions. The verification structure of the impairment-only model, although flawed, at least requires the firm to defend the estimate against an objective trigger (an event-driven impairment indicator); an annual remeasurement model would require the firm to defend an unconstrained estimate against the auditor’s professional judgment, which is a weaker discipline. The moral-hazard concern of Chapter 4 also bears on the question: where management has discretion over an unverifiable estimate that flows directly to earnings, the predictable response is the kind of opportunistic adjustment that Healy (1985) and Burgstahler and Dichev (1997) document in other contexts.
What would resolve the disagreement. A credible test would compare the predictive ability and contracting usefulness of carrying amounts under the two regimes. The impairment-only literature provides one half of the comparison; the other half does not yet exist for U.S. goodwill, because no U.S. regime has required annual remeasurement. The IFRS literature on annual remeasurement of other intangibles is partially informative but is not a clean analog.
Tentative conclusion. Both positions have framework support, and the framework alone does not resolve the question. The choice turns on an empirical judgment about which kind of measurement error is larger — the delay-and-bias error of the impairment-only model or the estimation-and-discretion error of annual remeasurement. The available evidence is consistent with the existence of both errors; it does not currently support a confident ranking between them.


14.3.3 Debate 3: Tax deductibility of stock-based compensation

Resolved: Stock-based compensation distorts performance measurement and should not be tax-deductible.

Framework anchors. Chapter 10 (the ASC 718 framework and the empirical literature on earnings management around equity-grant timing); Chapter 12 (the multitasking framework on performance measurement). The tax angle adds an additional dimension that is touched on only indirectly in the Companion but that students are encouraged to engage with seriously.
The affirmative case. The argument has two parts. First, stock-based compensation (SBC) is large enough that its accounting treatment has substantial consequences for performance measurement: if the recognition of SBC introduces predictable distortions, the tax system’s deductibility of SBC compounds the distortion at an additional cost to the public fisc. Second, the empirical literature documents that SBC is concentrated in firms whose performance metrics are particularly susceptible to managerial influence. Bergstresser and Philippon (2006) find that the use of discretionary accruals is more pronounced in firms whose CEOs have a higher fraction of compensation tied to equity, and Heron and Lie (2007) document patterns in option-grant timing consistent with backdating. Both findings are consistent with the more general proposition that equity compensation creates incentives to manage reported earnings — incentives that the tax deductibility of SBC subsidizes.
The negative case. Two counterarguments deserve serious engagement. First, SBC is a real economic cost — the chapter’s pitfall callout in Section 10.7 makes this point — and denying tax deductibility would treat it differently from cash compensation, distorting the form-of-payment decision in ways that are themselves welfare-reducing. The multitasking framework of Holmstrom and Milgrom (1991) implies that equity compensation has a legitimate role in aligning managerial incentives along dimensions that are difficult to measure directly; restricting its tax treatment would distort that alignment. Second, the performance-measurement distortions of SBC are, on this argument, a separate problem better addressed through governance and contracting reforms (e.g., clawbacks, vesting design, independent compensation committees) than through the tax system.
What would resolve the disagreement. A clean test would compare firms’ use of SBC, the quality of their reported earnings, and the allocation of compensation across forms under tax regimes that differ in the deductibility of SBC. Section 162(m) of the Internal Revenue Code, which limits the deductibility of executive compensation above $1 million, has been studied as a partial natural experiment; the existing evidence is mixed. No clean test of full versus partial deductibility of SBC is currently available in the U.S. setting.
Tentative conclusion. The affirmative case rests on empirical claims about the distortion that the tax deductibility of SBC produces; the negative case rests on theoretical claims about the role of equity compensation in alignment. Both are reasonable. The framework does not provide a sharp answer because the question is fundamentally a comparative-institutions question (i.e., which combination of tax rules, accounting rules, and governance arrangements minimizes the joint cost of agency and reporting distortions). The available evidence is consistent with a meaningful distortion but does not pin down the magnitude.


14.3.4 Debate 4: ESG reporting — adverse selection or moral hazard?

Resolved: Mandatory ESG reporting is a solution to a moral hazard problem, not an information asymmetry problem.

Framework anchors. Chapter 2 (adverse selection and mandatory disclosure); Chapter 4 (moral hazard and the institutional response). The prompt is explicitly synthetic — it asks students to apply the conceptual distinction between the two underlying problems to a contested area of contemporary policy. The distinction matters because the two problems imply different institutional responses: adverse selection (i.e., a hidden-type problem) is addressed through disclosure of ex-ante characteristics, while moral hazard (i.e., a hidden-action problem) is addressed through contractual mechanisms that condition on ex-post performance.
The affirmative case. The ESG concerns that mandatory reporting is intended to address — corporate behaviors with environmental and social externalities — are principally moral hazard problems. The firm’s behavior, not the firm’s type, is the relevant object of concern; investors and regulators want to influence corporate behavior, not merely sort firms by their underlying characteristics. The natural institutional response to a moral hazard problem is a contracting mechanism (regulation, taxation, liability), not a disclosure mandate. On this view, a disclosure regime that does not condition outcomes on behavior is a poor instrument for the underlying problem, and the political support for ESG disclosure reflects, in part, the lower political cost of disclosure relative to direct regulation.
The negative case. ESG reporting addresses an adverse-selection problem in capital markets. Investors with social or environmental preferences cannot easily distinguish firms by their ex-ante characteristics; mandatory ESG reporting reduces the lemons discount such investors apply to firms whose ESG characteristics are unobserved. The argument is the same as the standard Akerlof (1970) case for mandatory disclosure of any economically material attribute, applied to a particular class of investors. On this view, the institutional response is appropriately a disclosure mandate, not a behavioral mandate, and the question of whether to influence behavior is a separate question that should be settled separately.
What would resolve the disagreement. The question is partly conceptual and partly empirical. The conceptual question is whether investors and regulators primarily seek to sort firms or to influence them; the empirical question is whether mandatory ESG disclosure, in practice, produces measurable changes in capital allocation (the adverse-selection prediction) or measurable changes in corporate behavior (the moral-hazard prediction). The early evidence on the EU regimes speaks to both predictions but does not yet provide a clean read on either.
Tentative conclusion. The two framings are not mutually exclusive — ESG reporting can serve both functions simultaneously. The interesting analytical question is which function it serves primarily, and the empirical evidence is currently insufficient to settle the question. Recognizing the distinction is itself the principal pedagogical payoff: students who can articulate why the two problems imply different institutional responses have internalized the central conceptual move of Arc I.

title: 14  Synthesis: The Capstone Debates
text:
14.4 The classroom format
The five-element analytical structure of Section 14.2 is the substance of each debate. The classroom format that wraps around it is described here.
For each debate, the class is divided randomly into two groups. One group is assigned the affirmative position; the other is assigned the negative position. Group composition is determined by the instructor at random; students do not select their position based on their personal views.
Each debate proceeds through six stages.

Constructive arguments. Each team presents a structured opening argument supporting its position. Constructive arguments are due to both the opposing team and the instructor by email at least 48 hours before the scheduled debate, so that the opposing team can prepare rebuttals.
Cross-examination. Each team has the opportunity to directly question the opposing team about the constructive argument. Cross-examination is timed and is intended to expose weaknesses in the opposing argument, not to score rhetorical points.
Rebuttal. Each team responds to the opposing constructive argument and to the cross-examination, providing additional evidence and reasoning in support of its position.
Concluding statements. Each team summarizes its position and identifies the central reasons the audience should find for it.
Audience question and answer. Students from the rest of the class question both teams in the time remaining. The questions are unscripted; the answers are extemporaneous.
Evaluation. Three non-presenting students, designated by the instructor before the debate, evaluate the performance of each team using a rubric distributed in advance.

The format is rigorous but the goal is not adversarial. The point of the structure is to force each team to articulate the strongest version of its assigned position, and to engage seriously with the strongest version of the opposing position. Students who emerge from a well-run debate having argued for a position they did not initially hold are, on this account, the students who have learned the most.

title: 14  Synthesis: The Capstone Debates
text:
14.5 Evaluation rubric
Each debate is evaluated by three non-presenting students using the following rubric. The total score for each team is the sum of the component scores; the team with the higher total wins the debate.








Component
Maximum Score
What is being assessed




Strength of constructive argument
25
Quality of the framework, evidence, and reasoning offered in support of the assigned position


Quality of cross-examination
15
The team’s ability to identify and probe weaknesses in the opposing argument


Quality of rebuttal
25
The team’s response to the opposing argument and to the cross-examination


Engagement with the framework
25
The team’s use of the analytical tools developed in the Companion, with appropriate attention to evidence and counterargument


Audience Q&A
10
The team’s ability to engage thoughtfully with audience questions


Total
100




The “engagement with the framework” component is the largest single piece of the rubric. It rewards teams that ground their arguments in the analytical content of the course rather than in extemporaneous opinion or general rhetorical skill. A team whose constructive argument cites specific chapters of the Companion, engages with specific empirical findings from the assigned readings, and applies specific analytical tools — the relevance-faithfulness trade-off, the multitasking framework, the political-economy analysis of standard-setting — will outscore a team whose argument is rhetorically polished but framework-sparse.

title: 14  Synthesis: The Capstone Debates
text:
14.6 Key takeaways






Key takeaways




Argue both sides, then take a position — in that order. The discipline of the chapter is to construct the strongest case for the position one does not initially hold, then to evaluate the two cases side by side. Skipping the first step is the standard route to overconfident conclusions.
Use the framework, not extemporaneous opinion. Each debate is anchored in the same vocabulary the Companion has developed: adverse selection, signaling, moral hazard, agency cost, decentralization, market efficiency, and the verifiability-relevance trade-off. Arguments that do not draw on the framework should be treated with skepticism, including one’s own.
Most contested questions are contested because the evidence is incomplete. The empirical literature on the four prompts is informative but not decisive; framework reasoning fills part of the gap, but only part. Calibrated uncertainty is the appropriate posture, not strong conviction in either direction.
The conceptual distinctions matter institutionally. Whether a problem is adverse selection or moral hazard determines whether the appropriate response is disclosure or contracting; whether a measurement issue is one of relevance or verifiability determines whether the appropriate response is fair value or historical cost. The same framework that classifies the problem also constrains the response.
Standards are compromises, not solutions. Chapter 5’s central message is reinforced here: the rules accounting operates under are the compromises a well-meaning standard-setter could reach under political, verifiability, and cost-benefit constraints. They are revisable, and the revision process is itself part of the framework.
The framework outlasts the course. Agency theory in corporate finance, mechanism design in microeconomic theory, contract theory in organizational economics, and the institutional analysis of regulation in public finance all use versions of the same tools. The course ends; the tools do not.

title: 14  Synthesis: The Capstone Debates
text:
14.7 A course retrospective
Beyond the debates themselves, the final session of Week 15 includes a course retrospective in which the instructor and students collectively reflect on what the semester has produced. The retrospective is intended to reinforce the central organizing insight of the course: that financial and managerial accounting are unified by the information-economics framework rather than divided by the financial/managerial distinction.
The retrospective is not a review session. It is the occasion at which students articulate, in their own words, the central insights they take from the semester. Students are asked to come prepared with a written one-page reflection addressing the following prompts.

What is the single insight from the semester that you expect to remember five years from now? Why?
Which of the topics covered in the semester do you wish had been covered in greater depth? Why?
Which of the topics covered in the semester do you wish had been covered in less depth? Why?
If you were teaching ACCTG 211H next year, what one substantive change would you make to the course?

The reflections are graded as part of participation. They are also used to inform the design of the course in subsequent years; many of the changes between the 2025, 2026, and 2027 versions of the course originated in suggestions students made in their retrospective reflections.

title: 14  Synthesis: The Capstone Debates
section: 14.8 What comes next, and what remains unsettled
text:
14.8 What comes next, and what remains unsettled
The semester ends here, but the framework does not. Students who continue in accounting (ACCTG 311, ACCTG 401, the upper-level electives) will encounter the institutional details of GAAP at much greater depth. Students who continue in finance, economics, or related fields will encounter many of the analytical tools developed in this course in different applications: agency theory in corporate finance, mechanism design in microeconomic theory, contract theory in organizational economics. Students who continue in industry will encounter the institutional infrastructure of accounting in the role of preparer, auditor, regulator, investor, or analyst — and the framework of this course is, in important respects, the same framework practitioners in those roles use to think about contested questions in their daily work.
A great deal remains unsettled. Whether the impairment-only model for goodwill is preferable to annual remeasurement, whether mandatory climate disclosure will improve capital allocation, whether the tax treatment of stock-based compensation distorts performance measurement on net, whether ESG reporting is best understood as a response to adverse selection or to moral hazard — none of these questions has a confident answer in the contemporary literature, and the Companion has tried not to invent one. The honest posture, after fourteen chapters of framework-building, is to say what the framework rules out, what it leaves open, and where the next round of evidence is most likely to move the conversation.
For students who want to go deeper, the academic literature in financial and managerial accounting is large, accessible, and continually evolving. The Sloan accrual anomaly (Sloan 1996), the Burgstahler-Dichev discontinuity (Burgstahler and Dichev 1997), the Holthausen-Watts and Barth-Beaver-Landsman exchange on value relevance (Holthausen and Watts 2001; Barth, Beaver, and Landsman 2001), the Holmstrom-Milgrom multitasking framework (Holmstrom and Milgrom 1991), and the Antle-Eppen capital-budgeting analysis (Antle and Eppen 1985) are all alive in the contemporary literature and will continue to develop. The Companion’s reading lists at the end of each chapter are designed as entry points; the bibliography contains the foundational papers that have shaped the field.






A final word



The honors seminar is, almost by definition, an opportunity to engage with material at a depth and rigor that introductory courses cannot afford. ACCTG 211H is intended to be that opportunity for accounting. Students who have read this Companion carefully, completed the empirical labs, prepared seriously for the debates, and engaged with the academic literature have done something more than introductory accounting: they have done their first piece of analytical accounting work, in the broad sense of that phrase. The same tools used here are the tools the academic literature uses, the tools serious investors and analysts use, the tools regulators and standard-setters use. Use them well, and use them with the calibrated uncertainty the material deserves.

title: Appendix B — A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart
text:
B.1 Why Python in Colab
The labs are designed to run in Google Colab, Google’s hosted Jupyter notebook environment. Colab is free, requires nothing more than a Penn State Google account, and runs in your browser — no local install required. It has the standard Python data-science stack (pandas, numpy, matplotlib, scipy, statsmodels) pre-installed, and the Wharton Research Data Services (WRDS) team maintains an official Python package, wrds, that connects directly to the WRDS PostgreSQL backend with a single line of code.
crumbs: B  A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart

title: Appendix B — A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart
section: B.2 Setting up your WRDS account
text:
B.2 Setting up your WRDS account
Smeal provides institutional access to WRDS. To register your account:

Go to https://wrds-www.wharton.upenn.edu.
Click “Register for an Account.”
Enter your psu.edu email address and create a password.
Wait for account approval (usually within one business day).

Once your account is active, you are ready to connect from Colab.
crumbs: B  A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart

title: Appendix B — A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart
text:
B.3 Connecting from Colab
A typical lab session opens with three cells:
# Cell 1: install the WRDS package
!pip install wrds --quiet

# Cell 2: import everything you need
import wrds
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

# Cell 3: connect (you will be prompted for your WRDS username and password)
db = wrds.Connection()
The wrds.Connection() call prompts for your WRDS credentials the first time it runs in a session. After that, the connection persists for the rest of the notebook. (In a long-running notebook, the connection can occasionally drop; if a query fails with a connection-related error, simply re-run the connect cell.)
crumbs: B  A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart

title: Appendix B — A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart
text:
B.4 Querying WRDS
The simplest way to pull data from WRDS is db.raw_sql(...), which executes an arbitrary SQL query against the WRDS PostgreSQL database and returns a pandas DataFrame. For example:
df = db.raw_sql("""
    SELECT gvkey, datadate, fyear, at, ib
    FROM comp.funda
    WHERE indfmt = 'INDL'
      AND datafmt = 'STD'
      AND fyear BETWEEN 2018 AND 2024
""", date_cols=['datadate'])
The date_cols argument tells pandas to parse the named columns as dates rather than strings.
crumbs: B  A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart

title: Appendix B — A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart
section: B.5 The five WRDS tables you will use most
text:
B.5 The five WRDS tables you will use most

crsp.dsf — daily stock file (returns, prices, shares outstanding)
crsp.msf — monthly stock file
crsp.dsi — daily stock index file (the value-weighted and equal-weighted CRSP market returns; also serves as the trading-day calendar)
comp.funda — Compustat North America annual fundamentals
comp.fundq — Compustat North America quarterly fundamentals

For linking CRSP and Compustat, you will additionally need:

crsp.ccmxpf_lnkhist — the CRSP/Compustat Merged linking table

Each is documented in detail at the WRDS site under “Data > [vendor] > [dataset] > Manual.”
crumbs: B  A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart

title: Appendix B — A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart
text:
B.6 Common pitfalls

Linking CRSP to Compustat. Use crsp.ccmxpf_lnkhist (the CRSP/Compustat Merged linking table). Filter on linktype IN ('LU','LC') and linkprim IN ('P','C').
Adjusting for splits. CRSP daily returns are already split-adjusted; CRSP daily prices are not.
Trading days vs. calendar days. Use crsp.dsi as your trading-day calendar; do not infer trading days from crsp.dsf alone.
Earnings announcement dates. Compustat’s rdq (on comp.fundq) is the date the firm announced; it is not always the date the market reacted to the announcement, especially for after-hours announcements.
Pickling intermediate DataFrames. Free Colab sessions disconnect after about 90 minutes of inactivity, and the WRDS pulls in the larger labs take several minutes each. Mount your Google Drive (drive.mount('/content/drive')) and save intermediate DataFrames to a folder there using df.to_pickle(...). You can then reload them in subsequent sessions without re-running the queries.
pip install is per-session. Colab does not persist installed packages across sessions. The !pip install wrds --quiet cell must run every time you reopen the notebook.
crumbs: B  A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart

title: Appendix B — A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart
text:
B.7 A minimal pandas reference
The labs use only a small subset of pandas. The operations you will need most often are:







Operation
Code




Inspect first rows
df.head()


Inspect shape
df.shape


Filter rows
df.loc[df['col'] > 0]


Sort
df.sort_values(['gvkey','fyear'])


Lag a column within group
df.groupby('gvkey')['at'].shift(1)


Merge two DataFrames
df1.merge(df2, on=['gvkey','datadate'], how='inner')


Drop duplicates
df.drop_duplicates(subset=['gvkey','datadate'])


Compute a within-group statistic
df.groupby('fyear')['news'].transform('median')


Cross-tab of counts
df['col'].value_counts()


Drop missing values
df.dropna(subset=['col'])



If you have not used pandas before, work through the pandas Getting Started tutorials before Lab 1. One to two hours is enough.
crumbs: B  A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart

title: Appendix B — A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart
text:
B.8 Plotting
The labs use matplotlib for all plots. The basic pattern:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.plot(x, y, lw=2, label='Series A')
ax.set_xlabel('...')
ax.set_ylabel('...')
ax.legend()
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig('myplot.pdf')
plt.show()
For more elegant defaults you may use seaborn (import seaborn as sns; sns.set_theme()), but it is not required.
crumbs: B  A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart

title: Appendix B — A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart
text:
B.9 When you get stuck

Check the WRDS support documentation: https://wrds-www.wharton.upenn.edu/pages/support/
Check the WRDS Python package documentation: https://github.com/wharton/wrds
Use the in-class WRDS lab session to ask the TA.
GenAI tools are permitted on labs (with disclosure) and can be useful for pandas syntax and SQL questions.
crumbs: B  A2. Python + WRDS in Google Colab Quickstart

C.1 The five account types
The mnemonic is DEAL: Debits increase Expenses and Assets; Liabilities, equity, and revenue (the rest) increase with credits.

crumbs: C  A3. Debits and Credits Cheat Sheet

title: Appendix C — A3. Debits and Credits Cheat Sheet
section: C.2 The accounting equation behind it
text:
C.2 The accounting equation behind it
Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Revenue and expenses are sub-accounts of equity (specifically, retained earnings). When revenue increases (credit) or expense increases (debit), retained earnings — and thus equity — moves accordingly.
crumbs: C  A3. Debits and Credits Cheat Sheet

title: Appendix C — A3. Debits and Credits Cheat Sheet
section: C.3 Why we use it at all
text:
C.3 Why we use it at all
The double-entry system is a verification device. Every transaction has equal debits and credits; if the books do not balance, something is wrong. This is itself an information-economics solution: a recordkeeping system designed to make tampering and arithmetic error visible. (See Chapter 1 for the broader context.)
crumbs: C  A3. Debits and Credits Cheat Sheet
